name: George T. Stagg
description: George T. Stagg is the uncut, unfiltered heavyweight of the annual Buffalo Trace Antique Collection: a roughly fifteen-year-old bourbon bottled straight from the barrel, with proof that varies by year — typically in the 130s, and famously above 140 in the "Hazmat" releases. Beneath the heat sits one of bourbon's densest flavor profiles: blackstrap molasses, dark chocolate, espresso, cherry preserves, tobacco, and old oak, delivered with a viscous body and a finish measured in minutes. Released each fall in small quantities, it is among the most sought-after American whiskeys; lottery systems and four-figure secondary prices are the norm. Critics have repeatedly ranked it among the world's finest bourbons, and for many drinkers it defines the barrel-proof category. The stated ABV here reflects a typical release; each year differs.
history: The whiskey is named for George T. Stagg, the nineteenth-century whiskey magnate who bought E.H. Taylor's O.F.C. Distillery in 1878; the Frankfort plant itself carried the George T. Stagg name from 1904 until Sazerac renamed it Buffalo Trace in 1999. Stagg joined the Buffalo Trace Antique Collection in 2002, two years after the collection began, and quickly became its flagship. Buffalo Trace skipped the 2021 release entirely after judging that year's batch not up to standard. A younger sibling, Stagg Jr. (now simply "Stagg"), launched in 2013.
production: Distilled from Buffalo Trace's undisclosed low-rye Mash Bill #1 and left in new charred oak for roughly fifteen years, losing well over half the barrel's contents to evaporation. The surviving whiskey is batched and bottled uncut and unfiltered at natural barrel proof, so both proof and yield vary with each year's warehouse conditions.
barrel: New charred American white oak, ~15 years in Buffalo Trace warehouses; heavy evaporative losses
coloring: No added coloring (prohibited for straight bourbon)

tastingNotes.nose: Molasses, dark cherry, espresso, charred oak, leather and pipe tobacco
tastingNotes.palate: Immense — dark chocolate, burnt caramel, cherry preserves, clove, rye spice and oak tannin
tastingNotes.finish: Extremely long and warming; coffee, leather and dark sweetness persist for minutes

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature (18–22 °C) with water on hand; a few drops unlock the nose
facts: Releases above 140 proof are nicknamed "Hazmat" because US airlines prohibit carrying them aboard.
No George T. Stagg was released in 2021; Buffalo Trace held the batch back on quality grounds.
Around 60 percent of the whiskey is typically lost to evaporation over its fifteen years.

timeline.event: George T. Stagg joins the Buffalo Trace Antique Collection
Stagg Jr. launched as a year-round younger sibling
Annual release skipped after the batch failed internal quality review

name: Pappy Van Winkle's Family Reserve 23 Year
description: Pappy Van Winkle's Family Reserve 23 Year is the most extreme expression of the Van Winkle wheated bourbon line and arguably the most famous cult whiskey in America. Twenty-three years in new charred oak takes a soft wheated spirit deep into dark territory: polished antique oak, leather, fig, dark caramel, and bittersweet chocolate, at 95.6 proof. Only a few thousand bottles are released each fall through lotteries and allocation lists; the suggested price of about three hundred dollars bears no relation to reality, with secondary-market prices commonly exceeding three thousand. Critics debate whether any bourbon can carry that much oak, but its scarcity, family story, and role in igniting the modern bourbon boom have made "Pappy 23" shorthand for unobtainable whiskey.
history: Julian "Pappy" Van Winkle, Sr. began as a whiskey salesman for W.L. Weller & Sons in 1893 and ran the Stitzel-Weller Distillery from its 1935 opening until his death in 1965. His grandson Julian III revived the family name as a bottler of aged wheated stock, launching the Family Reserve 20 Year in 1994 and the 23 Year in the late 1990s from Stitzel-Weller-era barrels. Since a 2002 partnership, the Van Winkle whiskeys have been distilled and bottled at Buffalo Trace to the family's wheated recipe.
production: Made from an undisclosed wheated mash bill — corn, wheat, and malted barley — matched to the old Stitzel-Weller recipe and distilled at Buffalo Trace since 2002. The whiskey rests twenty-three years in new charred American oak, with the great majority of each barrel lost to evaporation, then is batched and bottled at 95.6 proof each autumn.
barrel: New charred American white oak, 23 years; only a fraction of each barrel survives
coloring: No added coloring (prohibited for straight bourbon)

tastingNotes.nose: Antique oak, leather-bound books, fig, dark caramel, vanilla pod and pipe tobacco
tastingNotes.palate: Dense dark caramel, bittersweet chocolate, dried fruit, wheat softness beneath heavy oak tannin
tastingNotes.finish: Very long, drying, oak and leather with a sweet molasses echo

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature (18–22 °C); no ice, minimal water
facts: Secondary prices above $3,000 make it one of the most counterfeited American whiskeys; empty bottles sell online.
In 2013 over 200 bottles of Pappy were stolen from Buffalo Trace in the "Pappygate" heist.
Early releases drew on stock from the closed Stitzel-Weller Distillery; modern releases are Buffalo Trace distillate.

timeline.event: Pappy Van Winkle's Family Reserve line debuts with the 20 Year
23 Year Family Reserve first bottled from aged Stitzel-Weller stock
Van Winkle production moves to Buffalo Trace Distillery
"Pappygate" theft of over 200 bottles makes national news

name: Maker's Mark
description: Maker's Mark is the best-known wheated bourbon in the world and one of the founding bottles of premium American whiskey. Conceived by Bill Samuels, Sr. to be a bourbon that "wouldn't blister your tongue," it replaces rye with soft red winter wheat, yielding a round, sweet profile of caramel, honey, baked bread, and vanilla at 90 proof. Every bottle is still hand-dipped in the signature red wax, an idea from Margie Samuels, who also devised the name, the label, and the pewter-inspired typography. Spelled "whisky" in the Scottish manner of the Samuels family, it is matured to taste rather than to a fixed age — roughly six years — and rotated by hand through the warehouses. It remains a benchmark introduction to bourbon and a fixture of back bars everywhere.
history: Sixth-generation distiller Bill Samuels, Sr. sold his family's old whiskey business, bought the historic Burks' Distillery near Loretto in 1953, and famously burned the family recipe to start over — settling the new wheated mash bill, by legend, after baking bread with candidate grain recipes. The first bottles reached shelves in 1958. The distillery was named a National Historic Landmark in 1980, the first American distillery so designated while operating. After passing through Hiram Walker and Allied Domecq, the brand joined Beam and is now part of Suntory Global Spirits.
production: Made at Star Hill Farm in Loretto from a disclosed mash bill of 70 % corn, 16 % soft red winter wheat, and 14 % malted barley, fermented with a house yeast dating to the founding and limestone-filtered lake water. The whisky ages about six years in new charred oak, with barrels hand-rotated between warehouse floors to even out maturation, then is bottled at 90 proof after each batch is approved by a tasting panel.
barrel: New charred American white oak; barrels hand-rotated through the warehouses, ~6 years
coloring: No added coloring (prohibited for straight bourbon)

tastingNotes.nose: Caramel, vanilla, fresh-baked wheat bread, orchard fruit
tastingNotes.palate: Soft and rounded — honey, butterscotch, light oak, a hint of baking spice
tastingNotes.finish: Clean and medium-length, sweet vanilla with gentle warmth

servingTemp: Neat, with a cube, or at room temperature (18–22 °C); very cocktail-friendly
facts: Margie Samuels created the name, the label, and the hand-dipped red wax seal; she was inducted into the Bourbon Hall of Fame in 2014.
It is labeled "whisky" without an e, a nod to the Samuels family's Scottish ancestry.
In 2013 the company briefly cut the proof from 90 to 84 to stretch supply, then reversed course within days after public outcry.

timeline.event: Bill Samuels, Sr. buys Burks' Distillery in Loretto
First bottles of Maker's Mark go on sale
Distillery named a National Historic Landmark
Proof reduction to 84 announced and reversed within a week

name: Four Roses Single Barrel
description: Four Roses Single Barrel showcases one specific recipe from the distillery's famous ten-recipe system: OBSV, the high-rye mash bill fermented with the "delicate fruit" yeast strain. Bottled barrel by barrel at 100 proof, typically after seven to nine years, it is among the most fruit-forward and floral of major Kentucky bourbons — ripe cherry, apricot, and rose-like top notes over caramel, cocoa, and assertive rye spice. Unlike most premium single barrels it remains genuinely findable at retail near its suggested price, which has made it a critical favorite and a common answer to "best readily available bourbon." Private barrel programs offer the other nine recipes at barrel strength, but the standard OBSV release is the brand's precision instrument.
history: The Four Roses trademark dates to 1888 under Paul Jones, Jr., and its Spanish Mission-style Lawrenceburg distillery was built in 1910. Under Seagram from 1943, the straight bourbon was pulled from the American market in the late 1950s in favor of a cheap blended whiskey, while the real thing thrived in Europe and Japan. Japanese brewer Kirin bought the brand in 2002, returned straight bourbon to the US, and launched this Single Barrel in 2004 as the top of the standard range.
production: Four Roses uses two mash bills and five proprietary yeast strains to create ten distinct bourbon recipes. Single Barrel is always OBSV: the high-rye "B" mash bill of 60 % corn, 35 % rye, 5 % malted barley, fermented with the fruity "V" yeast. It ages in single-story rack warehouses in Cox's Creek that avoid the temperature extremes of tall rickhouses, and each barrel is bottled individually at 100 proof, non-chill filtered.
barrel: New charred American white oak, aged in single-story rack warehouses at Cox's Creek
coloring: No added coloring (prohibited for straight bourbon)

tastingNotes.nose: Ripe cherry, apricot, rose petal, caramel and rye spice
tastingNotes.palate: Plum and berry fruit, cocoa, toffee, cinnamon and robust pepper
tastingNotes.finish: Long and spicy-sweet, fruit fading into toasted oak

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature (18–22 °C); excellent with a single cube
facts: The four-letter recipe code OBSV encodes the distillery (O), high-rye mash bill (B), straight whiskey (S), and yeast strain (V).
Four Roses is the only major Kentucky distillery using five distinct proprietary yeast strains in regular production.
The 1910 Lawrenceburg distillery is built in a Spanish Mission style unique in Kentucky and is on the National Register of Historic Places.

timeline.event: Paul Jones, Jr. trademarks the Four Roses name
Kirin Brewery acquires Four Roses and restores straight bourbon to the US
Four Roses Single Barrel (OBSV) launched in the US market

name: Knob Creek 9 Year Small Batch
description: Knob Creek 9 Year is the flagship of the Small Batch Bourbon Collection that Booker Noe created at Jim Beam in 1992, a lineup that helped launch the premium bourbon era. Aged a full nine years and bottled at 100 proof in a squat, flask-inspired square bottle with a paper-band label meant to evoke pre-Prohibition packaging, it is a deliberately big bourbon: heavy toasted oak, peanut brittle, brown sugar, and char, with the robust body Beam's deep-char barrels deliver. The age statement was dropped in 2016 when stocks ran short and restored in 2020 — a rare reversal in the industry. Consistently priced under forty dollars, it is a standing answer for high-proof, age-stated bourbon that is actually on the shelf.
history: Booker Noe, Jim Beam's sixth-generation master distiller, created Knob Creek in 1992 as part of the Small Batch Bourbon Collection alongside Booker's, Baker's, and Basil Hayden's, betting that drinkers would pay up for older, stronger bourbon during whiskey's leanest decade. The name refers to Knob Creek in Kentucky, the site of Abraham Lincoln's boyhood farm. The line later added a single barrel, a rye, and 12- and 15-year extensions, and the 9-year age statement returned in 2020 after a four-year hiatus.
production: Distilled at the Jim Beam plants in Clermont and Boston, Kentucky, from Beam's standard bourbon mash bill (not officially published; widely reported as roughly 77 % corn, 13 % rye, 10 % malted barley) with the house jug yeast. Aged nine years in new oak given Beam's deepest char, then batched in relatively small runs and bottled at 100 proof.
barrel: New American oak with a deep level-4 char, aged 9 years in Beam rickhouses
coloring: No added coloring (prohibited for straight bourbon)

tastingNotes.nose: Toasted oak, peanut, brown sugar, vanilla and char
tastingNotes.palate: Full-bodied — maple, dark caramel, nutmeg, roasted nuts and firm oak
tastingNotes.finish: Long, warm and toasty, with char and sweet oak lingering

servingTemp: Neat or over a large cube; room temperature (18–22 °C)
facts: Named for the creek beside Abraham Lincoln's boyhood home; Lincoln's father reportedly worked at a distillery there.
One of the four original Small Batch Bourbon Collection brands Booker Noe launched in 1992.
Lost its 9-year age statement in 2016 amid shortages and regained it in 2020.

timeline.event: Knob Creek launched in the Small Batch Bourbon Collection
9-year age statement removed during stock shortage
9-year age statement restored

name: Woodford Reserve Distiller's Select
description: Woodford Reserve Distiller's Select is Brown-Forman's super-premium bourbon, produced at the historic stone distillery on Glenn's Creek where James Crow systematized sour-mash whiskey making in the 1830s. It is unusual among Kentucky bourbons for blending spirit from copper pot stills — triple-distilled on site — with column-still whiskey from Brown-Forman's Shively plant, then bottling at a precise 90.4 proof. The house style leans balanced and polished: dried fruit, toasted oak, cocoa, and vanilla with an 18 %-rye spice backbone, marketed around more than two hundred identified flavor notes. The flattened flask-shaped bottle is a bar-shelf staple, and since 1999 Woodford has been the presenting sponsor and official bourbon of the Kentucky Derby, cementing its association with Kentucky tradition.
history: Distilling on the Glenn's Creek site near Versailles dates to Elijah Pepper around 1812; his son Oscar built the stone distillery in 1838, where hired distiller James Crow refined the sour-mash method that became industry standard. Later known as Labrot & Graham, the plant was bought, sold, and shuttered by Brown-Forman before the company repurchased and restored it in 1994, launching Woodford Reserve in 1996. The distillery is a National Historic Landmark, and Distiller's Select remains the brand's flagship.
production: Made from a disclosed mash bill of 72 % corn, 18 % rye, 10 % malted barley. Woodford triple-distills in three Scottish-built copper pot stills, and the final bourbon marries that pot-still spirit with column-distilled whiskey of the same mash bill from Brown-Forman's Louisville distillery. Maturation runs roughly six to seven years in new charred oak, partly in heat-cycled stone warehouses, before bottling at 90.4 proof.
barrel: New charred American white oak; some maturation in heat-cycled 19th-century stone warehouses
coloring: No added coloring (prohibited for straight bourbon)

tastingNotes.nose: Dried apricot, toasted oak, vanilla, cocoa and mint
tastingNotes.palate: Silky — caramel, dark fruit, hazelnut, cinnamon and rye spice
tastingNotes.finish: Medium-long, creamy vanilla drying into spiced oak

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature (18–22 °C), or in a mint julep with crushed ice
facts: James Crow's work at this site in the 1830s–40s standardized the sour-mash process used across bourbon today.
One of the few bourbons combining copper pot-still and column-still distillates in one bottle.
The official bourbon of the Kentucky Derby, with a commemorative Derby bottle released annually.

timeline.event: Elijah Pepper begins distilling on Glenn's Creek
Brown-Forman repurchases and restores the Labrot & Graham distillery
Woodford Reserve Distiller's Select launched
Becomes official bourbon of the Kentucky Derby

name: Wild Turkey 101
description: Wild Turkey 101 is the signature bourbon of the Russell family and one of American whiskey's great constants: a high-proof, high-flavor pour whose recipe and character have changed remarkably little since the mid-twentieth century. Barreled at a low entry proof so less water is added at bottling, it concentrates flavor — toffee, vanilla, orange peel, and the brand's hallmark black-pepper rye kick — while staying under thirty dollars. A blend of roughly six-to-eight-year-old barrels bottled at 101 proof, it earns near-universal respect from bartenders and collectors alike as one of the best values in whiskey. Master distiller Jimmy Russell, who joined the distillery in 1954 and is the longest-tenured active master distiller in the world, has guarded its old-school profile for decades.
history: The Ripy brothers built the distillery above the Kentucky River at Lawrenceburg in 1869. The Wild Turkey name arrived in 1940, when Austin Nichols executive Thomas McCarthy brought warehouse samples on an annual wild turkey hunt and friends asked for "that wild turkey whiskey"; the 101-proof bourbon was formally launched in 1942. Jimmy Russell started in 1954 and was joined by son Eddie in 1981. Pernod Ricard owned the brand from 1980 until Campari Group acquired it in 2009 and built a new distillery on the site in 2011.
production: Distilled in Lawrenceburg from a mash bill the Russells state as 75 % corn, 13 % rye, 12 % malted barley. Wild Turkey famously barrels at a low entry proof (raised modestly over the years to around 115) into heavily charred number-4 "alligator char" oak, so the whiskey needs minimal dilution to reach 101 proof. The batch mingles barrels roughly six to eight years old from tall open-rick warehouses.
barrel: New American oak, deep #4 alligator char; low barrel-entry proof preserves flavor
coloring: No added coloring (prohibited for straight bourbon)

tastingNotes.nose: Toffee, vanilla, orange peel, charred oak and rye grain
tastingNotes.palate: Bold caramel and honey, cinnamon, black pepper, toasted oak
tastingNotes.finish: Long and spicy, char and brown sugar trailing off slowly

servingTemp: Neat, on the rocks, or in stirred cocktails; room temperature (18–22 °C)
facts: Jimmy Russell, master distiller since the 1960s, has worked at the distillery continuously since 1954.
The name comes from a 1940 wild turkey hunt attended by an Austin Nichols executive carrying warehouse samples.
Wild Turkey's low barrel-entry proof means less water at bottling — a key to its concentrated flavor.

timeline.event: Ripy brothers establish the Lawrenceburg distillery
Wild Turkey 101 launched by Austin Nichols
Campari Group acquires Wild Turkey
New distillery opens on Wild Turkey Hill

name: Sazerac Rye
description: Sazerac Rye — affectionately "Baby Saz" to distinguish it from its 18-year Antique Collection sibling — is Buffalo Trace's standard-bearer for the New Orleans rye tradition, named for the Sazerac Coffee House and the cocktail invented there. Bottled at 90 proof around six years old, it is a gentle, Kentucky-style take on rye: candied anise, clove, and mint over a surprisingly sweet caramel-vanilla base, without the aggressive grain bite of higher-rye recipes. That balance makes it the house pour for a proper Sazerac cocktail and an easy crossover for bourbon drinkers. Demand often thins shelves and lifts street prices somewhat above retail, though it remains far easier to find than Buffalo Trace's allocated bourbons.
history: The Sazerac name traces to the Sazerac Coffee House, the mid-1800s New Orleans bar associated with the Sazerac cocktail, and to the Sazerac Company that grew out of it — today Buffalo Trace's parent. Rye was the cocktail's base by the late nineteenth century after phylloxera devastated cognac supplies. Sazerac 18 Year joined the Antique Collection in 2000, and this standard six-year expression followed in the mid-2000s to give bartenders an accessible rye bearing the cocktail's name.
production: Distilled at Buffalo Trace in Frankfort from an undisclosed rye mash bill, generally understood to be close to the legal minimum of 51 % rye, giving it a rounder, sweeter character than Northern-style ryes. Aged approximately six years in new charred oak, batched, and bottled at 90 proof.
barrel: New charred American white oak, ~6 years at Buffalo Trace
coloring: No added coloring (prohibited for straight rye)

tastingNotes.nose: Anise, clove, candied spice, vanilla and faint citrus
tastingNotes.palate: Caramel sweetness first, then white pepper, mint and licorice
tastingNotes.finish: Medium, softly spiced, with vanilla and dried fruit

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature (18–22 °C); classically in a chilled Sazerac
facts: Named for the New Orleans coffee house where the Sazerac cocktail was born.
Nicknamed "Baby Saz" to distinguish it from the 18-year version in the Buffalo Trace Antique Collection.
Rye replaced cognac in the Sazerac cocktail after Europe's 19th-century phylloxera blight.

timeline.event: Sazerac Rye 18 Year debuts in the Buffalo Trace Antique Collection
Standard 6-year Sazerac Rye introduced

name: Rittenhouse Rye Bottled-in-Bond
description: Rittenhouse Rye Bottled-in-Bond is the modern bartender's default rye: 100 proof, robustly spiced, resistant to dilution, and priced under thirty dollars. Made by Heaven Hill in the "Pennsylvania style" that the brand's Philadelphia origins invoke — though distilled in Kentucky from a barely-legal rye recipe with substantial corn — it splits the difference between bourbon sweetness and rye bite: cocoa, caramel, and citrus wrapped in cinnamon and black pepper. Its bottled-in-bond status guarantees one distillery, one season, four years minimum in bond, and exactly 100 proof. When the craft cocktail revival of the 2000s needed an affordable, assertive rye for Manhattans and Sazeracs, Rittenhouse became the house answer, and it has anchored back bars ever since.
history: Rittenhouse was introduced after Repeal by Continental Distilling of Philadelphia, named for the city's Rittenhouse Square, and was one of the last standard-bearers of Pennsylvania rye as the style collapsed mid-century. Heaven Hill acquired the brand and kept it alive through rye's leanest decades, when only a handful of American ryes survived. Production moved to the Bernheim facility in Louisville after the 1996 fire destroyed Heaven Hill's Bardstown stillhouse. The cocktail renaissance rediscovered it in the 2000s, making it a staple once more.
production: Distilled at Heaven Hill's Bernheim distillery in Louisville from an undisclosed low-rye mash bill reported at the legal minimum of 51 % rye with corn and malted barley, which explains its bourbon-adjacent sweetness. Aged at least four years in bonded warehouses per the Bottled-in-Bond Act and bottled at exactly 100 proof.
barrel: New charred American white oak, minimum 4 years in federally bonded warehouses
coloring: No added coloring (prohibited for straight rye)

tastingNotes.nose: Cocoa, dried citrus peel, cinnamon, toasted grain
tastingNotes.palate: Caramel and chocolate, then surging rye spice, clove and black pepper
tastingNotes.finish: Long for the price — peppery, drying, with dark chocolate echoes

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature (18–22 °C); built for stirred cocktails
facts: Named for Rittenhouse Square in Philadelphia, a nod to the vanished Pennsylvania rye tradition.
One of only a handful of American rye whiskeys that remained in continuous production through the late 20th century.
Its 100-proof bottled-in-bond strength is prized by bartenders because it holds up to stirring and dilution.

timeline.event: Bottled-in-Bond Act creates the standard Rittenhouse is made to
Heaven Hill's Bardstown distillery fire; rye production later moves to Bernheim in Louisville

name: WhistlePig 10 Year
description: WhistlePig 10 Year put super-premium rye on the map. Launched in 2010 from a Vermont dairy farm, it is a 100 % rye whiskey distilled in Canada — originally at Alberta Distillers — then aged, finished, and bottled by WhistlePig at 100 proof. The all-rye grain bill delivers the style at full volume: mint, dill, and baking spice over butterscotch, orchard fruit, and oak, with a long peppery finish. The late Dave Pickerell, former Maker's Mark master distiller, selected and shaped the original stocks, openly embracing sourcing at a time when it was taboo; the transparency debate followed the brand for years even as critics ranked the whiskey among the best ryes available. It remains the flagship while the farm's own estate-grown distillate matures.
history: Raj Peter Bhakta bought a 500-acre former dairy farm in Shoreham, Vermont in 2007 and recruited Dave Pickerell, who located aged stocks of 100 % rye whiskey in Canada suited to American tastes. The first WhistlePig 10 Year bottles appeared in 2010 to immediate acclaim. The farm planted its own rye, restored an 1870s barn into a distillery licensed in 2015, and expanded the range with 12-, 15-, and 18-year expressions and the estate-driven Farmstock line. Pickerell died in 2018; blending is now led by Meghan Ireland.
production: Distilled from a 100 % rye mash — no corn or barley — originally at Alberta Distillers in Calgary, whose all-rye distillate is prized for purity of spice and fruit. The whiskey ages at least ten years, with maturation completed in new American oak at the Vermont farm before proofing to 100 and bottling. Later batches increasingly incorporate WhistlePig's own distilling and finishing work.
barrel: Canadian-aged rye finished in new charred American oak at the Shoreham farm
coloring: No added coloring

tastingNotes.nose: Mint, dill, allspice, caramel, green apple and oak
tastingNotes.palate: Butterscotch and orange giving way to cinnamon, clove and rye grass
tastingNotes.finish: Long, warm and peppery with drying oak and lingering mint

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature (18–22 °C); a splash of water tames the spice
facts: The whiskey is 100 % rye, distilled in Canada and matured/finished at a Vermont farm — a sourcing story the brand eventually made part of its identity.
Dave Pickerell, who created WhistlePig's blending program, spent 14 years as Maker's Mark's master distiller.
The farm grows its own rye and runs a distillery in a restored 1870s barn for its Farmstock line.

timeline.event: Raj Bhakta purchases the Shoreham, Vermont farm
WhistlePig 10 Year launched from Canadian-sourced rye
On-farm distillery licensed in the restored barn
Master distiller Dave Pickerell dies; blending team continues his program

name: Jack Daniel's Old No. 7
description: Jack Daniel's Old No. 7 is the best-selling American whiskey on earth, moving well over ten million cases a year from a hollow in dry Moore County, Tennessee. What separates it from bourbon is the Lincoln County Process: before barreling, the new spirit drips through roughly ten feet of sugar-maple charcoal, a days-long mellowing step that strips harshness and adds the whiskey's signature sooty-sweet smoothness. The profile is approachable by design — banana, caramel, toasted oak, and light char at 80 proof — served worldwide with cola or on ice. The black label and square bottle are among the most recognized packages in spirits, and the meaning of "Old No. 7" remains a genuinely unsolved piece of brand folklore.
history: Jasper Newton "Jack" Daniel learned distilling as a boy from Reverend Dan Call and, crucially, from Nearest Green, an enslaved (later free) distiller whom Daniel eventually made his first head distiller — a role Brown-Forman formally recognized in 2016. The distillery claims an 1866 federal registration, among the first in the country, though historians place its true start around 1875. A gold medal at the 1904 St. Louis World's Fair launched its fame; Brown-Forman bought the company in 1956. Moore County itself remains dry, so the distillery sells commemorative bottles only.
production: Made from a disclosed mash bill of 80 % corn, 12 % rye, 8 % malted barley with iron-free water from Cave Spring Hollow. After distillation the spirit is mellowed drop by drop through ten feet of sugar-maple charcoal burned on site (the Lincoln County Process), then aged roughly four to five years in new charred oak made at Brown-Forman's own cooperages. Barrels are batched and bottled at 80 proof.
barrel: New charred American white oak from Brown-Forman's own cooperage, ~4–5 years in hillside barrelhouses
filtration: Charcoal-mellowed before aging (Lincoln County Process); chill filtered at bottling
coloring: No added coloring

tastingNotes.nose: Banana, caramel, vanilla, light char and sweet corn
tastingNotes.palate: Soft and sweet — toffee, banana bread, toasted oak, faint smoke
tastingNotes.finish: Short to medium, gentle char and caramel sweetness

servingTemp: On the rocks or with cola; neat at room temperature (18–22 °C) works too

awards.award: Gold Medal — St. Louis World's Fair

facts: Nearest Green, who taught Jack Daniel to distill while enslaved, was named the brand's first master distiller in a 2016 corporate recognition.
The distillery sits in a dry county: Jack Daniel's cannot be sold by the glass where it is made.
No one knows for certain what "Old No. 7" refers to; explanations range from a railway shipment number to a lost recipe.

timeline.event: Claimed federal registration of the distillery (historians suggest ~1875)
Old No. 7 wins gold at the St. Louis World's Fair
Brown-Forman acquires Jack Daniel's
Nearest Green formally recognized as first master distiller

name: Westland American Oak
description: Westland American Oak is a founding document of American single malt, the Seattle distillery's flagship since 2013 and a bottle that helped define what the category could be when the US government finally codified it in 2024. Instead of imitating Scotland, Westland treats malt like a brewer: a five-malt grain bill including Munich, extra special, pale chocolate, and brown malts, fermented with a Belgian brewer's yeast chosen for fruity esters, then matured primarily in new charred American oak. The result reads like a cross between craft beer and bourbon-country single malt — chocolate, coffee, red fruit, and vanilla custard at 46 % ABV, non-chill filtered. It is the standard bearer for the Pacific Northwest school of whiskey making and a natural bridge for stout and porter drinkers.
history: Westland was founded in 2010 by Matt Hofmann and Emerson Lamb with the explicit goal of building a Pacific Northwest single malt tradition, opening its SoDo, Seattle distillery in 2013 and releasing American Oak the same year. The distillery co-founded the American Single Malt Whiskey Commission in 2016 to lobby for a legal definition, which the TTB finalized in December 2024. French luxury group Rémy Cointreau acquired Westland in 2017, funding barley research and the Garryana oak program while the founding team's brewing-driven house style continued.
production: Brewed from a five-malt bill — Washington-grown pale malt plus Munich, extra special, pale chocolate, and brown malts (100 % malted barley) — and fermented long and cool with a Belgian brewer's yeast to build fruit esters. Twice distilled in copper pot stills, then matured principally in new charred American oak with some ex-bourbon casks, in Westland's coastal-climate warehouse. Bottled at 46 % ABV without chill filtration.
barrel: Primarily new charred American oak with ex-bourbon casks; slow coastal-climate maturation
coloring: No added coloring

tastingNotes.nose: Milk chocolate, plum, malted grain, vanilla custard and light oak
tastingNotes.palate: Creamy — cocoa, coffee, red berries, honeyed malt and baking spice
tastingNotes.finish: Medium-long, chocolatey and gently oaky with a fruity fade

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature (18–22 °C); a drop of water lifts the malt
facts: Uses five different malts, several borrowed from brewing traditions, plus a Belgian brewer's yeast.
Westland co-founded the American Single Malt Whiskey Commission; the TTB made the category official in December 2024.
Its sibling Garryana series matures whiskey in casks of Quercus garryana, an oak species native to the Pacific Northwest.

timeline.event: Westland founded in Seattle
American Oak released as the flagship single malt
Rémy Cointreau acquires Westland
American Single Malt becomes an official US whiskey category, a cause Westland championed

name: Crown Royal Deluxe
description: Crown Royal Deluxe is the best-selling Canadian whisky in the United States and the definitive image of the category: a crown-shaped decanter bottle delivered in a purple drawstring bag, created in 1939 as a gift fit for a king. The whisky itself is a blend of around fifty distinct whiskies from the Gimli, Manitoba distillery — corn-based base whiskies and flavoring ryes and bourbons-style distillates married for smoothness. Expect vanilla, butterscotch, light oak, and a wisp of rye spice in a soft, easy-drinking frame at 40 % ABV. It is a mixing mainstay across North America (whisky-and-cola, highballs, shots) and the foundation of a large family that includes Northern Harvest Rye, Black, and many flavored line extensions.
history: Samuel Bronfman of Seagram created Crown Royal in 1939 to mark the first visit of a reigning monarch to Canada — King George VI and Queen Elizabeth — presenting cases to the royal train. It stayed a Canada-only product until 1964, then conquered the American market. Production consolidated at the purpose-built Gimli distillery on Lake Winnipeg, opened in 1968. When Seagram's drinks empire was broken up in 2000, Diageo took Crown Royal, and in 2016 Northern Harvest Rye briefly made global headlines as Jim Murray's World Whisky of the Year.
production: Distilled and blended at Gimli, Manitoba, where twelve column and pot still configurations produce a range of corn, rye, and barley whiskies. Components age separately in a mix of new charred, once-used bourbon, and refill casks on a 1.5-million-barrel campus, then roughly fifty whiskies are blended and bottled at 40 % ABV. Canadian rules require a minimum of three years' aging.
barrel: Blend of new charred oak, ex-bourbon and refill casks aged by Lake Winnipeg
coloring: Caramel colouring permitted under Canadian whisky rules; not disclosed

tastingNotes.nose: Vanilla, butterscotch, apple, faint rye spice
tastingNotes.palate: Smooth and light — creamy vanilla, honey, soft oak, gentle fruit
tastingNotes.finish: Short and clean, sweet with a mild peppery flicker

servingTemp: On the rocks or in a highball; chilled or room temperature
facts: Created for the 1939 royal visit of King George VI to Canada; cases traveled aboard the royal train.
The purple bag was designed to suggest royal robes, and the bottle a crown on a cushion; the bags became a folk craft material.
Every drop is distilled at Gimli, Manitoba, on the shore of Lake Winnipeg — one of the coldest whisky-aging climates anywhere.

timeline.event: Crown Royal created by Seagram for the royal visit to Canada
First sold outside Canada, entering the US market
Gimli, Manitoba distillery opens
Diageo acquires Crown Royal in the Seagram breakup

name: Lot No. 40 Rye
style: Canadian Rye Whisky (100 % rye, copper pot still)
description: Lot No. 40 is the whisky that restored Canadian rye's reputation among serious drinkers: a 100 % rye, distilled in a single copper pot still at Hiram Walker in Windsor, Ontario, and aged entirely in new charred oak — an American-style regimen almost no other Canadian whisky follows. The all-rye grain bill and pot-still weight produce an intensely aromatic spirit: baked rye bread, clove, dark fruit, floral perfume, and bittersweet chocolate at 43 % ABV. First released in 1998 to a market that ignored it, it was discontinued, mourned by insiders, and triumphantly relaunched in 2012, going on to win top honors repeatedly at the Canadian Whisky Awards. Bartenders treat it as Canada's answer to high-end straight rye, equally at home neat or in a Manhattan.
history: The name commemorates lot number 40 in Ontario's Mosa Township, where Joshua Booth — an ancestor of the whisky's creating family line at Hiram Walker — operated one of Upper Canada's early stills in the 1790s. Lot No. 40 first appeared in 1998 as part of Corby's heritage Canadian Whisky Guild series, disappeared in the early 2000s for lack of sales, and returned in 2012 under master blender Dr. Don Livermore as craft cocktail culture rediscovered rye. It has since become a permanent flagship of Corby and Pernod Ricard's Canadian portfolio.
production: Mashed from 100 % rye grain — no corn or malted barley; enzymes handle conversion — fermented and first run through a column, then distilled in Hiram Walker's dedicated 12,000-liter copper pot still. Unusually for Canadian whisky, it matures entirely in virgin charred American oak, giving it bourbon-like structure beneath the rye perfume. Bottled at 43 % ABV.
barrel: Virgin charred American oak — rare for Canadian whisky
coloring: Canadian rules permit caramel colouring; Lot No. 40 relies on new-oak colour

tastingNotes.nose: Rye bread, clove, violet, dark cherry, toasted oak
tastingNotes.palate: Rich baking spice, bittersweet chocolate, orchard fruit, caraway and vanilla
tastingNotes.finish: Long, spicy and slightly drying, florals and pepper persisting

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature (18–22 °C) or in spirit-forward cocktails
facts: Made from 100 % rye in a single copper pot still — a rarity in the column-dominated Canadian industry.
Named for a 1790s land lot where United Empire Loyalist Joshua Booth ran one of Upper Canada's first stills.
Discontinued in the early 2000s for slow sales, it returned in 2012 and repeatedly earned Canadian Whisky of the Year honors at the Canadian Whisky Awards.

timeline.event: Lot No. 40 first released in the Canadian Whisky Guild series
Relaunched by Corby under master blender Dr. Don Livermore

name: Buffalo Trace Kentucky Straight Bourbon
description: Buffalo Trace is the flagship bourbon of the Frankfort, Kentucky distillery that shares its name, introduced in 1999 when Sazerac renamed the historic George T. Stagg plant. Bottled at 90 proof from the distillery's undisclosed low-rye mash bill (the same recipe behind Eagle Rare and George T. Stagg), it is aged roughly eight years without carrying an age statement. The profile is classic middle-of-the-road Kentucky bourbon: caramel and vanilla up front, light brown-sugar sweetness, a touch of dried fruit and baking spice, and a clean oak-driven finish. Its combination of consistent quality and a price around thirty dollars has made it one of the most recommended entry points into bourbon, and demand now periodically outstrips supply in some markets even though it remains a high-volume, nationally distributed brand.
history: The Frankfort site has distilled whiskey since the nineteenth century, trading as O.F.C. under Colonel E.H. Taylor, Jr. from 1870 and later as the George T. Stagg Distillery. It was one of a handful of plants allowed to keep operating through Prohibition under a medicinal whiskey permit. Sazerac bought the distillery in 1992, renamed it Buffalo Trace in 1999 after the ancient bison migration route that forded the Kentucky River nearby, and launched this namesake bourbon the same year as the new flagship.
production: Distilled at Buffalo Trace Distillery from the house's undisclosed Mash Bill #1, a low-rye bourbon recipe estimated at around ten percent rye. Spirit enters new charred American white oak barrels and ages in the distillery's mix of brick and metal-clad warehouses, typically for about eight years. Barrels are batched, cut to 90 proof, and chill filtered before bottling.
barrel: New charred American white oak; matures roughly eight years in Buffalo Trace's Frankfort warehouses
coloring: No added coloring (prohibited for straight bourbon)

tastingNotes.nose: Caramel, vanilla bean, sweet corn, hints of mint and molasses
tastingNotes.palate: Brown sugar and toffee, oak, dried fruit, light rye spice and anise
tastingNotes.finish: Medium length, drying oak with lingering vanilla and a whisper of pepper

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature (18–22 °C), or with a single large cube

awards.award: Double Gold — San Francisco World Spirits Competition

facts: Named for the ancient buffalo migration route that crossed the Kentucky River at Frankfort.
The distillery kept operating through Prohibition under a medicinal whiskey permit.
The exact mash bill has never been published; Buffalo Trace calls it simply Mash Bill #1.

timeline.event: Buffalo Trace launched as the distillery's new flagship bourbon
Buffalo Trace Distillery designated a National Historic Landmark

name: Eagle Rare 10 Year
description: Eagle Rare 10 Year is Buffalo Trace's age-stated older sibling to the flagship bourbon, made from the same undisclosed low-rye mash bill but matured a full decade and bottled at 90 proof. The extra years shift the profile toward polished oak, leather, and toasted nuts layered over the house caramel-vanilla core, with a drier, longer finish than the standard Buffalo Trace. For years it was an under-the-radar value; the bourbon boom of the 2010s turned it into one of the most hunted shelf bottles in America, and it now sells out on allocation in most markets with street prices well above its suggested retail. It remains a benchmark for what ten years in Kentucky oak does to a gentle mash bill.
history: The Eagle Rare brand was created in 1975 by Seagram as a 101-proof, ten-year bourbon, one of the last new bourbon brands launched before the 1980s glut. Sazerac acquired it in 1989 and moved production to what became Buffalo Trace Distillery. From 2005 it was sold as a single-barrel ten-year at 90 proof; in 2018 the words "single barrel" quietly left the label because the automated bottling line could not guarantee that every bottle came from one barrel.
production: Distilled from Buffalo Trace's undisclosed low-rye Mash Bill #1 and aged at least ten years in new charred American oak in the Frankfort warehouses. Barrels destined for Eagle Rare are selected for a drier, oakier character than those batched for Buffalo Trace, then cut to 90 proof and chill filtered.
barrel: New charred American white oak, minimum 10 years in Buffalo Trace warehouses
coloring: No added coloring (prohibited for straight bourbon)

tastingNotes.nose: Toffee, orange peel, aged oak, leather, hints of herbs and toasted almond
tastingNotes.palate: Caramel and cocoa, dried cherry, tobacco leaf, substantial but rounded oak
tastingNotes.finish: Long and drying, oak and leather with lingering dark sweetness

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature (18–22 °C); stands up to a large cube
facts: Launched by Seagram in 1975 as one of the last new bourbon brands of that era.
"Single Barrel" was removed from the label in 2018 because the bottling line could not guarantee it.
A 17-year version of Eagle Rare is part of the annual Buffalo Trace Antique Collection.

timeline.event: Eagle Rare introduced by Seagram at 101 proof
Brand acquired by Sazerac
Relaunched as a 90-proof single-barrel 10-year from Buffalo Trace
"Single Barrel" designation removed from the label

name: E.H. Taylor, Jr. Small Batch
description: E.H. Taylor, Jr. Small Batch is Buffalo Trace's tribute to Colonel Edmund Haynes Taylor, Jr., the nineteenth-century distiller and industry reformer who once owned the Frankfort site. It is a bottled-in-bond bourbon — by law the product of one distillery, one distilling season, aged at least four years in a federally bonded warehouse, and bottled at exactly 100 proof. Made from the distillery's low-rye Mash Bill #1 and sold in a distinctive embossed bottle inside a canister recalling Taylor-era packaging, it layers butterscotch, ripe apple, and baking spice over firm oak. Since its 2011 debut it has become a heavily allocated shelf trophy, routinely selling for more than double its suggested price, while remaining one of the most complete 100-proof pours in the Buffalo Trace stable.
history: Colonel E.H. Taylor, Jr. built the O.F.C. Distillery on the Frankfort riverbank in 1870, pioneered climate-controlled aging warehouses, and lobbied for the Bottled-in-Bond Act of 1897, the first federal consumer-protection law for whiskey. Buffalo Trace launched the E.H. Taylor line in 2011 on the strength of that legacy, beginning with Old Fashioned Sour Mash and this Small Batch, and has since added Single Barrel, Barrel Proof, Straight Rye, and various limited releases.
production: Distilled from Buffalo Trace's undisclosed low-rye Mash Bill #1 during a single distilling season, as bond rules require. Aging takes place in Warehouse C, the 1881 brick warehouse Taylor himself built, among others. Batched barrels are married, cut to precisely 100 proof, and bottled under the Bottled-in-Bond Act's supervision requirements.
barrel: New charred American white oak; aged in bonded warehouses including Taylor's 1881 Warehouse C
coloring: No added coloring (prohibited for straight bourbon)

tastingNotes.nose: Butterscotch, caramel corn, ripe apple, allspice and old oak
tastingNotes.palate: Toffee and vanilla, cinnamon bark, candied orange, firm mid-palate oak
tastingNotes.finish: Long, warming and spicy, with lingering caramel and tobacco

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature (18–22 °C); a few drops of water soften the proof
facts: Colonel Taylor championed the Bottled-in-Bond Act of 1897, the standard this bourbon is made to.
Some of the whiskey ages in Warehouse C, which Taylor built in 1881.
Taylor also served as mayor of Frankfort and helped found what became Buffalo Trace Distillery.

timeline.event: Bottled-in-Bond Act passed with Taylor's backing
E.H. Taylor, Jr. line launched, including Small Batch

name: W.L. Weller Special Reserve
description: W.L. Weller Special Reserve is the green-label entry point to Buffalo Trace's wheated bourbon line, in which wheat replaces rye as the flavoring grain. The result is a notably soft, sweet bourbon — honey, caramel, and fresh bread rather than rye's pepper and mint — bottled at 90 proof without an age statement. Because it shares its undisclosed wheated mash bill with the Van Winkle bourbons made at the same distillery, Weller became a target of "poor man's Pappy" hunting during the bourbon boom, and this once-ubiquitous budget bottle is now allocated in most American markets, commonly reselling at two or more times its modest suggested price. On its own merits it is a gentle, approachable pour and a textbook introduction to the wheated style.
history: The brand honors William Larue Weller, a Louisville whiskey merchant active from 1849 whose firm is traditionally credited with popularizing wheated bourbon. His company later merged with the Stitzel distillery to form Stitzel-Weller, which opened in 1935 with Julian "Pappy" Van Winkle, Sr. at the helm. After the brand passed through corporate hands, Sazerac acquired Weller in 1999 and production settled at Buffalo Trace, where the line has since expanded to Antique 107, 12 Year, Full Proof, Single Barrel, and C.Y.P.B.
production: Made from Buffalo Trace's undisclosed wheated mash bill — corn, winter wheat, and malted barley with no rye — the same recipe used for the Van Winkle bourbons. Aged roughly six to seven years in new charred oak, batched, cut to 90 proof, and chill filtered before bottling.
barrel: New charred American white oak, aged in Buffalo Trace's Frankfort warehouses
coloring: No added coloring (prohibited for straight bourbon)

tastingNotes.nose: Honey, caramel, fresh bread dough, vanilla and a touch of stone fruit
tastingNotes.palate: Soft and sweet — butterscotch, honeyed corn, gentle oak, faint cinnamon
tastingNotes.finish: Medium-short, smooth and mellow with lingering honey and vanilla

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature (18–22 °C), or over one large cube
facts: Shares its wheated mash bill with the Van Winkle bourbons, earning the nickname "poor man's Pappy".
William Larue Weller's namesake barrel-proof bourbon is part of the Buffalo Trace Antique Collection.
Wheated bourbon substitutes wheat for rye as the secondary grain, softening the spice.

timeline.event: William Larue Weller enters the Louisville whiskey trade
Stitzel-Weller Distillery opens, carrying the Weller wheated tradition
Sazerac acquires the W.L. Weller brand; production at Buffalo Trace

name: Blanton's Original Single Barrel
description: Blanton's Original is generally recognized as the first commercially marketed single-barrel bourbon, created in 1984 by master distiller Elmer T. Lee and sold in a round grenade-shaped decanter topped with a horse-and-jockey stopper. Every bottle is drawn from one barrel — no batching — so character varies bottle to bottle around a core of caramel, citrus, honey, and the spicier snap of Buffalo Trace's higher-rye Mash Bill #2. Bottled at 93 proof with the barrel number, warehouse, and dump date handwritten on the label, it became a global status bottle in the 2010s; American allocations vanish on arrival and street prices run well beyond the suggested retail. It remains the template that the entire single-barrel premium bourbon category grew from.
history: When Age International asked Elmer T. Lee to create a premium bourbon for the Japanese market in 1984, he recalled how former distillery president Colonel Albert B. Blanton entertained guests with bourbon drawn from single "honey barrels" in Warehouse H. Lee named the whiskey for Blanton and launched it as the first modern single-barrel bourbon. The brand is owned by Age International (majority held by Japan's Takara Shuzo since 1992) and distilled under contract at Buffalo Trace, with much of its output long directed to export markets.
production: Distilled from Buffalo Trace's undisclosed higher-rye Mash Bill #2 (shared with Elmer T. Lee and Rock Hill Farms) and aged in Warehouse H, the distillery's metal-clad warehouse, whose faster temperature swings accelerate maturation. Each barrel is dumped individually — typically after six to eight years — cut to 93 proof, and bottled one barrel at a time.
barrel: New charred American white oak, aged in metal-clad Warehouse H at Buffalo Trace
coloring: No added coloring (prohibited for straight bourbon)

tastingNotes.nose: Caramel corn, orange zest, vanilla, fresh-cut oak and a flash of rye spice
tastingNotes.palate: Honey and butterscotch, citrus, clove and pepper, creamy mid-weight body
tastingNotes.finish: Medium-long, spiced caramel fading to soft oak and citrus peel

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature (18–22 °C)
facts: Widely credited as the first commercially marketed single-barrel bourbon (1984).
The eight stopper designs spell B-L-A-N-T-O-N-S, each showing a horse and jockey at a different stride of a race.
Warehouse H is metal-clad, a design Colonel Blanton favored for faster aging.

timeline.event: Blanton's launched as the first marketed single-barrel bourbon
Takara Shuzo takes a majority stake in brand owner Age International
Collectible horse-and-jockey stopper series spelling the brand name introduced

name: Hennessy V.S
description: Hennessy V.S is the best-selling cognac on earth and, for a large share of drinkers worldwide, the default image of the category. It is a blend of roughly forty eaux-de-vie drawn from across the Cognac appellation's crus, each distilled twice in copper Charentais pot stills and aged in French oak for at least the two years the VS (Very Special) designation requires — the youngest tier in cognac's VS / VSOP / XO ladder (minimums of two, four and ten years respectively). The style is direct and youthful: grape, toasted oak, vanilla and a peppery edge, built for mixing as much as sipping. Hennessy alone accounts for around forty percent of global cognac sales, and V.S is the engine of that volume, with a cultural footprint — especially in the United States and in hip-hop — that no other brandy approaches.
history: Richard Hennessy, an Irish émigré from County Cork who had served in French military service, founded the trading house in 1765 and began shipping barrels of cognac abroad. His descendant Maurice Hennessy introduced the star-grading system in 1865, and the three-star cognac that resulted is the direct ancestor of today's V.S. The house merged its distribution with Moët & Chandon in 1971, a partnership that became a cornerstone of LVMH in 1987. Through eight generations, blending has remained in the hands of the Fillioux family, currently master blender Renaud Fillioux de Gironde.
production: White wine from ugni blanc grapes — thin, acidic and ideal for distillation — is double-distilled in copper pot stills by Hennessy and a network of well over a thousand growers and distillers. The young eaux-de-vie age in French oak casks, are tasted daily by the house tasting committee, and are blended to a consistent V.S profile before proofing to 40% ABV.
barrel: French oak casks (Limousin and Tronçais), youngest component aged at least 2 years
coloring: Cognac AOC rules permit minor caramel adjustment; the house does not disclose specifics

tastingNotes.nose: Fresh grape and apple, toasted oak, vanilla, a hint of citrus peel
tastingNotes.palate: Lively and warming — caramel, roasted almond, clove and light chocolate over a grapey core
tastingNotes.finish: Medium length, oak spice and vanilla with a faint bite of youth

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature (18–20 °C), over ice, or in cocktails
facts: Hennessy accounts for roughly 40% of the world's cognac sales — more than 8 million cases a year.
The founder, Richard Hennessy, was an Irishman from County Cork; the brand remains hugely popular in Ireland.
The Fillioux family has supplied Hennessy's master blenders for eight consecutive generations.
V.S descends from the 'three-star' grade Maurice Hennessy devised in 1865.

timeline.event: Richard Hennessy founds the trading house in Cognac
Maurice Hennessy introduces the star classification — origin of today's V.S
Hennessy merges with Moët & Chandon, later a founding pillar of LVMH (1987)

name: Rémy Martin XO
description: Rémy Martin XO is the house's flagship luxury blend below the fabled Louis XIII, and a benchmark for the XO (Extra Old) tier, whose youngest eau-de-vie must now be at least ten years old. It carries the Fine Champagne appellation — a blend of Grande Champagne and Petite Champagne fruit with at least half from Grande Champagne — the chalky heartland crus prized for eaux-de-vie that age longest and most gracefully. The house describes it as a marriage of up to 400 eaux-de-vie, around 85% Grande Champagne, aged well past the legal minimum. The result is opulent: candied orange, ripe stone fruit, fig, cinnamon and the faint mushroomy-nutty character the region calls rancio. Presented in a curved decanter-style bottle, it is one of the most widely recognized prestige cognacs in the world.
history: The house traces to 1724, when winegrower Rémy Martin began selling cognac under his own name; the centaur emblem, drawn from founder lore around the Sagittarius zodiac, arrived in 1870. Rémy Martin built its modern identity on Fine Champagne blends, above all the V.S.O.P that became a global standard. XO Special launched in 1981 to crown the core range and has been re-dressed several times since, becoming a fixture of duty-free and gift culture. The brand anchors the Rémy Cointreau group, with cellar master Baptiste Loiseau overseeing the blend since 2014.
production: Ugni blanc wines from the chalk soils of the Grande and Petite Champagne crus are distilled on the lees in copper pot stills, a house practice that concentrates aromatic richness and body. The eaux-de-vie then mature for a decade and often far longer in Limousin oak, with older, less active casks preserving fruit while slowly building rancio complexity. Finally the cellar master marries hundreds of components into the consistent Fine Champagne XO profile and brings it to 40% ABV.
barrel: Limousin oak casks, youngest component at least 10 years old
coloring: Cognac AOC rules permit minor caramel adjustment; the house does not disclose specifics

tastingNotes.nose: Candied orange, ripe plum and fig, jasmine, cinnamon, polished oak
tastingNotes.palate: Velvety and layered — baked apricot, hazelnut, gingerbread, dark chocolate and early rancio
tastingNotes.finish: Very long, honeyed spice fading into leather and dried fruit

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature (about 20 °C); cup the glass briefly to warm
facts: Fine Champagne on a label means a Grande + Petite Champagne blend with at least 50% Grande Champagne — Rémy XO is about 85%.
The house says up to 400 separate eaux-de-vie can go into a single XO blend.
Rémy Martin is one of the few large houses focused almost entirely on Champagne-cru cognacs.
The centaur emblem has marked Rémy Martin bottles since 1870.

timeline.event: Rémy Martin founds his cognac house
Rémy Martin XO Special launched
XO minimum age across cognac rises from 6 to 10 years

name: Pierre Ferrand 1840 Original Formula
description: Pierre Ferrand 1840 Original Formula is a cognac built deliberately for the cocktail shaker. Developed by Maison Ferrand's Alexandre Gabriel with drinks historian David Wondrich, it revives the style of cognac that dominated mixed drinks in the mid-nineteenth century, when brandy — not whiskey — was the base of the American bar. Bottled at 45% ABV rather than the modern standard 40%, it keeps its footing against citrus, sugar and dilution, which is why it has become the modern default answer to 'what cognac goes in a Sidecar or a cognac Sazerac'. The fruit comes from Grande Champagne, cognac's premier cru, and the blend leans young, bright and grapey, with enough oak and spice to read clearly in a drink. Its price and proof have made it a fixture on serious cocktail-bar shelves worldwide.
history: Alexandre Gabriel, who revived the Ferrand name in 1989 as a négociant-producer rooted in Grande Champagne, released 1840 Original Formula around 2010 amid the craft-cocktail renaissance. The project grew out of research with David Wondrich into nineteenth-century brandy styles and old bottle strengths; the packaging itself echoes an antique cognac bottle from Gabriel's collection. It joined Ferrand's Ambre and Réserve as the bar-focused corner of the range, and Ferrand went on to build a portfolio including Citadelle gin and the Planteray (ex-Plantation) rums.
production: Ugni blanc grown on the chalky slopes of Grande Champagne, cognac's first cru, is fermented to a tart low-alcohol wine and double-distilled in Charentais copper pot stills. The blend deliberately favors relatively young, aromatic eaux-de-vie matured in French oak, keeping bright grape and citrus character rather than heavy cask influence. It is assembled and bottled at 45% ABV — closer to nineteenth-century strengths — so the cognac's fruit and spice survive shaking, stirring and dilution in a cocktail.
barrel: French oak casks

tastingNotes.nose: Ripe grape, apricot, orange zest, toasted oak and a floral lift
tastingNotes.palate: Bold and juicy — stone fruit, honey, cinnamon and white pepper at punchy strength
tastingNotes.finish: Warm and medium-long, citrus peel and oak spice

servingTemp: Room temperature neat, or chilled in stirred and shaken cocktails
facts: Created with cocktail historian David Wondrich to recreate the higher-proof cognacs of the 1840s American bar.
Bottled at 45% ABV specifically so the cognac holds up to dilution in cocktails.
The original Sazerac was a cognac drink — rye only took over after phylloxera devastated French vineyards in the 1870s.
Made by Maison Ferrand, the same house behind Planteray rum and Citadelle gin.

timeline.event: 1840 Original Formula launched, developed with David Wondrich

name: Château de Laubade VSOP
description: Château de Laubade VSOP is the calling card of one of the largest family-owned estates in Bas-Armagnac, the western sub-region whose sandy soils are considered the source of the appellation's finest brandies. Where cognac is dominated by big houses buying from thousands of growers, Laubade is a single estate: the grapes, distillation, cooperage-aged maturation and blending all happen on the property at Sorbets in the Gers. The VSOP blends eaux-de-vie from the estate's four grape varieties aged well beyond the four-year legal minimum for the designation, and it shows armagnac's character next to cognac's polish — a touch more rustic and expansive, with prune, baked apple, toffee, toasted oak and gentle earth. It is widely used as the introduction to the category and delivers notable depth for its price.
history: The château and its estate were established in 1870 at Sorbets in Gascony. In 1974 the Lesgourgues family acquired the property and progressively rebuilt it into a reference producer, expanding the vineyard to around a hundred hectares and modernizing while keeping estate distillation and long cellar aging. Armagnac itself is France's oldest documented spirit — distillation in Gascony is recorded as early as 1310 — though the region's remoteness kept it in cognac's commercial shadow. Laubade, run today by the second generation of Lesgourgues brothers, has become one of the appellation's most visible ambassadors abroad.
production: Estate-grown ugni blanc, baco 22A, folle blanche and colombard are picked and vinified separately, then distilled in a single continuous pass through a copper alambic armagnacais. The spirit comes off the still at a lower strength than double-distilled cognac, keeping more of the wine's congeners and aromatic richness. Each variety's eaux-de-vie mature separately in French oak — traditionally tannic Gascon oak — in the estate cellars before being blended for the VSOP and brought to 40% ABV.
barrel: French oak casks, traditionally Gascon oak, aged in estate cellars

tastingNotes.nose: Prune, baked apple, vanilla pod, toasted oak and a whiff of damp earth
tastingNotes.palate: Round and generous — toffee, dried apricot, hazelnut, cocoa and warm baking spice
tastingNotes.finish: Medium-long, drying oak with lingering dried fruit and pepper

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature (18–20 °C), classically after dinner
facts: Armagnac is France's oldest brandy — Gascon distillation was documented around 1310, centuries before cognac's rise.
Unlike most cognac, armagnac is typically distilled once in a continuous alambic armagnacais, giving a fuller, more rustic spirit.
Laubade is a single estate: growing, distilling, aging and blending all happen on the property at Sorbets.
The estate's roughly 100 hectares of vines make it one of the largest family holdings in Bas-Armagnac.

timeline.event: Château de Laubade established at Sorbets in the Gers
Lesgourgues family acquires and begins rebuilding the estate

name: Christian Drouin Sélection Calvados
description: Christian Drouin Sélection is the entry point to one of Normandy's most respected calvados houses and a textbook introduction to apple brandy itself. It is a blend of relatively young calvados from the Pays d'Auge, the appellation's prestige zone, where the spirit must be double-distilled in pot stills from cider made of traditional bitter, bittersweet, sweet and sharp apple varieties. Youth is the point here: the Sélection leads with fresh orchard fruit — baked apple, pear skin, cider funk — over light oak, vanilla and honey, closer to the orchard than to the cellar. In Normandy such a glass is as likely to appear mid-meal as after it, in the tradition of the trou normand, a small calvados taken between courses. It mixes well, too, in apple-brandy takes on classic sours and highballs.
history: Christian Drouin, a businessman with Norman roots, bought a farm in 1960 and began laying down calvados, buying and distilling with obsessive attention to older vintages. His son Christian Jr. professionalized the house, and grandson Guillaume Drouin, a trained oenologist, has run it since the mid-2000s, making Drouin one of calvados's most internationally visible producers. The family is famous for a vintage library reaching back decades, sold under the Coeur de Lion name, and operates from a picture-book half-timbered farmstead at Coudray-Rabut near Pont-l'Évêque.
production: Dozens of traditional cider-apple varieties — spanning the bitter, bittersweet, sweet and sharp classes that give calvados structure, aroma and acidity — are pressed and slowly fermented into a dry cider. As the Pays d'Auge appellation requires, the cider is then double-distilled in copper pot stills, concentrating the fruit. The young spirit ages a legal minimum of two years in French oak casks, many previously seasoned by older calvados so the wood stays gentle, before blending and bottling at 40% ABV.
barrel: French oak casks of various ages and sizes

tastingNotes.nose: Fresh baked apple, pear, cider, light vanilla and hay
tastingNotes.palate: Bright orchard fruit, honey, gentle oak and a soft farmhouse funk
tastingNotes.finish: Clean and medium, apple skin and delicate spice

servingTemp: Slightly cool or room temperature (16–20 °C); traditionally as a trou normand between courses
facts: Pays d'Auge is the top calvados appellation, requiring double distillation in pot stills.
The trou normand — a mid-meal shot of calvados to 'make room' — is a living Norman tradition.
Christian Drouin keeps a celebrated library of vintage calvados stretching back to the mid-20th century.
Calvados must age at least two years in oak before it can carry the name.

timeline.event: Christian Drouin begins producing and collecting calvados in Normandy
Third-generation Guillaume Drouin takes over production

name: Green Chartreuse
description: Green Chartreuse is the most storied herbal liqueur in the world: a 55% ABV maceration and distillation of 130 plants, flowers and spices made by Carthusian monks to a recipe only two of them know in full at any time. Its electric yellow-green color — entirely natural, from the plants themselves — is so distinctive that it named the color chartreuse rather than the other way around. The flavor is unlike anything else: an intense wave of alpine herbs, mint, anise, pine and pepper carried on syrupy sweetness and serious heat, endlessly layered and long. Long a niche digestif, it became indispensable to modern bartending through cocktails like the Last Word. Since the monks capped production in the early 2020s to preserve their contemplative life, bottles have been allocated worldwide and street prices routinely run well above the official tag.
history: In 1605 the Carthusian monks received a manuscript for an 'elixir of long life' from Marshal François Hannibal d'Estrées. The recipe was fully worked out at the Grande Chartreuse monastery by 1737, and the milder Green Chartreuse liqueur followed in 1764. The order was expelled from France in 1903 and made Chartreuse in Tarragona, Spain, for decades; production returned home and, after a 1935 landslide destroyed the Fourvoirie distillery, settled in Voiron and later Aiguenoire. In 2023 the order publicly confirmed it would cap production, citing monastic priorities and the environment, tipping the liqueur into permanent allocation.
production: Only two monks prepare the secret blend of 130 botanicals, sorted at the monastery and macerated in alcohol. The liquid is distilled at the Aiguenoire distillery, colored solely by a final maceration of plants, sweetened, and matured in the enormous oak vats of the order's cellars — among the longest liqueur cellars in the world — before bottling at 55% ABV.
barrel: Matured in large oak vats in the Carthusians' cellars
coloring: None — the green comes entirely from macerated plants (natural chlorophyll)

tastingNotes.nose: Crushed alpine herbs, mint, anise, pine resin, citrus and honey
tastingNotes.palate: Sweet, hot and intensely botanical — spearmint, basil, clove, gentian bitterness, pepper and candied angelica in successive waves
tastingNotes.finish: Extremely long, warming, mentholated and faintly bitter

servingTemp: Well chilled or over ice as a digestif; room temperature to explore the botanicals
facts: Only two Carthusian monks know the complete 130-plant recipe at any given time.
The color chartreuse is named after the liqueur, not the reverse.
From 1903 to 1989 a parallel 'Tarragona' Chartreuse was made in Spain after the monks' expulsion from France; old bottles are prized collectibles.
The monks announced in 2023 that production would stay capped so monastic life is not consumed by the liqueur business — the root of today's shortages.

timeline.event: Carthusians receive the elixir manuscript from Marshal d'Estrées
Green Chartreuse liqueur first produced
Landslide destroys the Fourvoirie distillery; production moves to Voiron
The order confirms capped production; worldwide allocation follows

name: Campari
description: Campari is the scarlet spine of Italian aperitivo culture and one of the most important cocktail ingredients ever created. A bitter infusion of herbs, aromatic plants and fruit in alcohol and water — the recipe is a closely guarded secret, with chinotto and cascarilla often speculated — it delivers a bracing wave of bitter orange, gentian-like bite and clove over a lightly syrupy body. Bottling strength varies by market from 20.5% to 28.5% ABV; the US version is 24%. Where its stablemate Aperol is soft, sweet and half as strong, Campari is assertively bitter and boozy, which is exactly what the Negroni, Americano and Boulevardier are built on. Its poster art, red-lit bars and the Camparino café in Milan's Galleria made it as much a design icon as a drink.
history: Gaspare Campari developed his 'bitter' in 1860 in Novara and brought it to Milan, where the family café beside the Duomo made it famous. His son Davide opened the Camparino bar in the Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II in 1915 and pushed the brand internationally, insisting bars display the Campari sign. The company commissioned era-defining poster artists — and Fortunato Depero's cone-shaped Camparisoda bottle of 1932 became a Futurist design landmark. Until 2006 the red came from carmine (cochineal insects); artificial coloring replaced it in most markets. Davide Campari-Milano grew by acquisition into today's Campari Group, one of the world's largest spirits companies.
production: The recipe is one of the drinks industry's best-kept secrets: the company acknowledges only an infusion of bitter herbs, aromatic plants and fruit in alcohol and water, said to be unchanged since 1860 and known in full to just a handful of people. Botanicals often speculated include chinotto and cascarilla, though Campari has never confirmed any. The concentrated infusion is blended with sugar, water and coloring, then bottled without any barrel aging; bottling strength varies by market from 20.5% to 28.5% ABV, with the United States receiving 24%.
coloring: Artificial red coloring in most markets since 2006; previously carmine from cochineal

tastingNotes.nose: Bitter orange peel, cherry-cola herbs, clove and a medicinal edge
tastingNotes.palate: Immediately bitter — orange pith, quinine-like bite, rhubarb and warm spice over light syrup
tastingNotes.finish: Long, dry and cleansing, the bitterness inviting another sip

servingTemp: Over ice with soda or in stirred cocktails; store at room temperature
facts: The recipe has reportedly remained unchanged since 1860 and is known to only a handful of people.
Campari's red came from cochineal insects (carmine) until 2006.
Fortunato Depero's 1932 cone-shaped Camparisoda bottle was a pioneering piece of single-serve packaging design.
ABV varies by market from 20.5% to 28.5% — the US gets 24%.

timeline.event: Gaspare Campari creates his bitter in Novara
Camparino bar opens in Milan's Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II
Carmine (cochineal) coloring replaced with artificial dye in most markets

name: Aperol
description: Aperol is the gentle gateway of Italian bitters — a neon-orange aperitivo whose entire modern identity is fused to one drink, the Aperol Spritz. Its cited flavorings include sweet and bitter oranges, rhubarb, gentian and cinchona, but everything about it is dialed softer than its red sibling Campari: at 11% ABV it carries less than half the alcohol (Campari ranges 20.5–28.5% by market, 24% in the US), and its bitterness is a mild, zesty undertow beneath candied orange and honeyed sweetness. That approachability is strategic. Mixed 3-2-1 with prosecco and soda over ice, it built one of the great drinks-marketing successes of the century, turning the terrace spritz into a global summer ritual and pulling an entire generation toward bitter flavors one easy glass at a time.
history: Brothers Luigi and Silvio Barbieri launched Aperol at the 1919 Padua International Fair after years refining their father's liqueur business. The name comes from 'apero', French slang for aperitif. It stayed a regional Veneto favorite — marketed early on for its lightness — until Campari Group acquired the Barbieri brands in 2003 and put its weight behind the Spritz serve. The 3-2-1 formula and orange-everything branding carried Aperol from Venetian cafés to worldwide ubiquity, making it one of the fastest-growing spirit brands of the 2010s and 2020s.
production: Aperol is built on a proprietary infusion of botanicals in neutral alcohol — the company cites sweet and bitter oranges, rhubarb, gentian and cinchona among the ingredients, with the full recipe kept secret and said to be essentially unchanged since 1919. The aromatic infusion is blended with sugar, water and coloring and bottled without aging at just 11% ABV in most markets (15% in Germany), roughly half the strength of Campari — a deliberate choice that keeps the finished Spritz at wine-like potency.
coloring: Contains food coloring (market-dependent)

tastingNotes.nose: Candied orange, tangerine, rhubarb and light herbs
tastingNotes.palate: Sweet orange and honey first, then a soft bitter twist of gentian and quinine
tastingNotes.finish: Short, clean and gently bitter — built for refreshment

servingTemp: In a spritz over plenty of ice, well chilled (4–6 °C)
facts: At 11% ABV, Aperol has less than half the alcohol of Campari — and markedly gentler bitterness.
Launched at the Padua International Fair in 1919 by the Barbieri brothers.
The Aperol Spritz's 3-2-1 build (prosecco, Aperol, soda) is printed on the bottle in many markets.
Aperol sold in Germany is bottled stronger, at 15% ABV.

timeline.event: Aperol debuts at the Padua International Fair
Campari Group acquires the Barbieri brands, including Aperol

name: Grand Marnier Cordon Rouge
description: Grand Marnier Cordon Rouge occupies a category of one: an orange liqueur built on a majority base of true cognac rather than neutral spirit. The blend is 51% cognac married with a distilled essence of bitter oranges — Citrus bigaradia, grown largely in the Caribbean and picked green when their oils are most intense — then sweetened and rested. At a full 40% ABV it drinks like a spirit as much as a liqueur, layering candied orange over cognac's vanilla, toffee and oak. That double identity gives it unusual range: an after-dinner sipper, the upgrade that turns a Margarita 'Cadillac', a classic Sidecar component, and the flambé heart of crêpes Suzette. The red-ribboned bottle, shaped like a cognac still's silhouette, has been a back-bar constant for well over a century.
history: The Lapostolle family founded a distillery at Neauphle-le-Château in 1827. In 1880 Louis-Alexandre Marnier Lapostolle, who had married into the family, created the cognac-and-bitter-orange liqueur; house lore credits hotelier César Ritz with suggesting the confident name 'Grand Marnier' at a time when exotic oranges were a luxury. The liqueur became a fixture of grand hotel dining and French pâtisserie. Marnier Lapostolle remained family-controlled until Campari Group acquired the company in 2016, and Cordon Rouge remains the flagship above a range of cuvées built on older cognacs.
production: Bitter oranges of the Citrus bigaradia variety, grown mainly in the Caribbean, are harvested while still green and their peels sun-dried to concentrate the aromatic oils. The peels are macerated in alcohol and distilled into an intense orange essence, which is then blended with cognac from the Charente — 51% of the finished liqueur — along with sugar and water. The married blend rests before bottling at a spirit-strength 40% ABV; the cognac component itself has been aged in French oak casks.
barrel: Cognac component aged in French oak before blending

tastingNotes.nose: Candied bitter orange, vanilla, toffee and polished oak
tastingNotes.palate: Rich orange marmalade over cognac depth — caramel, hazelnut, baking spice
tastingNotes.finish: Long and warm, orange zest drying into oak

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature as a digestif, chilled or in cocktails
facts: The blend is 51% genuine cognac — unique among the major orange liqueurs.
Its bigaradia bitter oranges are harvested green, when the peel oils are most aromatic.
House lore says César Ritz suggested the name 'Grand Marnier'.
Adding it to a Margarita in place of triple sec creates the 'Cadillac Margarita'.

timeline.event: Lapostolle family founds its distillery at Neauphle-le-Château
Louis-Alexandre Marnier Lapostolle creates Grand Marnier Cordon Rouge
Campari Group acquires Société des Produits Marnier Lapostolle

name: St-Germain Elderflower Liqueur
description: St-Germain is the modern classic that put elderflower on the world's back bars. Launched in 2007, it is made from fresh wild elderflower blossoms (Sambucus nigra) gathered in late spring in France; the delicate petals are macerated quickly before their aroma fades, then blended with eau-de-vie, sugar and a touch of citrus. The result pours pale gold and tastes like the smell of a June hedgerow — lychee, pear, honeysuckle and grapefruit zest with gentle 20% ABV sweetness. Bartenders reached for it so reflexively in the 2010s that it earned the nickname 'bartender's ketchup': a splash flatters gin, sparkling wine and citrus alike. The fluted art-deco bottle, styled after a Belle Époque Paris that matches its Saint-Germain-des-Prés name, is recognizable across a crowded room.
history: American drinks entrepreneur Robert J. Cooper, third generation of a liqueur family (his father created Chambord), developed St-Germain and launched it in 2007 through his Cooper Spirits Company. It arrived precisely as the craft-cocktail revival was searching for fresh, natural modifiers and became one of the fastest-adopted new liqueurs in memory, spawning a wave of elderflower imitators. Bacardi acquired the brand in 2013. Cooper died in 2016 at 39; the liqueur he created remains the defining product of the floral-liqueur category.
production: Fresh elderflower blossoms are harvested once a year, over a window of only a few weeks in late spring in France, and must be processed almost immediately — the blossoms' volatile aroma fades within days of picking.
