The flowers are macerated in spirit to draw out their perfume, and the resulting extract is blended with eau-de-vie, sugar and subtle citrus notes before bottling at 20% ABV. There is no aging; freshness is the entire point, and each year's production is bound to that single spring harvest.

tastingNotes.nose: Honeysuckle, lychee, ripe pear and cut grass
tastingNotes.palate: Silky and floral-sweet — elderflower, white peach, grapefruit zest and honey
tastingNotes.finish: Soft and fragrant, citrus keeping the sweetness in check

servingTemp: Chilled, in spritzes and cocktails; best used within months of opening
facts: Nicknamed the 'bartender's ketchup' for how universally it was splashed into late-2000s cocktails.
The elderflower harvest lasts only a few weeks each spring, and blossoms must be processed quickly before the aroma fades.
Creator Rob Cooper was liqueur royalty — his father created Chambord.
The art-deco bottle is inspired by the Belle Époque Paris of its Saint-Germain-des-Prés namesake.

timeline.event: Rob Cooper launches St-Germain
Bacardi acquires the brand

name: Luxardo Maraschino Originale
description: Luxardo Maraschino Originale is the survivor and standard-bearer of maraschino, the crystal-clear cherry liqueur that classic cocktails cannot do without. Distilled — not merely infused — from sour marasca cherries, it tastes nothing like candy cherries: instead there is bitter almond from the cherry stones, rose and orange blossom, hay and a funky, almost bandage-like complexity that bartenders prize for the depth a quarter-ounce adds. It is essential to the Last Word, Aviation, Martinez and Hemingway Daiquiri. The squat bottle in its hand-fitted straw sleeve recalls the era when the wrap protected bottles shipped from Dalmatia. Luxardo grows its own marasca orchards in the Euganean Hills and also produces the famous jarred Luxardo cherries, the garnish counterpart to the liqueur.
history: Girolamo Luxardo founded the distillery in 1821 in Zara, Dalmatia (now Zadar, Croatia), after his wife Maria Canevari perfected a refined version of the local rosolio maraschino; the firm earned imperial privileges and worldwide exports. The Second World War destroyed the Zara distillery and killed most of the family. The sole surviving brother, Giorgio Luxardo, re-founded the company at Torreglia in Italy's Veneto in 1947, replanting marasca cherry trees carried from Dalmatia. Still family-owned into its sixth generation, Luxardo rode the 2000s cocktail revival back to indispensability.
production: Marasca cherries from the family's own orchards in the Euganean Hills — flesh, stones and all, with leaves contributing an aromatic, gently bitter edge — are macerated at length in larchwood vats before distillation in small copper pot stills. The cherry distillate then rests for about two years in vats of Finnish ash, a wood chosen precisely because it imparts no color or tannin, and is finally sweetened with sugar and brought to 32% ABV, bottled crystal clear.
barrel: Distillate rested about two years in Finnish ash vats (no color imparted)
coloring: None — bottled crystal clear

tastingNotes.nose: Bitter almond, cherry blossom, rose water and a funky herbal edge
tastingNotes.palate: Sweet but sharply aromatic — marzipan, sour cherry, orange flower and hay
tastingNotes.finish: Medium, drying almond-pit bitterness beneath the sugar

servingTemp: Room temperature for mixing; chilled neat as a digestif
facts: The almond note comes from distilling the cherries with their stones.
After WWII destroyed the Zara distillery, Giorgio Luxardo replanted Dalmatian marasca saplings at Torreglia — the orchards still supply the house.
The straw sleeve is a relic of 19th-century shipping protection, originally fitted by hand by workers called impagliatrici.
Luxardo also makes the candied marasca 'Luxardo cherries' — no relation to neon-red maraschino garnish cherries.

timeline.event: Girolamo Luxardo founds the distillery in Zara, Dalmatia
Giorgio Luxardo re-establishes the firm at Torreglia, Veneto, after the war

name: Fernet-Branca
description: Fernet-Branca is the uncompromising extreme of the amaro family: a near-black, minty-bitter digestif of 27 herbs and roots from four continents, bottled at 39% ABV with barely any sweetness to soften the blow. Disclosed ingredients include myrrh, saffron, rhubarb, chamomile, gentian, bitter orange and aloe; the complete formula is a family secret, and the company's president traditionally supervises the blending of the aromatics personally. Menthol coolness, medicinal roots and a saffron-earthy depth make the first sip a rite of passage — and a lifelong habit for converts. A chilled shot of Fernet is the 'bartender's handshake', the industry's insider greeting, especially in San Francisco. In Argentina, where Branca operates its only distillery outside Milan, fernet mixed with cola is effectively a national drink.
history: Created in Milan in 1845 by Bernardino Branca — family legend also credits an apothecary figure named 'Fernet' — the liqueur was first sold partly as a medicinal tonic, reputedly good for cholera and digestion alike, and even survived American Prohibition by being sold in pharmacies. Fratelli Branca Distillerie remains family-run five generations on, with Niccolò Branca leading today. Massive Italian emigration made Argentina the brand's second home: a Buenos Aires distillery opened in 1925, and Argentines now drink most of the world's fernet, overwhelmingly as Fernet con Coca.
production: Twenty-seven herbs, roots and spices sourced from four continents — the disclosed ones include myrrh, saffron, rhubarb, chamomile, gentian, bitter orange and aloe — are extracted separately by infusion and decoction in alcohol, then combined according to the secret formula; by tradition the company president personally supervises the dosing of the aromatic blend. The assembled liqueur rests for about a year in large oak vats at the Milan distillery, marrying and mellowing slightly, before being bottled at 39% ABV for the US market (strength varies slightly elsewhere).
barrel: Rested about one year in large oak vats

tastingNotes.nose: Menthol, medicinal herbs, saffron, licorice root and damp earth
tastingNotes.palate: Fiercely bitter and cooling — mint, myrrh, rhubarb root, clove and espresso-like depth with minimal sugar
tastingNotes.finish: Very long, numbing menthol and root bitterness

servingTemp: Chilled or room-temperature shots; in Argentina, tall over ice with cola
facts: A shot of Fernet is the 'bartender's handshake' — the service industry's insider greeting, with San Francisco its US capital.
Argentina consumes the large majority of the world's Fernet-Branca, mostly as Fernet con Coca; Branca has distilled in Buenos Aires since 1925.
It survived US Prohibition by being sold in pharmacies as a medicinal bitter.
Branca is one of the world's largest buyers of saffron, a key ingredient in the secret 27-herb formula.

timeline.event: Bernardino Branca creates Fernet-Branca in Milan
Branca opens its Buenos Aires distillery to serve Argentine demand

name: High Noon Sun Sips Pineapple
description: High Noon Sun Sips Pineapple is the flagship flavor family of the brand that redefined the American ready-to-drink category. Unlike the malt-based seltzers that ignited the 2010s boom, High Noon is built on actual distilled spirits: real vodka, sparkling water and real fruit juice, with no added sugar, landing at 4.5% ABV (9 proof) and about 100 calories per 355 ml (12 oz) can. The pineapple expression — juicy, tangy and only lightly sweet — is consistently among its best sellers. Launched by wine giant E. & J. Gallo in 2019, High Noon overtook the malt incumbents within a few years to become the top-selling hard seltzer and spirits-based RTD in the United States, a shift that pulled the whole category toward 'real vodka + real juice' positioning.
history: E. & J. Gallo, the Modesto, California wine company, created High Noon in 2019 as a spirits-based answer to the White Claw-era seltzer wave, launching with pineapple among its first four flavors. Distribution muscle, beach-and-sun branding and the 'real vodka, real juice' message drove explosive growth through the early 2020s; by 2023 retail trackers reported it had become the best-selling hard seltzer brand in the US, dethroning malt-based rivals, and it remains the benchmark spirits RTD.
production: The base is a five-times-distilled neutral vodka rather than the fermented malt or sugar wash behind most first-wave hard seltzers — a distinction that also puts High Noon in liquor-store spirits aisles under US law. The vodka is blended with carbonated water, real pineapple juice and natural flavors, with no sugar added, then canned at 4.5% ABV in 355 ml (12 oz) cans. The finished drink runs about 100 calories per can and is gluten-free by composition.

tastingNotes.nose: Fresh-cut pineapple and a clean soda-water sparkle
tastingNotes.palate: Light, fizzy and juicy — tangy pineapple with the faintest vodka warmth
tastingNotes.finish: Crisp and short, dry rather than sugary

servingTemp: Ice-cold from the can (around 4 °C), or over ice with fresh fruit
facts: Made with real vodka and real fruit juice — most first-wave hard seltzers were fermented malt or sugar bases.
Each 355 ml can is 4.5% ABV (9 proof) and about 100 calories with no added sugar.
By 2023, retail data showed High Noon had become the best-selling hard seltzer brand in the US.
It is produced by E. & J. Gallo, the family wine company better known for Barefoot and Apothic.

timeline.event: High Noon launches with pineapple among its first four flavors
Reported by retail trackers as the top-selling US hard seltzer brand

name: On The Rocks Old Fashioned
description: On The Rocks Old Fashioned is a bar-strength, bartender-formulated cocktail in a bottle: Knob Creek Kentucky Straight Bourbon stirred with sugar and aromatic bitters and sealed at a genuine 35% ABV (70 proof) — the same territory as a cocktail actually built in a glass, where most early RTDs were diluted compromises. The premise is simple honesty: pour it over a large cube, add an orange twist if you like, and you have a credible Old Fashioned with zero prep, which is why the squat square bottles became fixtures of airline carts, stadiums, hotel minibars and beach houses. It anchors an OTR lineup that pairs each classic cocktail with a name-brand base spirit, and it is a large part of why premium bottled cocktails became the fastest-growing corner of the RTD market.
history: On The Rocks was founded in Texas in 2015 by beverage-industry veterans — bartender Rocco Milano central among them — with the then-novel idea of premium, full-proof cocktails using recognizable spirit brands. Beam Suntory partnered with the company in 2019 and acquired it outright in 2020, matching OTR cocktails to its own portfolio: Knob Creek in the Old Fashioned, Hornitos in the Margarita, Cruzan in the Mai Tai. Under Suntory (renamed Suntory Global Spirits in 2024) the brand expanded nationally and into travel retail, where single-serve bottled cocktails found a natural home.
production: OTR batches the drink the way a bartender would build it, then bottles the result: Knob Creek Kentucky Straight Bourbon — itself aged in new charred American oak — is combined with cane sugar and aromatic bitters, then brought to cocktail proof with water at 35% ABV, mimicking the dilution that stirring over ice provides. The finished cocktail is bottled at 375 ml (with 100 ml minis for travel), is shelf-stable without refrigeration, and is designed to be poured straight over a large cube.
barrel: Made with Knob Creek bourbon aged in new charred American oak

tastingNotes.nose: Caramel, orange oil, vanilla and aromatic bitters spice
tastingNotes.palate: Sweet bourbon heart — toffee, oak, clove and bitter orange in balance
tastingNotes.finish: Warm and medium-long, bitters and oak trailing off cleanly

servingTemp: Poured over a large ice cube; a light chill on the bottle also works
facts: Bottled at a true cocktail strength of 35% ABV (70 proof) — most early RTDs were far weaker.
The bourbon inside is Knob Creek, matched to the brand after Beam Suntory acquired OTR in 2020.
OTR bottles became a travel staple, poured on airlines and sold throughout airport retail.
The brand was co-created by working bartenders in Texas in 2015, before 'premium RTD' was a category.

timeline.event: On The Rocks founded in Texas
Beam Suntory acquires OTR; cocktails matched to its spirit brands

name: Tanqueray London Dry Gin
description: Tanqueray London Dry is the archetype of the juniper-forward London Dry style and proof that minimalism can carry a global brand. Its recipe, essentially unchanged since Charles Tanqueray settled it in the 1830s, uses just four botanicals — juniper, coriander seed, angelica root and licorice — with no citrus peel at all; the lemony brightness comes from the coriander. Bottled at a bracing 47.3% ABV in the United States, it delivers piney, peppery clarity that cuts through tonic and vermouth alike, which is why bartenders treat it as a reference gin for martinis and gin and tonics. The green shaker-shaped bottle with its red pineapple seal — a Georgian symbol of hospitality — is among the most recognized silhouettes in spirits. 'London Dry' here denotes the production style; the gin has been distilled in Scotland since the late 1990s.
history: Charles Tanqueray, a clergyman's son who chose distilling over the church, founded his Bloomsbury distillery in 1830 at about age 20 and quickly won a reputation for consistency. The company merged with Gordon's in 1898. German bombing in the Blitz of 1941 destroyed the Vine Street distillery — famously sparing a single still — and production eventually consolidated in Scotland, moving to Diageo's Cameronbridge site in Fife in 1998. Tanqueray is today one of the world's best-selling premium gins, particularly dominant in the United States.
production: A neutral grain spirit is redistilled in copper pot stills with the four botanicals steeped directly in the spirit — the classic one-shot London Dry method, with nothing added after distillation except water. Juniper dominates the bill, angelica binds and dries, coriander seed supplies citrus-spice lift and a touch of licorice root rounds the base. Distillation now takes place at Cameronbridge in Fife, Scotland, to the historic Bloomsbury recipe.
coloring: None

tastingNotes.nose: Piercing pine-fresh juniper, coriander citrus-spice and a clean spirity lift.
tastingNotes.palate: Dry and assertive — juniper resin, white pepper, subtle licorice sweetness and earthy angelica.
tastingNotes.finish: Crisp and long, peppery juniper with a faintly sweet rooty echo.

servingTemp: Ice-cold in a martini, or over plenty of ice with tonic and a lime wedge
facts: The recipe uses only four botanicals and no citrus peel — the lemon-lime brightness comes entirely from coriander seed.
The red seal on the bottle depicts a pineapple, an 18th-century symbol of hospitality.
The Blitz destroyed Tanqueray's London distillery in 1941; production later moved to Scotland, where the gin has been made since 1998.

timeline.event: Charles Tanqueray founds his distillery on Vine Street, Bloomsbury, London
Tanqueray merges with Gordon's to form Tanqueray Gordon & Co
The London distillery is destroyed in the Blitz; one still survives
Production consolidates at Cameronbridge, Fife, Scotland

name: Hendrick's Gin
description: Hendrick's is the gin that cracked open the modern craft-gin era: a deliberately eccentric Scottish spirit that softened juniper's grip with infusions of cucumber and Bulgarian rose, packaged in a dark Victorian apothecary bottle when every competitor was clear and tall. Created in 1999 by master distiller Lesley Gracie for William Grant & Sons, it marries spirit from two very different antique stills — a small 1860s Bennett pot still worked hard for depth, and a 1948 Carter-Head whose vapor basket lends airy delicacy — before the signature essences are added. The result is unmistakable: floral, cool and faintly sweet, with juniper present but polite. Its runaway success, driven by the now-canonical cucumber-garnished gin and tonic, licensed a generation of distillers to experiment far beyond the London Dry template. Bottled at 44% ABV in the US (41.4% elsewhere).
history: William Grant & Sons, the family firm behind Glenfiddich, bought two curious old stills at auction in 1966; decades later chairman Charles Grant Gordon asked Lesley Gracie, a chemist at the company, to develop an unconventional gin with them. Launched in 1999 and aimed first at the American market, Hendrick's grew from oddity to phenomenon through the 2000s. The name is said to honor a gardener who worked for the Grant family. In 2018 the company opened the Hendrick's Gin Palace at Girvan, a purpose-built distillery, laboratory and walled-garden complex on the Ayrshire coast.
production: Two batches are distilled from the same eleven botanicals — juniper, coriander, angelica, orris root, cubeb berries, caraway, chamomile, elderflower, yarrow, lemon and orange peel. The Bennett pot still steeps and boils them for a rich, earthy spirit; the Carter-Head passes vapor through a botanical basket for a light floral one. The two are blended, then infused post-distillation with cucumber and Bulgarian rose essences — which is why Hendrick's is a 'distilled gin' rather than a London Dry.
coloring: None

tastingNotes.nose: Fresh cucumber and rose petal over soft juniper, chamomile and citrus peel.
tastingNotes.palate: Cool and rounded — floral rose, melon-like cucumber, gentle juniper, coriander spice and a whisper of caraway.
tastingNotes.finish: Clean and medium-length, florals fading into soft peppery herbs.

servingTemp: Over ice with tonic and a cucumber ribbon — never lime — or in a 50/50 martini
facts: The cucumber and rose are infused after distillation, technically making Hendrick's a 'distilled gin' rather than a London Dry.
Its two stills are genuine antiques: a Bennett pot still from the 1860s and a Carter-Head from 1948, bought at auction in 1966.
The brand is reportedly named after a gardener employed by the Grant family — fitting for a gin garnished from the vegetable patch.

timeline.event: Hendrick's launched by William Grant & Sons, created by Lesley Gracie
Hendrick's Gin Palace distillery complex opens at Girvan, Ayrshire

name: Monkey 47 Schwarzwald Dry Gin
description: Monkey 47 is the maximalist masterpiece of modern gin: 47 botanicals, 47% ABV, sold in a 500 ml brown apothecary bottle with a paper band and cork. Launched in 2010 from Germany's Black Forest, it stunned skeptics who assumed so many ingredients would blur into mud — instead the gin unfolds in layers, from sharp juniper and citrus through six varieties of pepper to its signature tart lingonberries and resinous spruce. Unusually, the base spirit is distilled from molasses, and the botanicals include local oddities like blackberry leaves and acacia flowers alongside classic juniper, coriander and angelica. Soft Black Forest spring water brings it to strength. Complex enough to sip, it also makes an extravagant gin and tonic and won the IWSC's worldwide gin trophy within a year of launch, propelling German gin onto the world stage.
history: Brand lore traces the recipe to Wing Commander Montgomery 'Monty' Collins, an RAF officer in post-war Berlin who sponsored a monkey named Max at the ruined zoo, later retired to the Black Forest, and left behind a gin recipe rediscovered decades on. The documented history: former Nokia executive Alexander Stein quit telecoms in 2008, partnered with celebrated distiller Christoph Keller, and launched Monkey 47 in 2010 from a converted mill in Loßburg. Pernod Ricard took a majority stake in 2016 as the gin became a global bar-shelf fixture.
production: A molasses-based neutral spirit — rare for gin — is macerated with the softer botanicals while tougher roots and berries are vapor-infused, then everything is distilled in small copper stills. The 47 botanicals include juniper, coriander, angelica, lingonberries, spruce shoots, blackberry leaves, acacia flowers, elderflower, lavender, honey pomelo and six kinds of pepper, roughly a third sourced from the Black Forest itself. The distillate rests about three months in earthenware vessels before dilution with local spring water.
coloring: None

tastingNotes.nose: Bright juniper and citrus zest over pine forest, lavender, berry tartness and peppery spice.
tastingNotes.palate: Dense and layered — resinous juniper, lingonberry, bitter herbs, six-pepper warmth and a faint sweetness from the molasses base.
tastingNotes.finish: Long and evolving, pepper and pine resolving into dried berries and herbs.

servingTemp: Neat from the freezer to study it, or with a light tonic and no garnish

awards.award: Gin Trophy — International Wine & Spirit Competition

facts: The name encodes the recipe: 47 botanicals, bottled at 47% ABV.
The base spirit is distilled from molasses rather than grain — almost unheard of in premium gin.
About one third of the botanicals, including lingonberries and spruce shoots, are sourced in the Black Forest around the distillery.

timeline.event: Monkey 47 launched by Alexander Stein and distiller Christoph Keller in Loßburg
Wins the IWSC Gin Trophy, vaulting German gin to international attention
Pernod Ricard acquires a majority stake in Black Forest Distillers

name: The Botanist Islay Dry Gin
description: The Botanist is the gin of Islay — made by the Bruichladdich whisky distillery from 22 botanicals hand-foraged on the island, layered over nine classic gin botanicals. Where its Hebridean home is famous for peat smoke, this spirit captures the island's other face: machair meadows, bog and hedgerow, expressed through apple mint, meadowsweet, gorse flowers, heather, bog myrtle and wild thyme. It is distilled achingly slowly — roughly 17 hours a run — in 'Ugly Betty', a squat Lomond still rescued from the demolished Inverleven distillery, at an unhurried simmer that preserves delicate aromatics. The result is a soft, herbaceous, gently floral gin with real mid-palate texture at 46% ABV, equally suited to a meditative martini or an herb-garnished gin and tonic. The embossed bottle lists all 22 foraged botanicals in Latin.
history: When Mark Reynier's team resurrected Bruichladdich in 2001, the distillery embraced experimentation; the gin project arrived at the end of the decade as a way to use spare still capacity and celebrate Islay's flora. Botanical scientists Richard and Mavis Gulliver identified the island plants that could be sustainably picked, and veteran distiller Jim McEwan shaped the spirit, first released in 2011. Professional foragers now gather the harvest each season. Rémy Cointreau's 2012 purchase of Bruichladdich carried The Botanist with it, funding global distribution.
production: Nine core botanicals — juniper, coriander seed, angelica, cassia, cinnamon, licorice, orris root, lemon and orange peel — are macerated overnight in grain spirit in the Lomond still nicknamed Ugly Betty. The 22 foraged Islay botanicals go into a vapor basket, and distillation proceeds at a bare simmer for around 17 hours, among the slowest in gin. One-shot spirit is cut with Islay water to 46% ABV; nothing is added after distillation.
coloring: None

tastingNotes.nose: Meadow herbs, mint and citrus blossom over restrained juniper, with honeyed gorse and coastal freshness.
tastingNotes.palate: Silky and herbaceous — sweet cicely, thyme, elderflower and lemon balm around a firm juniper-coriander core.
tastingNotes.finish: Long and gently drying, wildflowers and soft spice with a saline trace.

servingTemp: With tonic and a sprig of mint or thyme, or in a dry martini
facts: All 22 foraged botanicals grow wild on Islay and are hand-picked in season; their Latin names are embossed on the bottle.
It is distilled in 'Ugly Betty', a Lomond still salvaged from the Inverleven distillery and barged to Islay.
Each run takes about 17 hours at a bare simmer — one of the slowest distillations of any gin.

timeline.event: The Botanist launches as Islay's first dry gin
Rémy Cointreau acquires Bruichladdich Distillery, including The Botanist

name: Plymouth Gin
description: Plymouth Gin is both a brand and a style: a slightly earthier, softer, subtly sweeter counterpoint to London Dry, made continuously at the Black Friars Distillery on Plymouth's Barbican since 1793 — England's oldest working gin distillery. Seven botanicals, heavy on root ingredients like angelica and orris, give it a rounded, less pine-dominant profile that made it the gin most often specified by name in early cocktail books, including the first printed dry martini recipes. Its Royal Navy history runs deep: the fleet carried Plymouth around the world, and the distillery still makes a 57% Navy Strength. The style enjoyed its own EU geographical indication until the producers let it lapse in 2015, leaving the heritage — a single distillery, a single style, one city — intact in practice if no longer in law. Bottled at 41.2% ABV.
history: The distillery occupies a former Dominican friary — hence Black Friars — with parts of the building dating to the early 15th century; Pilgrim Fathers are said to have lodged there in 1620 before sailing on the Mayflower. Distilling under Coates & Co began in 1793, and by the late 19th century Plymouth was among the world's biggest gin names, a thousand cases a week crossing the Atlantic. After decline and a 1990s management buyout revival, the brand passed via V&S to Pernod Ricard in 2008.
production: A one-shot batch distillation in a Victorian copper pot still redistills wheat-based neutral spirit with seven botanicals: juniper, coriander seed, sweet orange peel, lemon peel, green cardamom, angelica root and orris root. The higher proportion of roots relative to juniper yields Plymouth's trademark earthy softness, and Dartmoor water brings it to 41.2%. Nothing is added post-distillation, but the style is legally distinct from London Dry by tradition and former GI.
coloring: None

tastingNotes.nose: Soft juniper, fresh orange and lemon peel, cardamom and a damp, rooty earthiness.
tastingNotes.palate: Smooth and full for the strength — earthy angelica, gentle juniper, citrus sweetness and warm spice.
tastingNotes.finish: Medium, softly spiced, with orange and orris lingering rather than pine.

servingTemp: In a stirred, ice-cold martini or a classic gin and tonic with orange peel
facts: The Black Friars building dates to the early 1400s, and the Pilgrim Fathers reputedly spent their last night in England there before the 1620 Mayflower voyage.
Plymouth Gin held its own EU protected geographical indication until the producers chose not to renew it in 2015.
Early cocktail bibles, including some of the first dry martini recipes in print, called for Plymouth by name.

timeline.event: Coates & Co begins distilling gin at the Black Friars Distillery, Plymouth
Brand joins Pernod Ricard through the acquisition of V&S Group
EU geographical indication for Plymouth Gin allowed to lapse

name: Grey Goose Vodka
description: Grey Goose essentially invented the super-premium vodka category. Conceived in 1997 by American beverage magnate Sidney Frank and built around French provenance, it is distilled from soft winter wheat grown in Picardy — the same bread-grade 'blé panifiable' used by bakers — and blended with spring water from Gensac-la-Pallue in the Cognac region. Recipe creator François Thibault, a trained cognac cellar master, kept the process deliberately restrained: a single continuous distillation to spirit, minimal filtration, and no additives, aiming to preserve wheat character rather than strip it out. The result is a gently sweet, faintly almond-and-citrus vodka with a soft, creamy texture that made it the default call in martinis through the 2000s. The frosted bottle with flying geese remains a status shorthand, and Bacardi's 2004 purchase of the brand was one of the largest single-brand deals in spirits history.
history: Sidney Frank, the importer who had turned Jägermeister into an American phenomenon, set out in 1996–97 to create a vodka he could sell above Absolut's price point, betting that French luxury cachet would justify it. He hired cognac maître de chai François Thibault to develop the liquid, launched in 1997 at around $30, and marketed it relentlessly on taste-test wins. Seven years later Bacardi bought Grey Goose for approximately $2.2 billion, at the time among the biggest prices ever paid for a single spirits brand.
production: Soft winter wheat from Picardy is milled and fermented, then distilled in a continuous five-column process in the same region — Grey Goose describes it as distilled once, to a precise spirit cut, rather than repeatedly redistilled. The high-proof spirit travels to Gensac-la-Pallue near Cognac, where it is blended to 40% ABV with limestone-filtered spring water, lightly filtered and bottled. No sugar, glycerin or flavoring is added.
coloring: None

tastingNotes.nose: Clean and softly grainy — fresh bread, faint almond and a citrus lift.
tastingNotes.palate: Smooth and gently sweet, creamy wheat texture with hints of vanilla and white pepper.
tastingNotes.finish: Short and clean, a soft almond warmth with no burn.

servingTemp: Freezer-chilled (−15 °C) neat, or stirred ice-cold in a martini
facts: Sidney Frank created the brand in 1997 and sold it to Bacardi in 2004 for about $2.2 billion — a landmark price for a single spirits brand.
The wheat is bread-grade soft winter wheat from Picardy, France's breadbasket.
Creator François Thibault trained as a cognac cellar master, not a vodka distiller, and designed the process to keep wheat flavor in rather than strip it out.

timeline.event: Grey Goose launched by Sidney Frank Importing Co.
Bacardi acquires Grey Goose for approximately $2.2 billion

name: Tito's Handmade Vodka
description: Tito's Handmade Vodka is the great American spirits success story of the 21st century: a 100% corn vodka from Austin, Texas that grew from a one-man shack operation into the best-selling spirit brand in the United States by revenue. Founder Bert 'Tito' Beveridge — a geologist-turned-mortgage-broker — obtained the first legal distillery permit in Texas and sold his first bottles in 1997, hand-labeling them himself. The corn base makes it naturally gluten-free and lends a subtle sweetness; the house style is soft, faintly peppery and unfussy, priced for mixing rather than posturing. A Double Gold at the 2001 San Francisco World Spirits Competition supercharged its word-of-mouth rise. The 'Handmade' claim drew class-action lawsuits as production scaled to millions of cases; courts dismissed them, and the plain screw-cap bottle has never changed. It remains the default well upgrade across American bars.
history: Beveridge began infusing and distilling as a hobby in the early 1990s, then mortgaged himself to build a pot-still operation on a plot southeast of Austin, receiving Texas's first legal distillery permit and selling his first cases in 1997. Growth was slow until the 2001 San Francisco Double Gold and word of mouth among bartenders. Through the 2010s Tito's exploded, overtaking Smirnoff to become America's top-selling spirit while remaining wholly owned by Beveridge's Fifth Generation, Inc.
production: Tito's is distilled from 100% yellow corn. The company describes an 'old-fashioned pot still' process based on batch distillation — at least six passes in early accounts — now operating at enormous scale across dozens of stills at the Austin campus, supplemented by column-produced spirit feeding the pot stills. The vodka is charcoal-filtered, blended with purified water to 80 proof, and bottled without added sugar or glycerin (per the company).
coloring: None

tastingNotes.nose: Neutral with a light sweet-corn roundness and faint citrus.
tastingNotes.palate: Soft, slightly sweet entry, gentle cracked-pepper warmth, very easy-going.
tastingNotes.finish: Short and warm with a mild peppery tingle.

servingTemp: Ice-cold from the freezer, or in any mixed drink

awards.award: Double Gold — San Francisco World Spirits Competition

facts: Bert 'Tito' Beveridge held the first legal distillery permit in Texas and sold his first bottles in 1997.
Its 2001 San Francisco Double Gold — beating a field of far pricier vodkas — is credited with launching its rise.
Corn-based and naturally gluten-free, Tito's became the best-selling spirit brand in the US by revenue in the early 2020s while still family-owned.

timeline.event: Fifth Generation, Inc. founded; distillery built southeast of Austin
First bottles of Tito's Handmade Vodka sold
Double Gold at the San Francisco World Spirits Competition

name: Reyka Vodka
description: Reyka was Iceland's first purpose-built vodka distillery and remains the definitive expression of the country's elemental branding made real: the stills run on volcanic geothermal steam, the water is drawn from a spring running through a 4,000-year-old lava field, and the spirit passes through beds of Icelandic lava rock. Launched in 2005 by William Grant & Sons in the coastal village of Borgarnes, it is made in genuinely small batches on a rare Carter-Head still — a design better known from gin — whose copper basket and rectification produce an unusually clean spirit without aggressive charcoal stripping. The character is crisp and glacial but not empty: faint vanilla, citrus and a stony mineral freshness distinguish it from big-column commodity vodkas. The name derives from the Old Norse root for smoke or steam, the same word that gave Reykjavík — 'smoky bay' — its name.
history: William Grant & Sons, the Scottish family firm behind Glenfiddich and Hendrick's, chose Iceland for a vodka project in the early 2000s, drawn by cheap renewable geothermal energy and pristine water. The Borgarnes distillery, about an hour north of Reykjavík, was commissioned and Reyka launched in 2005 as Iceland's first dedicated vodka distillery, exporting a national identity that previously had no international vodka presence. It has since become Iceland's flagship white spirit abroad.
production: Wheat-and-barley neutral spirit is redistilled in Borgarnes on a copper Carter-Head still — one of only a handful in the world — in small batches of roughly 255 cases per run. Volcanic geothermal energy heats the process, and the spirit is filtered through locally quarried lava rock. Water from the Grábrók spring, naturally filtered through a lava field, brings it to 40% ABV; no additives are used.
coloring: None

tastingNotes.nose: Glacially clean — wet stone, faint vanilla and a breath of citrus.
tastingNotes.palate: Crisp and lightly creamy, subtle grain sweetness with a cool mineral edge.
tastingNotes.finish: Short, fresh and stony with a whisper of white pepper.

servingTemp: Freezer-cold neat, or in a crisp martini or highball
facts: The distillery runs on geothermal steam, making Reyka one of the world's first renewable-energy spirits operations.
The vodka is filtered through lava rock and cut with spring water that runs beneath a 4,000-year-old lava field.
Its Carter-Head still is a design far more common in gin — Hendrick's, its William Grant sibling, uses one too.

timeline.event: Purpose-built geothermal distillery commissioned in Borgarnes
Reyka launches as Iceland's first dedicated vodka distillery

name: Brennivín
description: Brennivín — 'burning wine' — is Iceland's national spirit: a clear, unaged aquavit flavored almost entirely with caraway, drunk ice-cold in shots at family gatherings and midwinter Þorrablót feasts, where it traditionally chases hákarl, the island's notorious fermented shark. Born in 1935 on the day Iceland's prohibition partially ended, it was sold by the state monopoly under a deliberately austere black label meant to discourage purchase; the design backfired into an icon, and the grim look earned the nickname Svarti dauði — 'Black Death'. The flavor is cleaner and drier than Scandinavian aquavits: a rush of toasted caraway and anise-adjacent herbaceousness over a smooth neutral base, with no cask aging and no sweetness. Long a souvenir bottle, it gained international distribution in the 2010s and a cult following among bartenders as a rye-adjacent botanical spirit. It remains produced by Ölgerðin in Reykjavík.
history: Iceland enacted full prohibition in 1915; when spirits became legal again on 1 February 1935, the state alcohol monopoly needed a domestic schnapps and Brennivín was the answer, its plain black label designed to keep temperance sentiment at bay. (Beer, notoriously, stayed banned until 1989.) Production passed to Ölgerðin Egill Skallagrímsson, Reykjavík's historic brewery founded in 1913. Anthony Bourdain's Iceland episodes and official US importation from 2014 turned a national curiosity into a modest export with bartender cachet.
production: Brennivín is an aquavit in the Nordic tradition: a clean neutral spirit — historically potato-based, described in modern accounts as fermented grain or potato mash — is redistilled or infused with caraway seed, the dominant botanical, and brought to 37.5% ABV with Icelandic water. There is no barrel aging, no added sugar and no coloring, giving a drier, sharper profile than Danish or Norwegian cask-aged aquavits. It is bottled in Reykjavík by Ölgerðin.
coloring: None

tastingNotes.nose: Toasted caraway and rye-bread spice over a clean neutral spirit, hints of fennel and citrus pith.
tastingNotes.palate: Dry and direct — caraway, anise-like herbs, cracked pepper and a smooth grain-sweet core.
tastingNotes.finish: Medium, warming caraway that lingers like dark bread crust.

servingTemp: Ice-cold from the freezer, traditionally as a shot
facts: Launched the day Iceland's 20-year prohibition on spirits ended in 1935; beer remained illegal for another 54 years, until 1989.
The stark black label was designed by the state monopoly to make the bottle unappealing — instead it created the 'Black Death' (Svarti dauði) legend.
It is the traditional chaser for hákarl, fermented Greenland shark, during the midwinter Þorrablót festival.

timeline.event: Iceland enacts nationwide prohibition
Brennivín launched as spirits prohibition ends on 1 February
Official importation to the United States begins

name: Fortaleza Blanco
description: Fortaleza Blanco is the modern touchstone for traditionally made tequila and the bottle enthusiasts reach for to demonstrate what the category tasted like before industrialization. Made in the town of Tequila by Guillermo Erickson Sauza — fifth generation of the family that first exported tequila to the United States — it is produced with methods most large distilleries abandoned decades ago: stone ovens, a volcanic tahona wheel, open wood-tank fermentation and tiny copper pot stills. Unaged and undiluted by shortcuts, it presents cooked agave in high definition: bright citrus, olive brine, black pepper, wet stone and a buttery, almost savory depth. Every bottle is hand-blown glass topped with a little glass agave piña. Since the tequila boom of the late 2010s it has been chronically allocated in the US, routinely selling above its suggested price — a scarcity born of the distillery's refusal to scale up its methods.
history: Don Cenobio Sauza founded the family's tequila dynasty in 1873 and is credited as the first to export tequila to the US. The family sold the Sauza brand in 1976, and the small La Fortaleza distillery beside grandfather Javier's hilltop home was preserved as a museum. In the late 1990s Guillermo Erickson Sauza resolved to make tequila the old way on the old site; after restoration, the first Fortaleza (sold as Los Abuelos in Mexico) was bottled in 2005 under NOM 1493.
production: Estate and valley-grown Blue Weber agave is slow-cooked for about 33 hours in stone-and-brick ovens, then crushed by a two-ton volcanic tahona wheel — no roller mill, no diffuser. Juice and fibers ferment together with native airborne yeast in open wooden tanks, and distillation is double, in small copper pot stills. The blanco is rested only briefly in stainless steel before bottling at 40%, unfiltered of character and without additives.
coloring: None (additive-free tequila)

tastingNotes.nose: Roasted agave, lime zest, green olive, wet river stone and a buttery, bready richness.
tastingNotes.palate: Full and oily — cooked agave sweetness, black pepper, citrus, earth and a saline, almost briny savor.
tastingNotes.finish: Long and peppery, minerals and sweet agave trading the last word.

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature in a copita; too good to shoot
facts: Guillermo Erickson Sauza is the fifth generation of the family whose patriarch, Don Cenobio Sauza, first exported tequila to the United States in the 1870s.
The agave is still crushed by a two-ton volcanic-stone tahona wheel, a method most distilleries retired half a century ago.
Each stopper is a hand-blown glass agave piña, and the distillery's hillside cellar is dug into the rock beneath the old family estate.

timeline.event: Don Cenobio Sauza founds the family's first distillery in Tequila
The family sells the Sauza brand; the old La Fortaleza distillery becomes a museum
First bottling of Fortaleza from the restored distillery, NOM 1493

name: El Tesoro Reposado
style: Tequila Reposado, 100% Blue Agave (Los Altos highlands)
description: El Tesoro Reposado is a masterclass in keeping agave first even after oak enters the picture. Made at La Alteña, the Camarena family's 1937 distillery in the Los Altos highlands town of Arandas, it rests nine to eleven months in ex-bourbon barrels — long for the reposado class — yet tastes vividly of cooked highland agave: sweet, fruity and floral, seasoned rather than smothered by vanilla and baking spice. The house's defining technique is distillation to proof: no water is added after the second distillation, so everything in the bottle came off the still at bottling strength, preserving texture and intensity. Brick ovens, a tahona, wood-tank fermentation with fibers and small copper pot stills complete one of tequila's most traditional processes. Master distiller Carlos Camarena is among the category's most respected figures, and this bottling is a fixture in arguments about the best reposado on the shelf.
history: Don Felipe Camarena Hernández built La Alteña in 1937, reviving a family agave tradition disrupted by the Mexican Revolution. His son Don Felipe J. Camarena created the El Tesoro de Don Felipe brand in 1988 with American importer Robert Denton, and grandson Carlos Camarena now leads production. The brand's US rights eventually passed to Beam, today Suntory Global Spirits, while the distillery itself remains family-run in Arandas, also producing Tapatío and collaborations like Tequila Ocho.
production: Fully mature highland agave is baked about two days in brick ovens, crushed by tahona, and fermented in open wooden vats together with the agave fibers, which continue into the copper pot stills for the first distillation. The second distillation runs to bottling proof — no dilution afterward. The reposado then rests 9–11 months in ex-bourbon American oak from sister distilleries, emerging pale gold and additive-free.
barrel: Ex-bourbon American oak barrels, 9–11 months
coloring: None (additive-free tequila)

tastingNotes.nose: Cooked agave, orchard fruit and honeysuckle with soft vanilla, cinnamon and fresh oak.
tastingNotes.palate: Silky, agave-forward sweetness — pear, citrus, white pepper, light caramel and baking spice in balance.
tastingNotes.finish: Long and gently spicy, agave outlasting the oak.

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature, or in a top-shelf margarita
facts: El Tesoro is distilled to proof — no water is added after the still — a rarity that preserves texture and flavor intensity.
The agave fibers stay in the fermentation and first distillation, an old-fashioned practice that deepens the agave character.
Master distiller Carlos Camarena also makes Tapatío and co-created Tequila Ocho, making La Alteña a pilgrimage site for agave purists.

timeline.event: Don Felipe Camarena builds La Alteña distillery in Arandas
El Tesoro de Don Felipe brand launched for export

name: Don Julio 1942
description: Don Julio 1942 is the definitive luxury tequila of the modern era — the tall, agave-leaf-inspired bottle held aloft in nightclubs and name-checked in lyrics, and the pour that convinced a mass audience that tequila could sell at cognac prices. Created in 2002 to mark the 60th anniversary of founder Don Julio González's first distillery, it is an añejo aged roughly two and a half years in American oak, engineered for opulence: warm vanilla, caramel, roasted agave and tropical notes in a plush, dessert-leaning frame that flavor-critical drinkers sometimes find closer to confection than to field-and-oven tequila. Its cultural gravity is undeniable — under Diageo's ownership it anchored the celebrity-fueled premiumization wave that reshaped the category through the 2010s and 2020s. Traditionalists reach for tahona-made bottlings; 1942 remains the category's most recognizable status symbol.
history: Don Julio González-Frausto Estrada began distilling in 1942, reputedly at age 17, near Atotonilco el Alto in the Jalisco highlands, and built La Primavera distillery around meticulous agave farming. The Don Julio brand — named for him in 1987 — pioneered premium 100% agave tequila. The 1942 expression launched in 2002 to honor the founding year; Diageo took full global control of the brand in a 2014–15 deal that traded away Bushmills, and 1942 became its flagship icon.
production: Fully ripened highland Blue Weber agave is slow-cooked in traditional masonry ovens for about three days at La Primavera (NOM 1449) in Atotonilco el Alto, then roller-milled, fermented with the house yeast strain, and double-distilled in stainless-and-copper pot stills. The añejo matures around 30 months in lightly charred American white oak ex-bourbon barrels before blending and bottling at 40% ABV (38% in Mexico).
barrel: American white oak ex-bourbon barrels, about 30 months

tastingNotes.nose: Warm vanilla, caramel, roasted agave, coconut and chocolate with a floral-tropical lift.
tastingNotes.palate: Plush and sweet — crème caramel, toasted oak, baked agave, cinnamon and candied orange.
tastingNotes.finish: Long, dessert-like vanilla and toffee with soft oak spice.

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature; often served chilled in luxury settings
facts: Launched in 2002 for the 60th anniversary of the year Don Julio González began distilling — reputedly at just 17 years old.
The elongated bottle is styled after an agave leaf, breaking with the squat decanters of its era.
Diageo acquired full control of Don Julio in a deal that sent Bushmills Irish whiskey to Jose Cuervo's owners in exchange.

timeline.event: Don Julio González begins distilling tequila in the Jalisco highlands
Don Julio 1942 released for the 60th anniversary
Diageo completes full acquisition of the Don Julio brand

name: Del Maguey Vida
style: Mezcal Artesanal Joven, Espadín (San Luis del Río)
description: Del Maguey Vida is the bottle that carried artisanal mezcal into the world's cocktail bars. Introduced in 2010 as the accessible, mixing-strength member of Del Maguey's Single Village range, it is made in San Luis del Río, a Zapotec village on the Rio Hormiga Colorada deep in Oaxaca, using the full traditional playbook: espadín agave roasted in an earthen pit over hot river stones, milled by a horse-drawn tahona, fermented wild in open wooden vats and twice distilled in small copper stills. The smoke is present but conversational — roasted agave, banana, honey and lime rather than an ashtray — and at 42% it anchors mezcal margaritas, palomas and negronis without overwhelming them. For countless drinkers Vida was the first sip of real palenque mezcal, and it remains the category's standard entry point and a bartender's well staple worldwide.
history: American artist Ron Cooper began traveling to Oaxaca in the 1960s and founded Del Maguey in 1995 to bottle unblended village mezcals — then almost unobtainable outside Mexico — naming each for its pueblo and paying producer families directly. The green-label Single Village bottlings earned cult status through the 2000s; Vida was created in 2010 with the palenqueros of San Luis del Río as a cocktail-friendly expression. Pernod Ricard acquired a majority stake in 2017, keeping Cooper and the village relationships in place.
production: Espadín agave (Agave angustifolia) is harvested at maturity, and the piñas roast for days in a wood-fired earthen pit lined with river stones — the source of mezcal's smoke. After resting, they are crushed by a horse-drawn tahona, fermented with wild yeasts in open wooden tanks, and double-distilled in small wood-fired copper pot stills at the village palenque. Vida is bottled young and unaged at 42% ABV; nothing is added.
coloring: None

tastingNotes.nose: Soft woodsmoke over roasted agave, ripe banana, honey and lime peel.
tastingNotes.palate: Round and fruity — baked sweet potato, tropical fruit, ginger and earthy smoke in balance.
tastingNotes.finish: Medium-long, warm smoke, citrus and a mineral, savory fade.

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature in a copita or veladora, or in smoky cocktails
facts: Del Maguey pioneered the 'single village' concept in 1995, naming each mezcal for its pueblo and its producing family.
Vida is made in San Luis del Río, a Zapotec village reached by hours of mountain road, on the banks of the Rio Hormiga Colorada.
Founder Ron Cooper is a working visual artist; the brand's folk-art labels come from his collaborator Ken Price and Oaxacan artists.

timeline.event: Ron Cooper founds Del Maguey Single Village Mezcal
Vida launched as the range's cocktail-strength expression
Pernod Ricard acquires a majority stake in Del Maguey

name: Ilegal Mezcal Joven
description: Ilegal Mezcal Joven is the unaged flagship of the brand whose origin story is its name: before it was a company, it was mezcal smuggled out of Oaxaca in suitcases and truck beds to supply Café No Sé, John Rexer's bar in Antigua, Guatemala. Formalized in 2009, Ilegal sources espadín mezcal from family palenques around Santiago Matatlán — the self-styled world capital of mezcal in the Tlacolula Valley — made with pit-roasting, small-batch fermentation and double distillation in copper. The joven is deliberately approachable: gentler smoke than many artisanal bottlings, with green apple, citrus, agave sweetness and a peppery, mineral edge, built to convert tequila drinkers and to carry a margarita. The brand's punk-ish, protest-poster marketing made it a bar-world favorite, and Bacardí, an early investor, acquired it outright in 2023.
history: John Rexer opened Café No Sé in Antigua, Guatemala in 2003 and, unable to buy good mezcal legally across the border, spent years running unbranded jugs out of Oaxaca — trips of bribes, buses and confiscations that supplied the bar's mezcal room and christened the eventual brand. Ilegal launched officially in 2009 with production in Santiago Matatlán. It grew through bartender advocacy and irreverent guerrilla marketing, took Bacardí investment in 2017, and was fully acquired by Bacardí in 2023.
production: Espadín agave from the Tlacolula Valley is roasted about three days in earthen pit ovens over river stone and oak, crushed (tahona and small mechanical mills are both used at partner palenques), and fermented with wild yeasts in open wooden vats. Double distillation in small copper pot stills follows, and the joven is rested briefly and bottled unaged at 40% ABV without additives. Production remains small-batch under maestros mezcaleros in Santiago Matatlán.
coloring: None

tastingNotes.nose: Green apple, lime and fresh agave with restrained campfire smoke and wet stone.
tastingNotes.palate: Crisp and lightly sweet — pear, citrus, white pepper and grassy agave over soft earth and smoke.
tastingNotes.finish: Clean and medium, peppery minerality with a smoky echo.

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature, or in a mezcal margarita or paloma
facts: The brand grew out of years of literal smuggling runs to stock Café No Sé, John Rexer's mezcal bar in Antigua, Guatemala — hence 'Ilegal'.
Production centers on Santiago Matatlán, the Oaxacan town that calls itself the world capital of mezcal.
Bacardí, an investor since 2017, acquired Ilegal outright in 2023 as mezcal became the industry's hottest small category.

timeline.event: Café No Sé opens in Antigua, Guatemala — the bar the smuggled mezcal supplied
Ilegal Mezcal launches as a legal brand with production in Santiago Matatlán
Bacardí completes full acquisition of Ilegal Mezcal

name: Appleton Estate 12 Year Rare Casks
description: Appleton Estate 12 Year Rare Casks is the benchmark aged expression from Jamaica's Nassau Valley and one of the most widely respected estate rums in the world. It blends copper pot still and column still rums, every component aged at least 12 years in the Jamaican climate, where evaporation is far faster than in Europe. The result balances Jamaica's signature fruity intensity with polish: dried orange peel, toasted hazelnut, cocoa and soft molasses over a distinctly dry, oaky frame. Unlike many aged sipping rums it carries no added sugar, and Jamaican law requires the age statement to reflect the youngest rum in the blend. Formerly labeled 'Extra' and then 'Rare Blend', it took the Rare Casks name in the brand's 2020 redesign. It is a staple recommendation for whiskey drinkers crossing over into rum.
history: The Appleton Estate in the Nassau Valley has grown cane since 1749, making it Jamaica's oldest sugar estate in continuous production, with rum production documented from the estate's early years. Owned by J. Wray & Nephew since 1916, the brand became Jamaica's flagship export rum through the 20th century. In 1997 Joy Spence was appointed master blender — the first woman to hold that title in the spirits industry — and she reshaped the modern range, including this 12 year blend. Campari Group acquired the company in 2012.
production: Estate-grown sugar cane is milled on site and the molasses fermented with a proprietary cultured yeast in a mix of open fermenters. Distillation runs on small copper pot stills — the source of the rum's fruity, funky top notes — and a column still for lighter spirit. The rums age separately in ex-bourbon American oak in the tropical heat of the Nassau Valley, losing several percent to evaporation each year, before blending with limestone-filtered estate spring water. No sugar or flavoring is added.
barrel: American white oak ex-bourbon barrels, minimum 12 years tropical aging

tastingNotes.nose: Dried orange peel, toasted hazelnut, cocoa, molasses and a whiff of Jamaican overripe banana funk.
tastingNotes.palate: Dry and structured for a rum — bitter orange, coffee, brown spice, oak and nutmeg over gentle molasses sweetness.
tastingNotes.finish: Long and warming, drying into oak, dark chocolate and orange pith.

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature (18–22 °C), or over a single large cube
facts: Joy Spence became the spirits industry's first female master blender when Appleton appointed her in 1997.
Jamaican regulations require the age statement to reflect the youngest rum in the blend — the opposite of solera-style 'up to' labeling.
The estate draws its blending water from a limestone-filtered spring in the Nassau Valley, in the foothills of Cockpit Country.

timeline.event: Appleton Estate established in the Nassau Valley, Saint Elizabeth
Joy Spence appointed master blender, a first for a woman in the spirits industry
Campari Group acquires parent company J. Wray & Nephew
12 Year expression renamed 'Rare Casks' in a global range redesign

name: Hampden Estate 8 Year
description: Hampden Estate 8 Year is the flagship of Jamaica's most extreme traditional distillery and the reference bottling for 'funk' — the wildly aromatic, high-ester style that once existed mainly in blenders' warehouses. Distilled entirely on pot stills after long wild fermentations, it erupts with overripe banana, rotting pineapple, varnish, olive brine and black tea, all amplified rather than tamed by eight years of Trelawny tropical aging. At 46% ABV with no added sugar and no dilution of character, it drinks closer to an intense single malt than to a smooth sipping rum. For rum enthusiasts it is a category landmark: the moment a working 18th-century estate began bottling its own name, and one of the most distinctive flavor profiles in all of spirits. Newcomers should approach it as a statement piece, not a starter rum.
history: Hampden Estate in Trelawny parish dates to 1753 and spent over two and a half centuries selling almost its entire output in bulk to European blenders, prized for ester-heavy marks used to stretch and perfume lighter rums. The Hussey family's Everglades Farms bought and revived the near-derelict estate in 2009. In 2018, in partnership with Italian bottler Velier, Hampden launched its first estate-branded rums — this 8 Year among them — turning a blending-house secret into a cult brand almost overnight.
production: Hampden works much as it did in the 1700s: molasses is fermented for one to several weeks with wild yeast, boosted by acidic dunder (stillage) and 'muck' cultivated in open pits, which drives ester levels far beyond ordinary rum. Distillation is exclusively on copper pot stills, and each batch is classified into historic ester marks. The 8 Year ages entirely at the estate in ex-bourbon casks, where tropical losses concentrate the spirit. Nothing is added.
barrel: Ex-bourbon American oak casks, 8 years of tropical aging at the estate

tastingNotes.nose: Explosive: overripe banana, pineapple in syrup, nail varnish, olive brine, mango chutney and damp earth.
tastingNotes.palate: Oily and intense — tropical fruit compote, black tea, salted caramel, cocoa nib and that unmistakable fermented funk.
tastingNotes.finish: Very long, fruit and earth trading places, ending on spiced oak and brine.

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature; a few drops of water open the esters further
facts: Fermentation at Hampden can run for weeks and uses wild yeast plus 'muck' — a cultivated bacterial sludge from dunder pits — to push ester levels sky-high.
For more than 250 years virtually all Hampden rum left Jamaica in bulk for European blending houses; the first estate-labeled bottlings only appeared in 2018.
Hampden's heaviest historic mark, DOK, sits near Jamaica's legal ceiling of 1,600 g/hLAA of esters — this 8 Year blends lighter, more approachable marks.

timeline.event: Hampden Estate established in Trelawny parish
Everglades Farms (Hussey family) acquires and revives the estate
First estate-branded rums, including the 8 Year, launched worldwide with Velier

name: El Dorado 12 Year
description: El Dorado 12 Year is the most accessible showcase of Guyana's Demerara rum tradition and of the antique wooden stills that survive nowhere else on earth. Blended at Diamond Distillery from marks that include spirit off the Enmore wooden Coffey still and the Port Mourant double wooden pot still, it layers burnt sugar, raisin, coffee and dark tropical fruit into a plush, dessert-like sipper. It is unambiguously a sweet-styled rum: independent hydrometer tests have repeatedly indicated post-distillation sugar in the tens of grams per litre, which the distillery does not disclose — a point of ongoing debate among enthusiasts even as the underlying distillate quality is widely admired. As an affordable introduction to the deep, smoky-sweet Demerara profile, it remains a modern classic and a fixture on back bars worldwide.
history: Sugar estates along Guyana's Demerara River once numbered in the hundreds, each with its own still and mark. As the industry consolidated, surviving stills were moved from estate to estate until everything gathered at Diamond, on the east bank of the river. Demerara Distillers Ltd (DDL) was formed in 1983 from this consolidation and launched the El Dorado premium range in 1992, transforming a bulk-rum supplier into a global brand and putting names like Port Mourant and Enmore on bottles rather than blending manifests.
production: Molasses from Demerara sugar is fermented and distilled across Diamond's unique still park: the Enmore wooden continuous Coffey still, the Versailles single wooden pot still, the Port Mourant double wooden pot still (its design dating to 1732) and modern metal columns. The greenheart-wood stills give characteristically heavy, smoky-fruity spirit. Marks age separately in ex-bourbon casks in the tropics for at least 12 years before blending; the final rum is noticeably sweetened, though DDL publishes no figure.
barrel: Ex-bourbon American oak casks, minimum 12 years tropical aging

tastingNotes.nose: Burnt sugar, raisin, dark chocolate, espresso, dried banana and a wisp of woodsmoke from the wooden stills.
tastingNotes.palate: Plush and sweet — toffee, prune, molasses, licorice and mocha with a smoky Demerara undertow.
tastingNotes.finish: Medium-long, sweet caramel fading into coffee grounds and charred oak.

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature, or over a large cube as an after-dinner pour
facts: Diamond Distillery operates the world's last working wooden stills, including the Port Mourant double wooden pot still, a design dating to 1732.
DDL is the last remaining rum distillery in Guyana, heir to the marks of hundreds of vanished Demerara sugar estates.
Independent hydrometer tests consistently detect undisclosed added sugar in El Dorado 12, one of the bottlings that sparked the rum world's sugar-transparency debate.

timeline.event: Port Mourant estate founded; its double wooden pot still design survives at Diamond to this day
Demerara Distillers Ltd formed as Guyana's estates consolidate at Diamond
El Dorado premium aged range launched, bringing Demerara marks to consumers

name: Diplomático Reserva Exclusiva
ageStatement: NAS — blend aged up to 12 years
description: Diplomático Reserva Exclusiva is the world's best-known Venezuelan rum and the standard-bearer for the openly dessert-like end of the category. Built around batch-kettle and pot still rums aged up to 12 years, it pours dark and drinks sweet: chocolate fudge, vanilla, orange peel and banana in a silky, liqueur-adjacent texture. That profile is achieved in part through post-distillation sugar — independent hydrometer tests place it around 40 g/L, which the producer does not itemize on the label — so purists classify it as a sweetened rum rather than a traditional dry one. Judged on its own terms, it is expertly blended, hugely popular as an after-dinner pour, and has introduced countless drinkers to aged rum. The squat apothecary-style bottle with its wax-look seal has become one of the category's most recognizable packages.
history: The distillery complex at La Miel, in the Andean foothills of Venezuela's Lara State, was built in 1959 by Licorerías Unidas with Seagram involvement, producing rum and other spirits for decades. When Seagram's empire was broken up, local investors acquired the site in 2002 and formed Destilerías Unidas S.A. (DUSA), building the Diplomático brand into a global export. Long-serving master blender Tito Cordero shaped the Reserva Exclusiva profile. In 2023 Brown-Forman completed its purchase of the Diplomático brand, with DUSA continuing to distill in Venezuela.
production: Diplomático ferments both molasses and concentrated sugar-cane honeys. Lighter components come off column stills, while the heavier heart of Reserva Exclusiva is distilled in copper pot stills and unusual batch kettle stills. The rums age up to 12 years in ex-bourbon and ex-whisky American oak casks before blending. The finished rum is sweetened post-distillation — a deliberate house style the brand describes as complex and smooth, and which independent tests measure at roughly 40 g/L of sugar.
barrel: Ex-bourbon and ex-whisky American oak casks, components aged up to 12 years

tastingNotes.nose: Chocolate fudge, vanilla bean, orange peel, ripe banana and maple.
tastingNotes.palate: Silky and sweet — toffee, milk chocolate, baked banana, licorice and soft oak, more dessert than fire.
tastingNotes.finish: Medium, sugared coffee and vanilla with gentle spice.

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature as a digestif; a large cube tames the sweetness
facts: The distillery sits at La Miel in Lara State, drawing water from the Terepaima foothills of the Venezuelan Andes.
DUSA ferments both molasses and sugar-cane honeys, splitting them between column, pot and rare batch-kettle stills.
Brown-Forman, the maker of Jack Daniel's, acquired the Diplomático brand in a deal completed in 2023.

timeline.event: Distillery complex built at La Miel, Lara State, by Licorerías Unidas
Local investors acquire the site from the Seagram breakup, forming DUSA
Brown-Forman completes acquisition of the Diplomático brand

name: Mount Gay XO Triple Cask Blend
ageStatement: NAS — blend of 5 to 17 year old rums
description: Mount Gay XO is the premium standard-bearer of the world's oldest running rum distillery, whose 1703 deed predates the bourbon and Scotch whisky industries as we know them. A blend of pot and column still rums aged five to seventeen years, it is matured in three cask types — refill American whiskey, ex-bourbon and ex-cognac — hence the Triple Cask name adopted in the brand's recent refresh. The style is quintessential Barbados: balanced, dry-ish and elegant rather than funky or syrupy, with ripe banana, apricot, toasted almond, vanilla and baking spice in careful proportion. Under master blender Trudiann Branker the recipe leans slightly brighter and more cask-driven than earlier XO bottlings. It is often recommended as the poised, whiskey-drinker-friendly counterpoint to Jamaica's wildness and Venezuela's sweetness.
history: A 1703 legal deed listing pot stills at the Mount Gilboa plantation in Saint Lucy makes Mount Gay the oldest surviving rum producer in continuous operation. The estate was later renamed for Sir John Gay Alleyne, the manager who modernized it in the 18th century. Rémy Cointreau acquired the brand in 1989 and has pushed it upmarket since. XO joined the range in the early 1990s; in 2019 Trudiann Branker became the distillery's first female master blender and subsequently reworked XO as the Triple Cask Blend.
production: Molasses-based washes are distilled both in copper pot stills, for weight and fruit, and in a column still for finesse, at the distillery in Saint Lucy parish using coral-filtered Barbados water. Component rums age five to seventeen years in the tropics across three cask types — refill American whiskey barrels, first-fill ex-bourbon and ex-cognac casks — then are blended and married. Barbados rum tradition and the pending GI prohibit added sugar or flavoring.
barrel: Refill American whiskey, ex-bourbon and ex-cognac casks (Triple Cask Blend), 5–17 years

tastingNotes.nose: Ripe banana, dried apricot, toasted almond, vanilla pod and warm oak with a saline breeze.
tastingNotes.palate: Medium-bodied and balanced — baked banana, salted caramel, nutmeg, ginger and cognac-like dried fruit.
tastingNotes.finish: Long and spicy-dry, oak and pepper over lingering orchard fruit.

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature, or with a single large cube
facts: Mount Gay's founding deed of 1703 is the oldest surviving evidence of a rum distillery in continuous operation anywhere in the world.
The brand is named for Sir John Gay Alleyne, the estate's celebrated 18th-century manager — 'Mount Alleyne' was already taken, so his middle name was used.
Trudiann Branker became the distillery's first female master blender in 2019, more than three centuries after its founding.

timeline.event: Deed records working pot stills at the Saint Lucy estate — the founding date on every bottle
Rémy Cointreau acquires Mount Gay
Mount Gay Extra Old (XO) joins the permanent range
Trudiann Branker appointed first female master blender; XO reworked as Triple Cask Blend

name: Planteray Barbados 5 Year Grande Réserve
ageStatement: 5 Years (tropical aging plus cognac-cask finish in France)
description: Planteray Barbados 5 Year Grande Réserve — known until 2023 as Plantation Barbados 5 Year — is one of the great value benchmarks in aged rum and the flagship of Maison Ferrand's double-aging concept. Distilled at the West Indies Rum Distillery in Barbados and aged there in ex-bourbon casks, it then crosses the Atlantic for a final maturation in Ferrand cognac casks in the Charente, layering French oak elegance over tropical richness. The house also applies a small, openly acknowledged 'dosage' of sugar in the cognac tradition, giving a rounded, dessert-leaning profile of coconut, vanilla, banana and orange zest. Critics of sweetening note the dosage; admirers point out that Ferrand discusses the technique openly, unlike many competitors. Equally at home neat on a budget or in daiquiris, it is a fixture of modern cocktail bars.
history: Alexandre Gabriel of Maison Ferrand launched the Plantation range in the 1990s, applying cognac cellar techniques — double aging and dosage — to sourced Caribbean rums. In 2017 Ferrand bought a majority of Barbados' historic West Indies Rum Distillery (founded 1893), turning a négociant brand into a distiller. After years of criticism that the word 'plantation' evoked slavery in the Caribbean, Ferrand announced in June 2023 that the brand would become Planteray, with new labels rolling out from 2024. The Barbados 5 Year carried its Grande Réserve name through the transition.
production: Molasses-based rum is distilled at the West Indies Rum Distillery on the Black Rock shore near Bridgetown, on a combination of pot and column stills. It ages three to four years in ex-bourbon casks in Barbados, then travels to Château de Bonbonnet in the Charente for a further year or more in seasoned Ferrand cognac casks. Before bottling it receives a modest dosage of sugar, a cognac-derived practice Ferrand discloses as part of its house style.
barrel: Ex-bourbon American oak in Barbados (3–4 years)
finishCask: Ferrand cognac casks in the Charente, France (about 1 year)

tastingNotes.nose: Toasted coconut, vanilla custard, ripe banana, orange zest and soft French oak.
tastingNotes.palate: Round and gently sweet — banana bread, caramel, apricot, cinnamon and a cognac-like grape note.
tastingNotes.finish: Medium, vanilla and warm spice with a sugared-almond fade.

servingTemp: Neat, over ice, or as the backbone of a daiquiri
facts: The brand's 2023 rename from Plantation to Planteray followed years of criticism that the old name evoked slavery-era estates.
Its 'double aging' — tropical years in Barbados, then a cognac-cask finish in France — mirrors Maison Ferrand's cognac cellar practice.
Ferrand openly practices 'dosage', a small sugar addition borrowed from cognac and champagne tradition, where many rum brands sweeten silently.

timeline.event: Maison Ferrand acquires majority control of the West Indies Rum Distillery in Barbados
Plantation brand renamed Planteray, announced June 2023
Planteray labels roll out worldwide on the Barbados range

name: Dr. Loosen Wehlener Sonnenuhr Riesling Kabinett
description: A textbook Mosel Kabinett from one of Germany's most storied vineyards: the Wehlener Sonnenuhr, a wall of blue slate rising almost vertically above the river, named for the sundial built among its vines in 1842. Kabinett is the lightest rung of Germany's Prädikat ladder, and this wine shows why the style has a cult following — around 8.5% alcohol, gently off-dry, with peach and green-apple fruit suspended between filigree acidity and cool slate minerality. The sweetness reads as juiciness rather than sugar, and the low alcohol makes it one of the world's great lunch and spice-food wines. Ernst Loosen's estate holds old, ungrafted vines in the site, and the wine ages far longer than its delicacy suggests — decades in good vintages.
history: The Loosen family has owned vineyards in the Middle Mosel for over two hundred years. When Ernst 'Erni' Loosen took over the St. Johannishof estate in 1988, he cut yields, revived old ungrafted parcels in grand-cru-class sites like Wehlener Sonnenuhr, and became one of German riesling's loudest global ambassadors — later adding the mass-market Dr. L label and the Eroica joint venture with Chateau Ste. Michelle in Washington State.
production: Fruit comes from steep, hand-worked terraces of pure blue Devonian slate in the Wehlener Sonnenuhr, including ungrafted vines over a century old. Grapes are picked at Kabinett must weights, pressed gently, and fermented cool with a mix of stainless steel and traditional 1,000-litre neutral Fuder casks; fermentation is stopped naturally or by chilling to retain roughly 40–50 g/L residual sugar balanced by Mosel acidity. No new oak is used.
barrel: Neutral 1,000-litre Fuder casks and stainless steel; no new oak

tastingNotes.nose: White peach, green apple and lime blossom over wet slate and a whiff of spring herbs
tastingNotes.palate: Featherweight yet intense; juicy orchard fruit and mandarin, sweetness lifted by electric acidity and stony minerality
tastingNotes.finish: Long, clean and mineral — slate and citrus zest with a mouth-watering close

servingTemp: 8–10 °C; can age 10–30 years in good vintages
facts: The Sonnenuhr ('sundial') vineyard is named for the working sundial erected among the vines in 1842 to tell workers the time.
Parts of the vineyard are planted with ungrafted, pre-phylloxera vines over 100 years old — the pure slate soil deters the louse.
At around 8.5% alcohol, Kabinett riesling is among the lightest fine wines made anywhere.

timeline.event: Sundial built in the Wehlener Sonnenuhr vineyard, giving it its name
Ernst Loosen takes over the family estate and refocuses on old-vine grand cru sites

name: Whispering Angel Rosé
description: Whispering Angel is the wine most responsible for turning pale Provençal rosé from a beach-bar afterthought into a global luxury category. Created by Sacha Lichine after he bought Château d'Esclans in 2006, it paired a barely-there onion-skin color and bone-dry, delicately fruity palate with relentless lifestyle marketing — and became a phenomenon, especially in the United States, where its summertime ubiquity in the Hamptons earned it the nickname 'Hamptons water'. The blend leans on grenache and cinsault with the local white grape rolle softening the mid-palate. It is now one of the best-selling premium rosés on earth, made in the millions of bottles yet still recognizably in the crisp, saline Côtes de Provence style that inspired a thousand imitators.
history: Sacha Lichine, son of the Bordeaux négociant and writer Alexis Lichine, sold Château Prieuré-Lichine in Margaux to bet on rosé, buying Château d'Esclans in the hills above the Esclans valley in 2006. Working with the late Bordeaux oenologist Patrick Léon, he launched Whispering Angel as the estate's entry wine, with Garrus and Les Clans above it. LVMH's Moët Hennessy bought a 55% stake in 2019, cementing rosé's luxury turn.
production: Grapes — principally grenache and cinsault with rolle (vermentino) — come from estate vines and contracted growers across the Côtes de Provence appellation. Harvest runs in the cool early morning; fruit is destemmed, gently pressed, and the juice cold-settled to keep color extraction minimal. Fermentation is in temperature-controlled stainless steel, with no oak on this cuvée, followed by early bottling to preserve freshness.
barrel: None — stainless steel only; oak is reserved for the estate's upper cuvées

tastingNotes.nose: Fresh strawberry, white peach and grapefruit zest with rose petal and a saline sea-breeze note
tastingNotes.palate: Bone dry and silky; red berries and citrus over a cool, stony core
tastingNotes.finish: Crisp and clean with a faintly salty, mouth-watering close

servingTemp: 8–10 °C; drink young, within a year or two of the vintage
facts: The name refers to the whispering-angel cherubs carved in the chapel at Château d'Esclans.
Its Hamptons-summer ubiquity earned it the tongue-in-cheek nickname 'Hamptons water'.
Sacha Lichine sold a classified Margaux estate to fund his bet that pale dry rosé could become a luxury category — a bet LVMH validated by buying a majority stake in 2019.

timeline.event: Sacha Lichine buys Château d'Esclans and creates Whispering Angel
Moët Hennessy (LVMH) acquires a 55% stake in Château d'Esclans

name: Taylor's 10 Year Old Tawny Port
description: Taylor's 10 Year Old is a benchmark of the aged tawny category: port that trades the inky power of vintage bottlings for the mellow, nutty elegance that comes from a decade's slow oxidation in seasoned oak casks. Field-blend Douro grapes are fortified with grape spirit mid-ferment, then left in the company's Vila Nova de Gaia lodges where the wine gradually fades from ruby to russet, trading fresh berry fruit for fig, toasted almond, butterscotch and orange peel. The '10 Year' on the label is an indicated average age blended across many harvests for a consistent house style. Ready to drink on release, best lightly chilled, and — unlike vintage port — happy to sit open for weeks, it is many drinkers' gateway into fine fortified wine.
history: Founded in 1692, Taylor (fully Taylor, Fladgate & Yeatman) is one of the oldest port houses still under independent family-linked control, today the anchor of The Fladgate Partnership alongside Fonseca and Croft. The house pioneered the commercial Late Bottled Vintage category with its 1965 vintage, released in 1970, and its aged tawny program draws on deep reserves maturing in the Gaia lodges across the river from Porto.
production: Traditional Douro varieties — touriga nacional, touriga franca, tinta roriz, tinta barroca and tinto cão — are grown on schist terraces, many foot-trodden or robotically mimicked in lagares. Fermentation is arrested partway by adding 77% grape spirit, preserving natural sweetness. The wine then ages an average of ten years in seasoned oak pipes in Gaia, deliberately exposed to gentle oxidation, before cross-vintage blending, light filtration and bottling ready to drink.
barrel: Average ten years in seasoned oak casks ('pipes') in the Vila Nova de Gaia lodges
filtration: Lightly filtered; does not throw a heavy sediment like vintage port

tastingNotes.nose: Fig, raisin and dried apricot with toasted almond, butterscotch and candied orange peel
tastingNotes.palate: Silky and medium-sweet; caramelized nuts, honeyed dried fruit and soft spice with balancing Douro acidity
tastingNotes.finish: Long, warm and nutty, drying gently to walnut and orange zest

servingTemp: Lightly chilled, 12–16 °C; keeps for weeks after opening
facts: Tawny port gets its color and nutty flavor from deliberate oxidative aging in wooden casks — the opposite philosophy to bottle-aged vintage port.
Taylor's created the commercial Late Bottled Vintage category, releasing the 1965 vintage as the first LBV in 1970.
An indicated-age tawny's '10 years' is a blended average, letting the house keep a consistent style from bottling to bottling.

timeline.event: Job Bearsley founds the house that becomes Taylor, Fladgate & Yeatman
Taylor's releases the first commercial Late Bottled Vintage port (1965 vintage)

name: Tío Pepe Fino Sherry
