description: Tío Pepe is the world's best-known fino sherry and one of the driest wines made anywhere: palomino fino grapes from Jerez's chalky albariza soils, fortified to 15% and aged under a living blanket of flor yeast that seals the wine from oxygen while feeding on its glycerol. The result is bracing and saline — green almond, dough, olive brine and a bone-dry chalky snap that makes it the definitive partner for jamón, olives and fried seafood. Aged in the vast González Byass solera in Jerez de la Frontera, each bottling blends butts spanning multiple years for perfect consistency. Long dismissed as a niche taste outside Spain, fino has been re-embraced by sommeliers and bartenders, with Tío Pepe as the category's flag-bearer.
history: Manuel María González Ángel founded his Jerez bodega in 1835 at age 23, guided by his uncle José Ángel de la Peña — the 'Tío Pepe' (Uncle Joe) whose private butt of pale, dry fino gave the brand its name, in use since the 1840s. English agent Robert Blake Byass joined the firm, creating González Byass. The rooftop Tío Pepe neon sign over Madrid's Puerta del Sol, first lit in the 1930s, is now a protected city landmark.
production: Palomino fino grapes grow on blinding-white albariza chalk around Jerez. The base wine is fortified to 15% — low enough for flor, the indigenous veil of Saccharomyces yeast, to thrive on the wine's surface inside American oak butts filled five-sixths full. Biological aging proceeds through the fractional solera system, wine moving through criaderas over an average of four-plus years, emerging pale, pungent and bone dry before light filtration and bottling.
barrel: American oak butts in the solera, filled part-full to sustain the flor veil

tastingNotes.nose: Green apple skin, green almond and bread dough with olive brine, chamomile and sea air
tastingNotes.palate: Bone dry, taut and saline; blanched almonds, lemon pith and a chalky mineral bite
tastingNotes.finish: Clean, pungent and appetizing — salt, yeast and bitter almond that begs for food

servingTemp: Well chilled, 6–8 °C; drink within days of opening, like a white wine
facts: Flor — a living film of yeast — protects fino from oxygen and consumes glycerol, making the wine paler, drier and more pungent as it ages.
The brand is named for founder Manuel María González's uncle José, whose personal cask of dry fino was the house's first hit.
The Tío Pepe neon sign above Madrid's Puerta del Sol is protected as part of the city's heritage — the only advertisement allowed on the square's skyline.

timeline.event: Manuel María González founds the bodega in Jerez de la Frontera
Pale dry fino from Uncle José's butt begins selling under the Tío Pepe name

name: Dom Pérignon Vintage 2013
description: Dom Pérignon is the prestige cuvée of Moët & Chandon and the wine that created the category: a champagne made only in declared vintages, only from chardonnay and pinot noir, and only released after extended aging on its lees — around eight years for this bottling. The 2013 comes from one of the last truly late harvests of the modern era, picking stretching into October after a cold spring, which built the taut acidity and smoky, mineral reduction that mark the wine. It was also the first vintage released under chef de cave Vincent Chaperon, who succeeded Richard Geoffroy in 2019. Poised between citrus-and-orchard freshness and the toasted, briochey depth of long lees contact, it is built to evolve for decades through successive 'plénitude' stages.
history: The brand honors Dom Pierre Pérignon, the Benedictine cellar master at the Abbey of Hautvillers from 1668 to 1715, who advanced blending and viticulture (though he did not, as legend claims, invent sparkling wine). Moët & Chandon launched Dom Pérignon in 1936 with the 1921 vintage — the first prestige cuvée ever marketed — and it has since become the most famous luxury champagne in the world, made in undisclosed but substantial quantities within LVMH.
production: Only declared vintages are made; weaker years are skipped entirely. The 2013 blends roughly equal parts chardonnay and pinot noir from grand and premier cru villages across the Montagne de Reims, Côte des Blancs and the Vallée de la Marne, plus the abbey's own Hautvillers vines. After primary fermentation in steel, the wine undergoes secondary fermentation in bottle and rests about eight years on its lees before disgorgement with a low dosage of around 5 g/L.
filtration: Riddled and disgorged after lees aging

tastingNotes.nose: White peach, citrus zest and green mango with brioche, toasted almond, iodine and a flinty, smoky edge
tastingNotes.palate: Precise and vibrant; tight-knit orchard fruit and grapefruit over creamy lees texture and saline chalk
tastingNotes.finish: Long and energetic, smoke and minerals outlasting the fruit

servingTemp: 9–12 °C — slightly warmer than party champagne, to open the aromatics
facts: Dom Pérignon is vintage-only: in years the house judges unworthy, no wine is made at all.
The 2013 harvest ran into October — a rarity in the climate-change era, and the last such late harvest in Champagne for years.
Launched in 1936 with the 1921 vintage, it was the world's first prestige cuvée.
Late-disgorged 'Plénitude' releases (P2, P3) of older vintages emerge after roughly 15 and 25+ years on lees.

timeline.event: Dom Pérignon launched with the 1921 vintage — the first prestige cuvée
Vincent Chaperon succeeds Richard Geoffroy as chef de cave
Vintage 2013 released after about eight years on lees

name: Veuve Clicquot Yellow Label Brut
description: Yellow Label is the non-vintage brut that carries the house of Veuve Clicquot — instantly identifiable by the saturated orange-yellow livery trademarked in 1877 and among the best-selling champagnes in the world. The style is deliberately pinot noir-led: fuller, toastier and more vinous than many NV bruts, softened by a high proportion of reserve wines that iron out vintage differences. Its scale is industrial, its consistency the point — the same biscuity apple-and-brioche profile in Tokyo, Chicago or Reims. Behind the ubiquity stands one of Champagne's most consequential histories: the widow Clicquot's inventions in the 1810s helped turn cloudy, sweet fizz into the clear, exportable luxury the region sells today, and her house's NV brut remains the reference point for celebration champagne.
history: Philippe Clicquot founded the house in 1772; his son François died in 1805, leaving 27-year-old widow ('veuve') Barbe-Nicole Clicquot-Ponsardin in charge — one of the first women to run a champagne house. She invented the riddling table around 1816, solving champagne's clarity problem, ran the 1811 'comet vintage' past wartime blockades to Russia in 1814, and produced an early blended rosé in 1818. The house joined LVMH in 1987; the distinctive yellow label was registered in 1877.
production: Pinot noir leads the blend at roughly half, with meunier and chardonnay completing it, sourced from a broad network including the house's own largely grand- and premier-cru vineyards. Reserve wines — often a third or more of the blend, some aged many years — provide the house signature. After tank fermentation and blending, secondary fermentation happens in bottle with about three years on lees (double the legal minimum), then disgorgement with a dosage near 9–10 g/L.
filtration: Riddled and disgorged after lees aging

tastingNotes.nose: Baked apple, pear and mirabelle with brioche, vanilla pod and a toasty, biscuity autolysis note
tastingNotes.palate: Round and vinous for an NV brut; orchard fruit and citrus over creamy mousse and gingerbread spice
tastingNotes.finish: Medium-long, toasty and dry with fresh acidity

facts: Madame Clicquot's riddling table (c. 1816) — allegedly adapted from her kitchen table — let sediment be worked into the neck of the bottle, making clear champagne commercially possible.
In 1814 the widow smuggled 10,000+ bottles of the 1811 'comet vintage' through wartime blockades to Russia, locking in the market that made her fortune.
The trademark yellow-orange label, registered in 1877, was originally a way to mark the house's dry champagne for the British market.

timeline.event: Philippe Clicquot founds the house in Reims
Madame Clicquot devises the riddling table, revolutionizing clarification
Yellow label trademark registered

name: Krug Grande Cuvée
description: Krug Grande Cuvée inverts champagne's usual hierarchy: rather than reserving its best wine for vintage years, the house pours its greatest effort into a multi-vintage blend recreated from scratch each year. A typical édition assembles well over a hundred base wines from ten or more different years, drawn from a library of reserve wines that can stretch back a decade and a half. First fermentation in small, seasoned 205-litre oak casks and six-plus years on the lees give the wine its signature breadth — honeyed brioche, dried fruit, hazelnut and citrus in layered procession. Each release is numbered (the édition system began publicly with the 163ème) and traceable via the Krug iD code on the back label. It is champagne argued as gastronomy, and priced accordingly.
history: Joseph Krug, a German-born former Jacquesson employee, founded his house in Reims in 1843 around a heretical idea recorded in his cherry-red notebook: the fullest expression of champagne should be independent of any single year's weather. His multi-vintage 'Private Cuvée' embodied the idea for over a century; in the late 1970s the house relaunched it as Grande Cuvée. Krug joined LVMH in 1999, with the founding family's sixth generation still involved and Julie Cavil named cellar master in 2020.
production: Plots across Champagne — including the walled Clos du Mesnil and Clos d'Ambonnay used for other cuvées — are vinified parcel by parcel in small 205-litre used oak casks, kept apart through primary fermentation. The blend unites pinot noir, chardonnay and meunier: typically 120+ wines from at least ten years, with reserve wines up to fifteen years old. After bottling, secondary fermentation and a minimum of about six to seven years on lees precede disgorgement and a restrained dosage.
barrel: First fermentation in small 205-litre seasoned oak casks; no new-oak flavor sought
filtration: Riddled and disgorged after extended lees aging

tastingNotes.nose: Honeyed brioche, hazelnut and almond nougat with dried apricot, candied citrus, gingerbread and blossom
tastingNotes.palate: Full, vinous and layered; baked apple, lemon curd and marzipan carried on a fine, creamy mousse
tastingNotes.finish: Exceptionally long — toasted nuts, honey and citrus oil unwinding slowly

servingTemp: 9–12 °C in a tulip or white-wine glass — the house discourages narrow flutes
facts: Every bottle carries a Krug iD code revealing the édition's composition and disgorgement details.
A single Grande Cuvée édition typically blends more than 120 wines from at least ten different years.
Founder Joseph Krug's 1848 cherry-red notebook setting out his blending philosophy still guides the house — and is still in its possession.

timeline.event: Joseph Krug founds the house in Reims
Grande Cuvée succeeds the Private Cuvée as the house's flagship
Krug iD introduced, numbering éditions and dating every bottle

name: Freixenet Cordon Negro Brut Cava
description: Cordon Negro — the frosted 'black bottle' — is the cava that introduced much of the world to Spanish sparkling wine. Made by Freixenet in Sant Sadurní d'Anoia, the capital of the cava industry, it uses the same bottle-fermentation method as champagne but with Catalonia's native trio of macabeo, xarel·lo and parellada, yielding a lighter, apple-and-citrus style with an earthy, faintly smoky Mediterranean accent. Launched in 1974 and propelled by decades of memorable advertising, it became one of the best-selling traditional-method sparkling wines on the planet, delivering genuine secondary-fermentation character — fine bubbles, a touch of pastry — at an everyday price. It is the standing rebuttal to the idea that bottle-fermented fizz must be expensive.
history: The Ferrer and Sala families, growers in the Penedès since the 1800s, sold their first sparkling wine under the Freixenet name in 1914 — the brand taken from La Freixeneda, the family's ash-grove estate. Carta Nevada's frosted bottle arrived in 1941, and Cordon Negro's matte black version in 1974 became a global icon. Germany's Henkell took majority control in 2018, creating the world's largest sparkling-wine group.
production: Macabeo, xarel·lo and parellada from the Penedès are pressed and fermented separately in stainless steel, then blended and bottled for a second fermentation in the bottle, as cava's DO rules require. The wine rests on its lees underground in Sant Sadurní d'Anoia for well beyond the nine-month legal minimum before riddling, disgorgement and a brut dosage, preserving crisp green-apple fruit with light toasty notes.
filtration: Riddled and disgorged after lees aging

tastingNotes.nose: Green apple, pear and lemon with white flowers, a little bread dough and a dry earthy note
tastingNotes.palate: Crisp and light-bodied; citrus and orchard fruit with brisk bubbles and a hint of almond
tastingNotes.finish: Short-to-medium, clean and dry with a gently bitter apple-skin close

servingTemp: 6–8 °C, well chilled
facts: Cava must be made by the traditional method — second fermentation in the bottle — just like champagne; the difference is place, grapes and price.
Freixenet means 'ash grove' in Catalan, after the family farm La Freixeneda.
Sant Sadurní d'Anoia, Freixenet's hometown, produces the overwhelming majority of all cava; the company's cellars tunnel directly beneath the town.

timeline.event: First sparkling wines sold under the Freixenet name
Cordon Negro launched in its matte black bottle
Henkell acquires majority control of Freixenet

name: La Marca Prosecco
description: La Marca, in its powder-blue livery, is one of the best-selling proseccos in the United States and a fair archetype of the modern category: glera grapes from the plains and hills of Treviso, fermented to sparkle in pressurized tanks rather than bottles, and released young to catch the grape's pear-blossom aromatics. Where champagne builds flavor from yeast and time, prosecco aims for immediacy — green apple, honeydew, white peach and flowers on a soft, gently off-dry palate with easygoing bubbles. Made by a consortium of Trevigiano growers' cooperatives and marketed in America by E. & J. Gallo, it fueled prosecco's explosive 2010s growth and remains the default pour for spritzes and casual celebration.
history: La Marca was founded in 1968 in Oderzo as a union of winegrowing cooperatives in the province of Treviso — 'La Marca Trevigiana' being the historic name for the territory. As prosecco's DOC framework matured (the modern Prosecco DOC dates to 2009, when the grape was officially renamed glera), the consortium's export brand rode the global boom, becoming a top-selling import prosecco in the US through its partnership with E. & J. Gallo.
production: Glera grapes from member growers across the Prosecco DOC zone in Treviso are pressed and cold-fermented in stainless steel. Secondary fermentation follows the Charmat (Martinotti) method: the base wine re-ferments in sealed, pressurized tanks for several weeks, preserving primary fruit and floral aromatics, then is filtered and bottled under pressure with extra-dry sweetness of roughly 12–17 g/L.
filtration: Sterile filtered and bottled under pressure

tastingNotes.nose: Pear, green apple and honeydew with acacia blossom and a hint of white peach
tastingNotes.palate: Soft, lightly off-dry and fresh; juicy orchard fruit with a gentle, frothy mousse
tastingNotes.finish: Short, clean and fruity — made for refreshment, not contemplation

servingTemp: 6–8 °C, well chilled; drink the youngest bottle you can find
facts: Prosecco's grape was officially renamed 'glera' in 2009 so 'Prosecco' could be protected as a place name.
The Charmat tank method costs far less than bottle fermentation and deliberately preserves fresh fruit aromas over bready autolysis.
La Marca is a consortium brand pooling thousands of grower families across Treviso's cooperatives.

timeline.event: La Marca founded in Oderzo as a union of Treviso cooperatives
Modern Prosecco DOC established; glera name adopted for the grape

name: Westvleteren 12
style: Trappist Quadrupel / Belgian Strong Dark Ale
description: Westvleteren 12 is the most mythologized beer on earth: a 10.2% Trappist quadrupel brewed by the monks of Saint-Sixtus Abbey in the West Flanders countryside and sold, in principle, only at the abbey gate by advance reservation. The bottles carry no label — all legal text lives on the crown cap (gold for the 12) — and production is capped at what the small community needs to sustain itself. Years of number-one rankings on beer-rating sites turned a modest monastic economy into a pilgrimage phenomenon, with gate-price cases resold abroad at a tenfold markup. The beer itself justifies much of the noise: dark fig-and-raisin fruit, bready caramel, peppery yeast spice and a port-like depth that keeps evolving for years in the cellar, all from an unfiltered, bottle-conditioned brew of monastic simplicity.
history: The abbey of Saint-Sixtus was founded in 1831 and began brewing in 1838. Unlike other Trappist houses, the monks refused commercial expansion: since 1946 sales have been direct from the monastery, and brewing capacity has barely grown. Westvleteren 12 first appeared in 1940. A one-time exception came in 2012, when gift packs were sold internationally to fund urgent abbey renovations. The monks' stated position remains that they brew to live, not live to brew.
production: A classic quadrupel grist of pale malts deepened with dark candi sugar, hopped modestly with continental varieties for balance rather than bitterness. Fermentation uses yeast obtained from Westmalle, the strain contributing dark-fruit esters and warming phenolic spice. The beer is neither filtered nor pasteurized and referments in the bottle, gaining complexity for years. The monks brew only around 60 weeks' worth of batches annually, assisted by a few lay workers, inside the abbey walls.
coloring: No added coloring — color from dark malts and candi sugar

tastingNotes.nose: Raisin, fig and plum bread with dark caramel, clove and a winey, port-like sweetness
tastingNotes.palate: Full and velvety; dark fruit compote, toffee and brown sugar cut by peppery yeast spice and soft carbonation
tastingNotes.finish: Long and warming — dried fruit, cocoa and spice drying slowly to a bittersweet close

servingTemp: 12–14 °C, poured gently to leave the yeast in the bottle; cellars beautifully for 5+ years
facts: Bottles are sold unlabeled — all legally required information is printed on the crown cap.
Buying at the abbey requires an advance reservation and comes with a per-car limit and a polite request not to resell.
The 2012 international gift-pack sale — a one-off to pay for abbey renovations — was the only sanctioned wide release in the beer's history.
Gate price is a few euros a bottle; secondary-market prices abroad routinely reach $20–25.

timeline.event: Brewing begins at Saint-Sixtus Abbey
Westvleteren 12 first brewed
Monks restrict sales to the abbey itself, ending commercial distribution
One-time worldwide gift-pack sale funds abbey renovations

name: Guinness Draught
description: Guinness Draught is the definitive dry Irish stout and one of the most recognizable drinks in the world: near-black with garnet edges, capped by a dense ivory head produced not by carbonation alone but by nitrogen. The nitro serve — tiny, stable bubbles from a restrictor faucet or the in-can 'widget' — gives a famously creamy mouthfeel to what is actually a light, sessionable 4.2% beer. Flavor comes from roasted unmalted barley: dry coffee and dark-chocolate bitterness over a lightly sweet malt middle, finishing clean and moreish. Brewed at St. James's Gate in Dublin since the Georgian era (and under license worldwide), it is less a craft object than a cultural institution, complete with its own two-part pouring ritual and the harp that doubles as a national symbol.
history: Arthur Guinness signed a famous 9,000-year lease on the St. James's Gate brewery in 1759 at £45 a year, moving from ale to porter and then to the stouter export versions that conquered the world. The harp trademark dates to 1876. The modern Draught product was born in 1959, when brewer Michael Ash's nitrogenation system solved the problem of replicating cask creaminess; the widget brought the nitro pour to cans in 1989 and later to bottles.
production: A grist of pale malt, flaked barley and roasted unmalted barley — the source of the color and dry, coffee-like bite — is brewed with bittering hops and fermented with the house's centuries-old yeast strain. The beer is filtered, then dispensed or packaged with a roughly 75/25 nitrogen-to-carbon-dioxide mix; nitrogen's insolubility creates the cascading surge and tight, long-lasting head that define the pour.
coloring: No added coloring — color from roasted barley

tastingNotes.nose: Roasted barley, coffee and dark cocoa with a faint bready sweetness
tastingNotes.palate: Creamy, light-bodied and smooth; milk chocolate and espresso over gentle malt sweetness
tastingNotes.finish: Dry, roasty and clean with a softly bitter coffee close

servingTemp: About 6 °C, via the two-part pour: fill three-quarters, let it settle, then top up
facts: Arthur Guinness's 1759 lease on St. James's Gate runs for 9,000 years at £45 per annum.
The Guinness harp, trademarked in 1876, faces the opposite direction from the Irish state's official harp emblem so the two could coexist.
Marketing canonized a 119.5-second 'perfect pour'; the surge is nitrogen physics, not carbonation.
The 1989 widget — a nitrogen-releasing capsule in the can — won the Queen's Award for Technological Achievement in 1991.

timeline.event: Arthur Guinness leases St. James's Gate for 9,000 years
Harp trademark registered
Nitrogenated Guinness Draught launched for the bicentenary
Widget brings the nitro pour to cans

name: Sierra Nevada Pale Ale
description: Sierra Nevada Pale Ale is the beer that defined the American pale ale and, by extension, lit the fuse of the craft-beer revolution. First brewed by Ken Grossman in Chico, California in November 1980, it took the balanced English pale-ale template and re-voiced it with a single audacious choice: generous whole-cone Cascade hops, whose piney, grapefruit character had never anchored a commercial flagship before. Against the bland lagers of its era it tasted radical; today its blueprint — caramel-tinged malt backbone, assertive but drinkable American hop finish, 5.6% strength — reads as the genre's textbook. Still brewed with whole-cone hops and still bottle-conditioned with a dab of yeast for freshness, it remains one of the best-selling craft beers in the United States more than four decades on.
history: Ken Grossman, a homebrew-shop owner, and co-founder Paul Camusi built the original brewery by hand in 1980 from scrapped dairy tanks and salvaged equipment, naming it for Grossman's beloved Sierra Nevada mountains. The pale ale was among its first beers and became its engine, funding growth into one of America's largest family-owned breweries, with a second brewery in Mills River, North Carolina opening in 2014 and a reputation for obsessive sustainability engineering.
production: A grist of two-row pale and caramel malts provides the amber color and toasty-sweet backbone. Magnum hops handle bittering, with Cascade — used exclusively in whole-cone form, never pellets — layered late in the boil and in the fermenter's aftermath for the signature pine-and-grapefruit aroma. Fermentation uses the house ale yeast, and the packaged beer is bottle-conditioned: refermented with a small yeast addition for natural carbonation and shelf stability.

tastingNotes.nose: Grapefruit peel and pine resin over toasted caramel malt and a floral, spicy hop note
tastingNotes.palate: Medium-bodied and balanced; bready caramel sweetness meeting brisk citrus-pine hop bitterness
tastingNotes.finish: Crisp and moderately bitter, grapefruit pith fading into clean malt

servingTemp: 7–10 °C; drink fresh for peak hop aroma
facts: Ken Grossman welded the first brewhouse together largely from discarded dairy equipment in 1980.
Sierra Nevada is one of the few large breweries that still insists on whole-cone hops rather than pellets.
Cascade, the hop that defines this beer, was only released by the USDA breeding program in 1972 — eight years before the beer that made it famous.

timeline.event: First batch of Sierra Nevada Pale Ale brewed in Chico, California
Second brewery opens in Mills River, North Carolina

name: Chimay Grande Réserve (Blue)
description: Chimay Blue is the strong dark flagship of the Abbey of Notre-Dame de Scourmont and, for much of the world, the first authentic Trappist beer within arm's reach. Born in 1948 as a Christmas beer and later made year-round (the 750 ml corked format carries the Grande Réserve name), it pours deep chestnut under a rocky tan head: fresh bread and caramel, plum and dried fig, and the peppery, faintly smoky spice of the abbey's own yeast strain, all wrapped around a warming 9% that stays surprisingly nimble. Bottle-conditioned and vintage-dated on the cap, it ages gracefully for years, drying out and gaining port-like nuance. Widely exported yet still brewed within the monastery walls under monastic oversight, it made 'Trappist' a style cue as much as a legal designation.
history: Monks from Westvleteren founded Scourmont Abbey near Chimay in 1850 and began brewing in 1862 — the first brewery to sell beer under the Trappist name. After WWII, Father Théodore De Haene isolated the abbey's house yeast strain in 1948 with guidance from Leuven brewing scientist Jean De Clerck, creating the modern Chimay range beginning with this dark Christmas ale. De Clerck is buried at the abbey. Profits fund the monastic community and regional charities.
production: Brewed inside the abbey with water from its own wells: pale and caramelized malts plus wheat starch, dark candi sugar for strength and color, and continental hops for restrained bitterness. The 1948 house yeast drives a warm fermentation rich in dark-fruit esters and spicy phenols. The beer is dosed with fresh yeast and sugar at packaging and refermented in the bottle, with caps and corks printed with the year for cellaring.
coloring: No added coloring — color from dark malts and candi sugar

tastingNotes.nose: Fresh-baked brown bread, plum and fig with caramel, clove and a peppery yeast note
tastingNotes.palate: Full, smooth and lively; dark fruit and toffee balanced by spicy phenols and gentle roast
tastingNotes.finish: Long and warming, drying to cocoa, raisin and a whisper of pepper

servingTemp: 10–12 °C in a chalice, poured slowly; vintage bottles cellar for 5+ years
facts: Chimay Blue began life in 1948 as 'Spéciale Noël', a Christmas beer, before going year-round.
Every cap and cork is printed with the bottling year — the brewery openly encourages cellaring.
Brewing scientist Jean De Clerck, who helped Father Théodore isolate the house yeast in 1948, chose to be buried at Scourmont Abbey.

timeline.event: Brewing begins at Scourmont Abbey — the first beer sold as 'Trappist'
House yeast isolated; dark Christmas ale that becomes Chimay Blue first brewed
Corked 750 ml format takes the name Grande Réserve

name: Pliny the Elder
description: Pliny the Elder is the archetypal double IPA — the beer that proved an 8% hop bomb could be bone-dry, snappily bitter and dangerously drinkable rather than syrupy. Vinnie Cilurzo, who had brewed the style's very first commercial example at Blind Pig in 1994, created Pliny for the inaugural Double IPA Festival in 2000, and it went on to spend years atop American best-beer polls. Pine resin, grapefruit and dank herbal hops dominate a deliberately stripped-back malt frame; the label's string of bottled-on dates and slogans ('Respect your elders... keep it cold, drink it fresh') enforce the freshness doctrine the beer helped canonize. Distribution remains deliberately thin — a handful of states, no contract brewing — which keeps queues forming wherever it lands.
history: Russian River Brewing was founded in 1997 by Korbel, the champagne-cellars company, and sold in 2003 to its brewer Vinnie Cilurzo and his wife Natalie, who opened the Santa Rosa brewpub in 2004 and a larger Windsor brewery in 2018. Pliny the Elder honors the Roman naturalist tied by legend to the naming of the hop plant; its even scarcer sibling, the triple IPA Pliny the Younger, causes multi-hour lines each February.
production: A pale grist with minimal crystal malt and a dose of sugar dries the body out so hops can lead. Amarillo, Centennial, CTZ and Simcoe supply bittering, flavor and heavy dry-hop additions — layered for pine, citrus and resinous dankness at around 100 IBU. Fermented clean with a Californian ale yeast, packaged unpasteurized in the signature 510 ml bottle, and aggressively date-stamped because hop aromatics fade within weeks.
filtration: Filtered; unpasteurized — meant to be drunk fresh

tastingNotes.nose: Pine resin, grapefruit zest and dank herbal hops over faint honeyed malt
tastingNotes.palate: Dry and vivid; resinous bitterness, tangerine and pine needle on a lean, clean body that hides its 8%
tastingNotes.finish: Long, sharply bitter and clean — pith and pine with no cloying sweetness

servingTemp: 7–8 °C, as fresh as you can find it — check the bottling date
facts: Vinnie Cilurzo brewed the first commercial double IPA at Blind Pig Brewing in Temecula in 1994, six years before creating Pliny.
The beer debuted at the Bistro's inaugural Double IPA Festival in Hayward, California in 2000.
Bottles carry the motto 'Respect your elders' alongside multiple freshness dates — the brewery would rather you not cellar it.
Its February-only sibling Pliny the Younger draws hours-long lines and hotel-booking spikes in Sonoma County.

timeline.event: Pliny the Elder debuts at the inaugural Double IPA Festival
Vinnie and Natalie Cilurzo buy Russian River Brewing from Korbel
Windsor production brewery opens, easing (slightly) the chronic shortage

name: Orval
description: Orval is the eccentric of the Trappist family: a single beer, sold in a skittle-shaped bottle, that belongs to no tidy style category. Brewed since 1931 at the Abbey of Notre-Dame d'Orval in Belgium's Gaume region, it is dry-hopped in the English manner — rare in Belgium — and re-seeded at bottling with Brettanomyces, the wild yeast that slowly eats residual sugars and reshapes the beer in the bottle. Young Orval is bright, hoppy and peppery; a year on it turns drier, funkier and more leathery, which is why devotees date-check caps and argue endlessly about the ideal age. Amber-orange under a monumental rocky head, bone-dry and endlessly aromatic, it is a fixture of every 'greatest beers' conversation and the only beer its abbey makes for the public.
history: Orval Abbey traces to 1132; legend has Countess Matilda of Tuscany recovering her lost wedding ring from the abbey spring in a trout's mouth, exclaiming that the place was a golden valley — 'Val d'Or' — the trout-and-ring image still on every bottle. Destroyed after the French Revolution, the abbey was rebuilt from 1926, and the brewery was established in 1931 expressly to fund reconstruction. Anne-Françoise Pypaert became the first female master brewer in Trappist history there in 2013.
production: Pale and caramel malts with liquid candi sugar form a deceptively simple base, bittered and then generously dry-hopped with continental varieties. Primary fermentation uses the house ale yeast; at bottling the beer is dosed with fresh sugar and a mixed culture including Brettanomyces for months of secondary evolution. Bottle-conditioning drives the ABV from roughly 5.2% toward 6.2% or beyond as the brett works, drying the beer and layering in its hallmark horse-blanket funk.

tastingNotes.nose: Orange peel, hop sack and white pepper over leather, hay and gentle horse-blanket funk
tastingNotes.palate: Bone dry and effervescent; bitter orange, herbal hops and tart, earthy brett character on a lean frame
tastingNotes.finish: Long, dry and appetizing — peppery funk and hop bitterness that call for another sip

servingTemp: 12–14 °C in the house chalice; compare a fresh bottle against one aged a year
facts: The trout with a golden ring on the label comes from the founding legend of Countess Matilda's recovered wedding ring.
Orval is the only beer its abbey sells to the public — plus a small 'Petite Orval' reserved for the monks.
Brettanomyces added at bottling means every bottle is a slow-motion experiment; 'young versus old Orval' is a genuine schism among fans.
Anne-Françoise Pypaert became the first female Trappist master brewer when she took over Orval's brewhouse in 2013.

timeline.event: Cistercian abbey established at Orval
Brewery founded to finance the abbey's reconstruction; Orval first sold
Anne-Françoise Pypaert becomes the first female Trappist master brewer

name: Dassai 23 Junmai Daiginjo
description: Dassai 23 is modern sake's calling card: a junmai daiginjo whose name announces its extravagance — each grain of Yamada Nishiki rice is milled down to 23% of its original size, stripping away the fats and proteins that mask aroma until only the starchy heart remains. When it launched in 1992 that was the most extreme polishing ratio in commercial sake. The result is a genre-defining ginjo profile: honeydew melon, white peach and pear blossom over a delicately sweet, silky palate that finishes clean and faintly floral. Asahi Shuzo brews it with heretical modernity — no master brewer, year-round production, decisions driven by fermentation data — and sells it worldwide as deliberately accessible luxury, a sake meant for wine glasses as much as ochoko.
history: Asahi Shuzo was a struggling rural brewery in mountain Yamaguchi when Hiroshi Sakurai took over in 1984. Betting everything on premium junmai daiginjo, he launched Dassai in 1990 and the 23 in 1992, abolishing the seasonal toji (master brewer) system after their toji departed and replacing craft intuition with measurement and a salaried team brewing year-round. The gamble built one of Japan's most successful sake businesses, culminating in the Dassai Blue brewery in New York State in 2023.
production: Yamada Nishiki — the king of sake rice — is polished for about 168 hours to a 23% seimaibuai, washed and steamed in small batches. Koji is grown by hand in temperature-controlled rooms, and the multiple parallel fermentation proceeds cold and slow in small tanks inside a climate-controlled multi-storey kura, monitored with daily analytical data rather than a toji's rule of thumb. The sake is pressed gently, with no added distilled alcohol — junmai — and shipped refrigerated.
filtration: No activated-charcoal filtration; pressed and lightly fined

tastingNotes.nose: Honeydew melon, white peach and steamed rice with pear blossom and a hint of anise
tastingNotes.palate: Silky and gently sweet; melon and ripe pear over a fine acidity and a whisper of umami
tastingNotes.finish: Clean, cool and moderately long, closing on white flowers and wet stone

servingTemp: Chilled, 8–12 °C; this delicate daiginjo style is not suited to warming
facts: Dassai means 'otter festival', from the brewery's home hamlet of Osogoe ('otter crossing') and a poetic name for scattered manuscripts.
Asahi Shuzo has no toji: brewing decisions are made by salaried staff from fermentation data, year-round, in a climate-controlled tower kura.
At launch in 1992, 23% was the lowest rice-polishing ratio in commercial sake — the number was chosen to beat a rival's 24%.
Dassai Blue, the company's Hyde Park, New York brewery, opened in 2023 to brew sake in the US.

timeline.event: Asahi Shuzo founded in rural Yamaguchi
Dassai brand launched, all-in on junmai daiginjo
Dassai 23 debuts with a record 23% polishing ratio
Dassai Blue brewery opens in New York State

name: Hakkaisan Junmai Ginjo
description: Hakkaisan is the standard-bearer of Niigata's tanrei-karakuchi school — sake that is light, dry and immaculately clean rather than fruity and flamboyant. Brewed in the snow country of Minamiuonuma beneath Mount Hakkai, whose melted snows supply the brewery's celebrated soft spring water, this junmai ginjo polishes its rice to around half its original size and ferments it cold and slow. The result deliberately underplays aroma in favor of texture and drinkability: faint pear and steamed rice, a dry, silky palate, and a finish that vanishes like snowmelt — engineered, in the brewery's own philosophy, to accompany food and conversation rather than dominate them. Where Dassai courts the wine drinker with perfume, Hakkaisan makes the izakaya's ideal companion, equally at home chilled or gently warmed.
history: Founded in 1922 in Minamiuonuma, Niigata — one of the snowiest inhabited places in Japan and the heart of Koshihikari rice country — Hakkaisan grew from a village kura into one of the prefecture's flagship producers while keeping its water source, the Raiden-sama spring, and its food-first house style at the center. The company has modernized boldly, storing sake in a snow-cooled yukimuro warehouse and expanding into whisky, beer and an international following.
production: Sake rice including Yamada Nishiki and Gohyakumangoku is polished to roughly 50%, then brewed in small batches with the ultra-soft snowmelt water of the Raiden-sama spring. Hand-made koji and a long, cold fermentation in the region's frigid winters yield the classic Niigata profile: dry (SMV around +4 to +5), low in amino acids, restrained in aroma. As a junmai, no brewer's alcohol is added; the sake is pressed, settled and bottled for drinking fresh.
filtration: Standard fine filtration; brewed for clarity and cleanness

tastingNotes.nose: Subtle — steamed rice, pear skin, white flowers and a cool mineral note
tastingNotes.palate: Dry, light and silky; gentle rice sweetness with crisp acidity and quiet umami
tastingNotes.finish: Short, clean and snow-cool, leaving the palate refreshed

servingTemp: Chilled 10–12 °C, or gently warmed to nurukan (~40 °C) in cold weather — both suit the dry style
facts: The brewery is named for Mount Hakkai, one of Niigata's three sacred peaks, which looms over the kura.
Its yukimuro storehouse uses about 1,000 tonnes of packed snow to keep sake at a constant ~3 °C year-round with no electricity — a Snow Aged line matures there for three years.
Minamiuonuma's meters-deep snowpack filters into the ultra-soft spring water that defines the house's tanrei-karakuchi (light and dry) style.

timeline.event: Hakkaisan Brewery founded in Minamiuonuma, Niigata
Third-generation yukimuro snow storehouse completed for snow-aged sake

name: Kikusui Junmai Ginjo
description: Kikusui Junmai Ginjo is very often the first premium sake a drinker outside Japan ever tries: inexpensive, dependable and stocked from izakaya chains to suburban supermarkets. Brewed in Shibata, Niigata by a house founded in 1881, it splits the difference between Niigata dryness and ginjo fruit — banana, melon and a touch of citrus over a light, smooth body with a softly dry finish. The rice is polished to 55%, comfortably beyond the ginjo threshold, and no brewer's alcohol is added. It makes no claim to profundity; its achievement is democratic consistency at scale, the gateway bottle that funds the brewery's more adventurous work, like the canned unpasteurized Funaguchi line that pioneered fresh nama sake in a can.
history: Kikusui — 'chrysanthemum water', a name drawn from a noh-theater legend in which chrysanthemum dew grants long life — was founded by the Takasawa family in Shibata, Niigata in 1881. The brewery survived floods and fires to become a modern regional power, making history in 1972 with Funaguchi, the first commercially successful unpasteurized genshu sold in a can, and establishing a sake research institute to study brewing culture.
production: Rice is polished to 55% seimaibuai, washed, steamed and inoculated with koji in the classic Niigata manner, then fermented cool with the brewery's house yeast to draw out gentle banana-melon ginjo esters. As a junmai ginjo, no distilled alcohol is added. The water is the soft groundwater of the Kaji river basin near Shibata, contributing the region's light, clean texture. The sake is pasteurized, filtered and bottled for wide, stable distribution.

tastingNotes.nose: Banana, honeydew and a little mandarin over fresh steamed rice
tastingNotes.palate: Light and smooth; soft fruit sweetness balanced by clean Niigata dryness
tastingNotes.finish: Short and tidy with a gently dry, faintly fruity close

servingTemp: Chilled 5–10 °C is the house recommendation; also pleasant at cool room temperature
facts: The name means 'chrysanthemum water', from a legend in which dew from chrysanthemum petals confers longevity.
Kikusui's Funaguchi, launched in 1972, was the first commercially viable unpasteurized (nama) genshu sake sold in a can — the gold can is now iconic in Japan.
Shibata sits in the Niigata snow belt whose soft water and cold winters define the prefecture's light, dry brewing style.

timeline.event: Kikusui founded in Shibata, Niigata
Funaguchi canned nama genshu launched — a category first

name: Jinro Chamisul Fresh
description: Chamisul Fresh is the flagship of Jinro, the best-selling spirit brand on the planet by volume — the squat green 360 ml bottle is as fundamental to a Korean dinner table as chopsticks. 'Chamisul' means 'real dew', and the product is modern diluted soju: high-purity neutral spirit cut with water to a gentle 16.5%, filtered through bamboo charcoal and rounded with a trace of sweetener. It tastes intentionally soft — faintly sweet, faintly minerally, with a whisper of alcohol heat — engineered for communal shots poured for one another (never for yourself) alongside barbecue and stew. ABV has drifted steadily downward for decades to match softer drinking habits; the green bottle, shared as a standard across Korean brands for recycling, is a category emblem in itself.
history: Jinro was founded in 1924, brewing its first soju in what is now North Korea before relocating south during the Korean War. Its toad mascot appeared in 1955 and became one of Korea's most beloved trademarks. Chamisul launched in 1998, its bamboo-charcoal filtration and falling ABV redefining mainstream soju; Jinro merged into HiteJinro in the 2000s. The brand routinely tops global spirits-volume rankings, moving on the order of ninety-plus million nine-litre cases a year.
production: Neutral spirit — distilled to high purity, largely from tapioca and other starches — is diluted with water to 16.5% and filtered repeatedly through charcoal made from Korean bamboo, which the company credits with the clean finish. A small dose of sweeteners and flavor-rounding agents softens the palate, a defining trait of modern huseok (diluted) soju as opposed to traditionally distilled rice soju. Bottled in the standard shared 360 ml green bottle.

tastingNotes.nose: Nearly neutral — faint rice-candy sweetness, mineral water and a trace of ethanol
tastingNotes.palate: Soft, lightly sweet and water-smooth with a mild peppery warmth
tastingNotes.finish: Short and clean, a gentle sweetness fading fast — built for the next round

servingTemp: Refrigerator-cold, 0–5 °C, in small shot glasses poured for your companions
facts: Jinro is consistently the world's number-one spirit brand by volume, far outselling any whisky or vodka.
Chamisul means 'real dew'; the brand's bamboo-charcoal filtration is its signature claim.
Korean soju brands share the same standardized green 360 ml bottle to simplify nationwide reuse and recycling.
Jinro's toad mascot has fronted the brand since 1955 and now stars as a plush-toy marketing phenomenon.

timeline.event: Jinro founded, brewing soju in Yonggang (now in North Korea)
Chamisul launched, resetting the mainstream soju category
Chamisul Fresh line introduced as ABVs trend downward

name: Hwayo 41
description: Hwayo 41 is the standard-bearer of premium distilled soju — the traditional, rice-based spirit that mass-market green-bottle soju long ago abandoned. Made by a company born from Kwangjuyo, one of Korea's great ceramics houses, it is distilled from 100% Korean rice and water, with no sweeteners or flavorings, then rested in breathable onggi earthenware jars. At 41%, it drinks like a fine white spirit rather than a dinner-table lubricant: steamed rice and pear on the nose, a subtly sweet, mineral palate, and a long, warming, remarkably clean finish. It answers an implicit question — what does soju taste like when treated with the care of a single malt? — and has become the bottle Korean fine dining reaches for, neat, on the rocks or in a highball.
history: The Kwangjuyo Group, revivalists of Joseon-dynasty ceramics, launched Hwayo in the early 2000s (first release 2005) to restore Korea's tradition of distilled rice soju, largely lost after twentieth-century rice-shortage laws pushed the industry to diluted neutral spirit. The name joins hwa (fire) and yo (kiln), tying the spirit to its ceramic heritage — literalized in the onggi clay vessels used for maturation at its Yeoju distillery.
production: Korean rice is milled, steamed and fermented with nuruk-derived cultures, then distilled under reduced pressure (vacuum distillation), which keeps temperatures low and the distillate clean and delicately aromatic. The spirit rests in traditional onggi earthenware — subtly porous clay jars that let it breathe and soften — before dilution with mineral-rich water to 41% and bottling. Nothing is added: no sugar, no flavoring, no oak.
barrel: Rested in traditional onggi earthenware jars (no oak)
filtration: Light filtration only; no charcoal treatment

tastingNotes.nose: Steamed rice, Asian pear and white blossom with a cool mineral edge
tastingNotes.palate: Silky and subtly sweet; rice cream, melon and faint anise over gentle warmth
tastingNotes.finish: Long, dry and very clean — warming without burn

servingTemp: Neat at cool room temperature, over a large ice cube, or lengthened into a highball
facts: Hwayo means 'fire' plus 'kiln' — the company grew out of Kwangjuyo, a celebrated Korean ceramics house, and matures its soju in clay onggi jars.
Vacuum distillation at low temperature preserves delicate rice aromatics that atmospheric stills would cook away.
Premium distilled soju like Hwayo revives the pre-industrial Korean tradition that 1965 grain-rationing laws nearly erased.
The range spans 17% to a cask-strength 53%; the 41 is the house's flagship strength.

timeline.event: Kwangjuyo Group establishes the Hwayo distilling venture
Hwayo premium rice soju launches

name: Angry Orchard Crisp Apple
description: Crisp Apple is the cider that reintroduced America to its founding drink. Launched in 2011 by Boston Beer Company — the Samuel Adams brewer — Angry Orchard vaulted within two years to the top of the US cider market and has stayed there, at times accounting for roughly half of all cider sold in the country. The flagship is engineered for mass appeal: bright golden, softly sparkling, unmistakably sweet up front with fresh apple juiciness, then a clean, lightly tart finish that keeps it closer to refreshment than dessert. Cider purists note the sweetness and the beer-aisle positioning; its defenders note that it built the shelf every American craft cidery now sits on. The gnarled tree on the label nods to the temperamental old orchard varieties behind the juice.
history: Boston Beer had experimented with cider since the 1990s (HardCore Cider) before launching Angry Orchard nationally in 2011, timed perfectly for the gluten-aware, beyond-beer moment. By 2013 it was America's best-selling cider. In 2015 the brand opened an innovation cidery on a 60-acre orchard in Walden, New York, where small-batch and wild-fermented projects run alongside the mainstream lineup.
production: The cider blends culinary apple juice with bittersweet cider apples in the French and Italian tradition — fruit sourced from growers in Europe and North America — fermented cool with selected yeasts to preserve fresh apple character. It is back-sweetened to its signature semi-sweet profile, balanced with malic acidity, filtered bright, carbonated and packaged like a beer: 355 ml amber bottles and cans, sold cold.

tastingNotes.nose: Fresh-pressed red apple, apple candy and a hint of honey
tastingNotes.palate: Sweet, juicy and softly sparkling; ripe apple with mild tannin and a gentle tart lift
tastingNotes.finish: Short and clean, sweetness trailing into crisp acidity

servingTemp: Well chilled, 4–7 °C, from the bottle or over ice
facts: Within about two years of its 2011 launch, Angry Orchard was the best-selling cider in the United States.
The brand is owned by Boston Beer Company, the maker of Samuel Adams.
Its Walden, New York innovation cidery sits on a 60-acre Hudson Valley orchard where experimental wild-fermented ciders are made alongside the flagship.

timeline.event: Angry Orchard launches nationally with Crisp Apple as flagship
Becomes the best-selling cider brand in the US
Innovation cidery opens on an orchard in Walden, New York

name: Eric Bordelet Sidre Brut Tendre
description: Eric Bordelet's sidres are the wine world's favorite ciders — orchard-driven, terroir-obsessed bottlings from the Normandy–Maine borderlands that get poured in Michelin dining rooms where cider otherwise never appears. A former sommelier at Alain Passard's three-star L'Arpège in Paris, Bordelet returned to his family farm in 1992 and began treating cider apples like grand cru grapes: dozens of heirloom bittersweet and bittersharp varieties, organically farmed on schist and granite soils, blended and bottle-fermented into a champagne-corked 750 ml format. The Sidre Brut Tendre style sits between his bone-dry and tender bottlings: burnished gold, finely sparkling, with baked apple, quince and earthy orchard-floor depth over tannic grip and gentle residual sweetness. It is mainstream cider's opposite pole — agricultural, vintage-variable and built for the table.
history: Bordelet trained and worked as a sommelier in Paris, most famously at L'Arpège, before taking over the family orchards at Charchigné in 1992 on the advice of winemaking friends. He replanted and expanded to dozens of traditional apple and pear varieties, applying organic and biodynamic-leaning farming and a vigneron's vocabulary of terroir. His poiré from centuries-old pear trees — notably Poiré Granit — carries the same cult status among sommeliers as the sidres.
production: More than twenty heirloom cider-apple varieties — bittersweet, bittersharp, sweet and acidic in careful proportion — are grown organically on schist and granite. Apples are milled and pressed after maturation; the juice ferments slowly with wild yeasts through the cold Norman winter. Bottled with residual sugars to finish fermenting under cork and cage, champagne-style, it gains a fine natural sparkle; the 'tendre' designation signals a softer, gently off-dry balance versus a strict brut.
filtration: Minimal intervention; may show light haze and sediment

tastingNotes.nose: Baked apple, quince and pear skin with orchard floor, hay and a honeyed note
tastingNotes.palate: Finely sparkling, gently off-dry; concentrated apple fruit with earthy tannin and citrusy acidity
tastingNotes.finish: Long for a cider — bittersweet apple skin, chalk and a saline echo

servingTemp: Cellar-cool, 8–10 °C; treat it like a sparkling wine at the table
facts: Bordelet was the sommelier at Alain Passard's three-Michelin-star L'Arpège before turning cider maker in 1992.
He farms dozens of heirloom apple and pear varieties organically on schist and granite, and talks about them in the language of cru vineyards.
His Poiré Granit is made from pear trees up to around 300 years old.
The ciders are bottled and corked like champagne and referment naturally in the bottle.

timeline.event: Eric Bordelet leaves Parisian sommellerie to take over the family orchards
First estate sidre vintages produced at Charchigné

name: Château Margaux 2015
style: Margaux AOC — Premier Grand Cru Classé (1855)
description: The grand vin of Château Margaux from a vintage widely counted among the finest of the modern era, and one with unusual emotional weight for the estate. 2015 was the year the château inaugurated its new Norman Foster-designed winery, and it became the final vintage overseen by longtime managing director Paul Pontallier, who died in March 2016 before the wine was bottled. The estate marked the occasion with a one-off commemorative bottle, screen-printed in gold rather than dressed with the usual paper label. In the glass it is quintessential Margaux: a wine of perfume and texture rather than sheer mass, layering blackcurrant, violet and cedar over fine-grained tannin. Critical consensus places it among the estate's greatest post-war releases, and it is priced and traded accordingly on the collector market.
history: Château Margaux's vineyard was assembled by the Lestonnac family in the late sixteenth century, and the estate was ranked a First Growth in the 1855 classification — the only one that shares its name with its appellation. Thomas Jefferson singled out its wine on his 1787 Bordeaux tour. The Mentzelopoulos family bought the struggling property in 1977 and restored it to the front rank; the 2015 vintage coincided with the estate's first major architectural expansion in two centuries.
production: Fruit comes from the estate's gravel croupes in the Margaux appellation, farmed plot by plot and harvested by hand. The 2015 grand vin blend is 87% cabernet sauvignon, 8% merlot, 3% cabernet franc and 2% petit verdot — an unusually cabernet-dominant selection representing only about a third of the crop. Fermentation is in oak and stainless vats, followed by roughly 18–24 months in 100% new French oak barriques, with traditional fining.
barrel: 18–24 months in 100% new French oak barriques

tastingNotes.nose: Blackcurrant, violet and crushed raspberry over cedar, pencil shavings and a cool graphite mineral note
tastingNotes.palate: Silky yet densely woven; cassis and black cherry framed by ultra-fine tannin, with tobacco leaf and subtle spice
tastingNotes.finish: Very long, perfumed and precise — florals and graphite persisting for minutes

servingTemp: 16–18 °C, decanted 1–2 hours; built to cellar for 30+ years
pairings: Roast rack of lamb with herbes de Provence
Filet of beef with black truffle jus
facts: Château Margaux is the only 1855 First Growth that carries the same name as its appellation.
The 2015 bottle is screen-printed in gold — a one-time design honoring the late director Paul Pontallier and the new Norman Foster winery, both milestones of that vintage.
The neo-Palladian château, completed in 1815, is nicknamed the 'Versailles of the Médoc'.
Only around a third of the estate's crop makes it into the grand vin in a given year.

timeline.event: Ranked a Premier Grand Cru Classé in the Bordeaux classification
André Mentzelopoulos purchases and begins restoring the estate
Norman Foster-designed winery inaugurated; final vintage of director Paul Pontallier
2015 grand vin released in a commemorative screen-printed bottle

name: Opus One 2019
description: Opus One is the Napa Valley estate founded as a fifty-fifty venture between Robert Mondavi and Baron Philippe de Rothschild of Château Mouton Rothschild — old-world pedigree grafted onto Oakville fruit. The 2019 vintage, from a long, moderate growing season, is considered one of the estate's strongest recent releases: a cabernet-led blend of all five major Bordeaux varieties that balances Napa's dark, plush fruit against European restraint, savory tannin and cellar-worthy structure. One wine is made each year (with a second label, Overture, drawn from declassified lots), and the château-style approach extends to en primeur sales through the Bordeaux négociant system — unusual for an American winery. It remains one of the few California wines with genuine global luxury distribution.
history: Robert Mondavi and Baron Philippe de Rothschild sketched the partnership in Hawaii in 1978 and made a first vintage in 1979, unveiled in 1984 at about $50 a bottle — then the most expensive California release. The name Opus One, chosen by the Baron, casts the wine as a composer's first masterwork. The estate's landmark semi-subterranean winery in Oakville opened in 1991. Today ownership is shared by Constellation Brands and Baron Philippe de Rothschild S.A.
production: Estate vineyards surround the winery in Oakville, planted to tightly spaced Bordeaux varieties on gravelly loam. The 2019 blend is 78% cabernet sauvignon with 8% petit verdot, 6% cabernet franc, 6% merlot and 2% malbec. Grapes are hand-harvested at night, sorted berry by berry, fermented in stainless steel with gentle extraction, then aged about 18 months in new French oak before blending and bottling at the estate.
barrel: About 18 months in new French oak barriques

tastingNotes.nose: Cassis, black cherry and blueberry with violets, graphite, cocoa and a savory bay-leaf edge
tastingNotes.palate: Full and velvety; layered dark fruit, espresso and baking spice over polished, structured tannin
tastingNotes.finish: Long and composed, dark fruit fading into mineral and fine oak spice

servingTemp: 16–18 °C, decanted about an hour; drinks well young but cellars 20+ years
facts: The label carries the silhouetted profiles of both founders, Robert Mondavi and Baron Philippe de Rothschild.
Opus One sells a portion of each vintage en primeur through Bordeaux's Place de Bordeaux négociant network — rare for a Napa estate.
The 1984 debut price of about $50 made it the most expensive California wine release of its day.

timeline.event: First vintage produced from Mondavi's To Kalon fruit
First vintages (1979 and 1980) released simultaneously
Purpose-built Oakville winery completed
2019 vintage released

name: Tignanello 2020
description: Tignanello is the wine that ignited the Super Tuscan movement: a sangiovese-led blend with cabernet, grown on a single limestone-and-schist hillside vineyard in the heart of Chianti Classico yet deliberately bottled outside the appellation's rules. When Piero Antinori dropped the mandatory white grapes, brought in French varieties and aged the wine in small barriques, Italian law relegated it to lowly 'table wine' status — while critics and collectors elevated it to icon. The 2020 vintage, from a warm season tempered by cool September nights, shows the house signature: dark cherry and spiced plum riding bright sangiovese acidity, with cabernet adding graphite depth. It remains one of Italy's most influential and consistently benchmark-setting reds.
history: The Antinori family has made wine since 1385, but Tignanello marks its modern turning point. The vineyard's 1970 Chianti Classico Riserva 'vigneto Tignanello' became, from the 1971 vintage, a stand-alone wine; white grapes were abandoned and cabernet joined the blend with the 1975 harvest. Its commercial success (alongside cousin Sassicaia) forced Italy to invent new legal categories, culminating in the IGT designation that legitimized the Super Tuscans.
production: Fruit comes exclusively from the 57-hectare Tignanello vineyard at 350–400 metres on the Antinori estate in Chianti Classico, on calcareous soils rich in galestro and alberese. The 2020 blend is roughly 80% sangiovese, 15% cabernet sauvignon and 5% cabernet franc. Varieties ferment separately in temperature-controlled vats with the malolactic completed in barrique, then the blended wine ages 12–16 months in French and Hungarian oak, part new, plus a year in bottle.
barrel: 12–16 months in French and Hungarian oak barriques, a portion new

tastingNotes.nose: Dark cherry, plum and blackberry with violets, sweet tobacco, balsamic herbs and cedar
tastingNotes.palate: Medium-full with vivid acidity; morello cherry and pomegranate over fine tannin, cabernet lending graphite and dark spice
tastingNotes.finish: Long and savory — cherry pit, leather and warm spice with a mineral close

servingTemp: 16–18 °C, decanted about an hour; best from 5–20 years after the vintage
facts: Tignanello was among the first Chianti-zone reds to abandon white grapes entirely and among the first sangiovese wines aged in French barriques.
Because it broke Chianti Classico rules, early vintages were legally classified as vino da tavola — table wine.
The neighboring Solaia vineyard, source of Antinori's cabernet-led flagship, sits on the same hillside with the blend proportions inverted.
Meghan Markle named her former lifestyle blog 'The Tig' after Tignanello.

timeline.event: First stand-alone Tignanello vintage bottled outside Chianti Classico rules
Cabernet enters the blend; white grapes dropped for good
2020 vintage released

name: Caymus Vineyards Napa Cabernet Sauvignon
description: Caymus Napa Valley Cabernet Sauvignon is arguably America's most recognizable steakhouse red: dark, plush and unapologetically generous. Made by the Wagner family in Rutherford since the 1972 vintage, it defined a crowd-pleasing California style — ripe dark fruit, soft rounded tannin and cocoa-rich oak — that countless wineries have since chased. Winemaker Chuck Wagner farms and buys fruit across a spread of Napa sub-appellations, blending for consistency rather than vineyard expression, and releases the wine young and ready to drink. Critics debate its opulence; restaurants cannot keep it on lists. Released annually in vast quantities for a wine at its price, it functions for many drinkers as the gateway to premium Napa cabernet, with the smaller-production Special Selection sitting above it.
history: Charlie Wagner and his wife Lorna Belle Glos Wagner, from families farming Napa since the nineteenth century, founded Caymus in 1972 with their son Chuck, producing their first cabernet that year. The Special Selection bottling followed in 1975 and twice took Wine Spectator's Wine of the Year — the only wine to do so. The family business has since grown into Wagner Family of Wine, spanning Conundrum, Mer Soleil, Belle Glos and other labels.
production: Fruit is drawn from Wagner-farmed and grower vineyards across Napa Valley — the label carries the broad Napa Valley appellation rather than a single district. Grapes are picked ripe, often with extended hang time for supple tannin, fermented in stainless steel with carefully managed extraction, and aged roughly a year and a half in oak barrels, around a third new French, before an early, drink-now release.
barrel: Roughly 16 months in oak, about one-third new French

tastingNotes.nose: Ripe blackberry, cassis and black cherry with cocoa powder, vanilla and toasted oak
tastingNotes.palate: Full, plush and rounded; dark fruit compote, mocha and baking spice over velvet-soft tannin
tastingNotes.finish: Warm and generous — dark chocolate and sweet oak with gentle grip

servingTemp: 16–18 °C; no decanting required — made to drink on release
facts: The name comes from Rancho Caymus, the Mexican land grant that once covered the Rutherford area.
Caymus Special Selection is the only wine to have been named Wine Spectator's Wine of the Year twice (for the 1984 and 1990 vintages).
Caymus popularized the ripe, extended-hang-time style that defines modern mainstream Napa cabernet.

timeline.event: Wagner family founds Caymus and crushes its first Napa cabernet
First Special Selection vintage produced
Special Selection 1990 named Wine Spectator Wine of the Year, the label's second win

name: Cloudy Bay Sauvignon Blanc
description: Cloudy Bay Sauvignon Blanc is the wine that put New Zealand on the world map. From its 1985 debut, its explosively aromatic style — passionfruit, gooseberry, fresh-cut grass and limey acidity — was unlike anything Europe was producing from the grape, and it became the international benchmark against which all Marlborough sauvignon is still measured. Demand through the late 1980s and 1990s outran supply so badly that allocations became legend in London wine shops. Now owned by LVMH, the winery blends fruit from vineyards across Marlborough's Wairau Valley, aiming each year for the same taut balance of tropical exuberance and mineral cut. It remains a rare thing: a mass-distributed wine that defined an entire national industry, made in a region where vines were first planted only in 1973.
history: David Hohnen of Western Australia's Cape Mentelle founded Cloudy Bay in 1985, hiring winemaker Kevin Judd and naming the venture for the bay at the Wairau River's mouth — christened by Captain James Cook in 1770. The first vintage was made partly in borrowed facilities, yet within a few years the label had cult status in the UK. Veuve Clicquot took a majority stake in 1990 and full LVMH ownership followed in 2003.
production: Grapes come from company-owned and long-term grower vineyards across the Wairau Valley's stony, free-draining alluvial soils, machine- and hand-harvested cool and pressed quickly to preserve aromatics. Fermentation is overwhelmingly in temperature-controlled stainless steel with selected yeasts; a small fraction ferments in older French oak for mid-palate weight. Parcels rest on light lees for several months before blending and early bottling under screwcap.
barrel: Predominantly stainless steel; a small fraction fermented in older French oak

tastingNotes.nose: Passionfruit, gooseberry and kaffir lime over cut grass, tomato leaf and wet stone
tastingNotes.palate: Vivid and dry with racy acidity; citrus and stone fruit with a saline, chalky undertow
tastingNotes.finish: Clean and mouth-watering, lime zest and mineral notes lingering

servingTemp: 8–10 °C; drink within 2–3 years of vintage for peak aromatics
facts: Cloudy Bay was named for the bay Captain Cook charted in 1770, itself named for the sediment-clouded waters at the Wairau River mouth.
Marlborough had no commercial vineyards before 1973 — twelve years before Cloudy Bay's first vintage helped make it famous.
Founding winemaker Kevin Judd is also a noted vineyard photographer whose images appeared on the label's early marketing; he left to found Greywacke in 2009.

timeline.event: First vintage of Cloudy Bay Sauvignon Blanc
Veuve Clicquot acquires a majority shareholding
Cloudy Bay passes fully into LVMH ownership

name: Springbank 10 Year
description: Springbank 10 is the flagship of Scotland's most stubbornly traditional distillery and the standard-bearer of the near-vanished Campbeltown style: oily, briny, lightly smoky and faintly industrial in the most flattering sense — drinkers call the signature note 'Campbeltown funk'. Every stage of production, from floor-malting the barley to bottling, happens on one site, essentially by hand, which caps output and has turned the 10 into one of whisky's most chased allocated bottles; street prices now run roughly double the official price. Bottled at 46% ABV without chill filtration or colouring, it layers salted citrus, vanilla cream, damp earth and wisps of smoke over a distinctly chewy texture. For many enthusiasts it is the benchmark of characterful, old-school Scotch made with no concessions to scale.
history: Springbank was founded in 1828 on the site of an illicit still, and the Mitchell family — connected with the distillery from its early years — still owns it through J & A Mitchell & Co, making it the oldest independent family-owned distillery in Scotland. Campbeltown once hosted over thirty distilleries and styled itself the whisky capital of the world before crashing in the 1920s; Springbank was one of the very few survivors. The family also revived neighbouring Glengyle in 2004, helping preserve Campbeltown's status as a recognised whisky region.
production: Springbank is the only Scottish distillery to malt 100% of its barley on its own floors, kilning it briefly over peat (roughly six hours of smoke) for a lightly peated profile. The spirit is distilled two and a half times — a partial triple distillation using a direct-fired wash still — then matured in roughly 60% ex-bourbon and 40% ex-sherry casks. Bottling, on site, is at 46% ABV, non-chill filtered and without added colour.
barrel: Approximately 60% ex-bourbon and 40% ex-sherry casks
coloring: No added colour

tastingNotes.nose: Brine, wet wool, light peat smoke, lemon and vanilla custard with an earthy, oily undertow.
tastingNotes.palate: Chewy and coastal — salted caramel, orchard fruit, malt, black pepper and that savoury Campbeltown funk.
tastingNotes.finish: Long, gently smoky and maritime, drying to minerals and sea salt.

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature (18–20 °C); no water needed at 46%
facts: The only distillery in Scotland that performs 100% of production — floor malting, distilling, maturing and bottling — on a single site.
Its spirit is distilled two and a half times, a hybrid of double and triple distillation.
Springbank makes three distinct whiskies on the same stills: lightly peated Springbank, heavily peated Longrow and unpeated triple-distilled Hazelburn.
Campbeltown had more than thirty distilleries in the Victorian era; today it has three, two of them owned by Springbank's parent family.

timeline.event: Springbank founded on the site of a former illicit still
Owners J & A Mitchell revive the neighbouring Glengyle distillery, safeguarding Campbeltown as a whisky region
Surging demand pushes the core range onto strict allocation worldwide

name: Johnnie Walker Black Label 12 Year
description: Johnnie Walker Black Label is the world's benchmark deluxe blended Scotch — a 12-year-old marriage of several dozen malt and grain whiskies from across Scotland, built around a thread of Islay smoke that gives it more character than most blends at the price. Vanilla, raisin and toffee sweetness from the grain whiskies carries dried orchard fruit, winter spice and that signature wisp of peat. Sold in the brand's square bottle with the label set at its trademark slant, it is stocked in practically every bar on earth and mixes as well as it sips; bartenders reach for it in highballs and Scotch cocktails as a smoky-but-safe default. As a one-bottle summary of what Scotland's regions taste like blended together, nothing else is so widely agreed upon.
history: John Walker began selling whisky from his Kilmarnock grocery in 1820, but it was his son Alexander who scaled the business, introducing the square bottle in 1860 and the blend that became Black Label — originally Extra Special Old Highland Whisky. The colour-coded name was formalised in 1909, a year after the Striding Man figure was sketched by cartoonist Tom Browne. Johnnie Walker grew into the best-selling Scotch whisky on the planet, now shipping well over twenty million cases a year for owner Diageo.
production: Black Label is blended from several dozen single malt and grain whiskies, each matured at least twelve years; the exact recipe is undisclosed and adjusted cask by cask to hold the flavour constant. Malts from Speyside and the Highlands supply fruit and depth while Islay and coastal malts contribute the smoky backbone, all rounded on a base of soft grain whisky. The components are married, chill filtered and bottled at 40% ABV.
barrel: Component whiskies matured a minimum of 12 years in ex-bourbon and ex-sherry oak casks
coloring: Caramel colour (E150a) used for batch consistency

tastingNotes.nose: Toffee, vanilla, orange zest and dried fruit with a curl of distant peat smoke.
tastingNotes.palate: Smooth and rounded — raisins, butterscotch, sweet malt and baking spice over gentle smoke.
tastingNotes.finish: Medium, warming and lightly smoky with lingering caramel and spice.

servingTemp: Neat, over ice, or in a 1:3 highball with chilled soda
facts: Alexander Walker adopted the square bottle in 1860 so more bottles survived sea freight and fit in a case.
The label is printed at a precise 24-degree slant, a branding quirk maintained since the nineteenth century.
The Striding Man logo was drawn in 1908 by cartoonist Tom Browne, reputedly on the back of a menu.
Johnnie Walker is the world's best-selling Scotch whisky, sold in roughly 180 countries.

timeline.event: John Walker starts selling whisky from his Kilmarnock grocery
Square bottle introduced by Alexander Walker
Tom Browne sketches the Striding Man emblem
The 12-year-old blend is renamed Black Label

name: Jameson Irish Whiskey
description: Jameson is Irish whiskey to most of the world — a triple-distilled blend of pot still and grain whiskeys that outsells every other Irish brand many times over, moving more than ten million cases a year. Its profile is deliberately friendly: light and smooth with grassy malt, vanilla, honey and a gentle nutty spice from the pot still component, with no smoke anywhere. That easy character made it the engine of Irish whiskey's global revival and a fixture of shot-and-beer culture, Irish coffees and whiskey sours alike. Serious drinkers graduate to its pot-still siblings, but Jameson's role in the category is hard to overstate: it kept Irish whiskey alive through the industry's collapse and remains the door through which most drinkers enter it.
history: The Bow Street distillery in Dublin was founded in 1780, and John Jameson — a Scot from Alloa — took control around the turn of that century, building it into one of Dublin's great whiskey houses. After Irish whiskey's twentieth-century collapse, Jameson's owners merged with rivals Powers and Cork Distilleries in 1966 to form Irish Distillers, and production moved to the New Midleton distillery in County Cork in 1975. Under Pernod Ricard ownership since 1988, Jameson has grown from under half a million cases to more than ten million, surpassed in 2022.
production: Jameson blends triple-distilled single pot still whiskey — made from a mash of malted and unmalted barley in Midleton's enormous copper pots — with lighter grain whiskey distilled from maize in column stills. The proportions are undisclosed. Both components mature in ex-bourbon barrels and ex-sherry butts, legally at least three years though typically longer, before being married and bottled at 40% ABV.
barrel: Ex-bourbon American oak barrels and ex-Oloroso sherry butts

tastingNotes.nose: Light floral malt, cut grass, vanilla and a touch of sherried orchard fruit.
tastingNotes.palate: Smooth and sweet — honey, vanilla, toasted wood and a soft pot-still nuttiness.
tastingNotes.finish: Short and clean with mild spice and lingering honeyed malt.

servingTemp: Neat, over ice, or with ginger ale and lime
facts: John Jameson was a Scot from Alloa who married into the Haig whisky dynasty before building his Dublin whiskey empire.
Every label carries the Jameson family motto 'Sine Metu' — without fear — said by family lore to honour ancestors who fought pirates.
Jameson outsells the next-largest Irish whiskey brand several times over, passing ten million annual cases in 2022.
Production left Dublin in 1975; the original Bow Street site is now a visitor centre in the Smithfield district.

timeline.event: Bow Street distillery founded in Dublin; John Jameson soon takes the helm
Jameson joins Powers and Cork Distilleries to form Irish Distillers
Production moves to the New Midleton distillery in County Cork
Annual sales surpass ten million cases

name: Redbreast 12 Year
description: Redbreast 12 is the benchmark of single pot still Irish whiskey — the style, unique to Ireland, made from a mixed mash of malted and unmalted barley distilled in copper pots. The raw barley delivers the category's signatures: a creamy, almost oily texture and a bristling spice the Irish call pot-still character, layered here with oloroso sherry fruit, baked apple, hazelnut and Christmas-cake richness. When Irish whiskey nearly died in the twentieth century, Redbreast came closer to extinction than any famous name, disappearing in 1985 before a 1991 revival that grew into today's acclaimed range. Critics routinely place the 12 among the best-value whiskeys of any nation, and it is the bottle most often used to show doubters that Irish whiskey can be every bit as serious as Scotch.
history: Redbreast began as a bonder's whiskey: from 1912 the London wine and spirit merchant W & A Gilbey sold Jameson-distilled pot still whiskey matured in its own casks under the name, inspired — the story goes — by the chairman's fondness for birds, hence the robin. As Irish whiskey contracted, bonding died out and Redbreast was discontinued in 1985. Irish Distillers revived it at Midleton in 1991, and the brand has since grown into the most decorated family in the single pot still revival.
production: A mash of roughly equal malted and unmalted barley — the exact ratio is undisclosed — is triple distilled in Midleton's large copper pot stills, taking a heavy, flavour-dense cut. The spirit matures a minimum of twelve years in a combination of first-fill and refill ex-bourbon barrels and first-fill oloroso sherry butts from Jerez, then is vatted and bottled at 40% ABV. The unmalted barley is what gives the whiskey its distinctive creamy weight and peppery spice.
barrel: First-fill and refill ex-bourbon barrels with first-fill Oloroso sherry butts

tastingNotes.nose: Baked apple, sherried dried fruit, toasted nuts, marzipan and a cut of fresh pot-still spice.
tastingNotes.palate: Creamy and full — Christmas cake, raisins, hazelnut, ginger and clove riding a silky, oily texture.
tastingNotes.finish: Long and spicy, sweet fruit slowly drying to oak and barley.

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature (18–20 °C)
facts: Named for the robin redbreast, reputedly a nod to the birdwatching hobby of a Gilbey's chairman.
One of the last surviving 'bonder's whiskeys' — merchants once bought new spirit from distilleries and matured it in their own casks.
Discontinued in 1985 at the nadir of Irish whiskey, and revived by Irish Distillers in 1991.
Single pot still whiskey's mixed mash of malted and unmalted barley originated partly as a dodge around a British tax on malted barley.

timeline.event: W & A Gilbey first sells Redbreast as a bonded Jameson pot still whiskey
Redbreast discontinued as bonding dies out
Irish Distillers relaunches Redbreast 12 from Midleton

name: Green Spot
description: Green Spot is a living relic of Ireland's whiskey-bonding tradition — a single pot still whiskey created for the Dublin wine merchants Mitchell & Son, who once daubed coloured spots of paint on their maturing casks to mark intended ages. Green marked ten years; blue, yellow and red marked others. For decades it was one of the rarest famous whiskeys in the world, a few hundred cases a year sold almost entirely over one Dublin shop counter, and the only bonder's whiskey to survive the industry's collapse. Now produced at Midleton with wider distribution, it is fresher and orchard-brighter than its sibling Redbreast: green apple, gooseberry and menthol over honeyed malt and classic pot-still spice. Its survival story and singular style have made it a quiet cult favourite among Irish whiskey drinkers.
history: Mitchell & Son, wine and provisions merchants in Dublin since 1805, began bonding whiskey in 1887, sending their own casks to the Jameson distillery to be filled and maturing them in cellars beneath the city.
