The Spot range emerged in the early twentieth century, and Green Spot alone survived Irish whiskey's long decline — by the 1980s reportedly down to around five hundred cases a year. Irish Distillers now makes it at Midleton in partnership with the Mitchell family; a US launch followed in 2014, and the discontinued Yellow and Red Spots have since been revived.
production: Green Spot is triple distilled at Midleton from a mixed mash of malted and unmalted barley — proportions undisclosed — in the single pot still tradition. It is vatted from whiskeys aged roughly seven to ten years, matured in ex-bourbon American oak with a portion (about a quarter) drawn from sherry casks, and bottled at 40% ABV. The unmalted barley lends the trademark green-orchard snap and peppery spice.
barrel: Ex-bourbon American oak barrels with roughly a quarter ex-sherry casks

tastingNotes.nose: Green apple, pear, cut hay and menthol with honey and toasted oak.
tastingNotes.palate: Lively orchard fruit and barley sugar, cream, clove and peppery pot-still spice.
tastingNotes.finish: Medium, crisp and gently spiced, fading through green fruit to vanilla.

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature (18–20 °C)
facts: The 'spot' names come from Mitchell & Son literally daubing paint spots on casks — blue, green, yellow and red for different intended ages.
For much of the late twentieth century Green Spot was the only surviving bonder's Irish whiskey, sold almost exclusively at one Dublin shop.
Mitchell & Son, founded in 1805, still trades in Dublin and remains involved with the brand.
Green Spot reached the United States for the first time in 2014.

timeline.event: Mitchell & Son founded in Dublin
Mitchells begin bonding whiskey filled at Jameson's Bow Street distillery
Green Spot launched in the United States

name: Bushmills 10 Year Single Malt
description: Bushmills 10 is the flagship single malt of Ireland's north and a gentle counterpoint to Scotch malt orthodoxy: made from 100% malted barley like its Scottish cousins, but unpeated and triple distilled in small pots, which strips out smoke and weight and leaves honey, vanilla, ripe orchard fruit and milk chocolate in a light, silky frame. It comes from the distillery in the County Antrim village of Bushmills that anchors one of whiskey's most repeated claims — a 1608 royal licence to distil in the surrounding territory, which the brand wears as 'the oldest licensed whiskey distillery in the world'. As an approachable, well-priced introduction to malt whiskey without smoke, it has few rivals, and it anchors a range whose older sherried siblings enjoy strong critical regard.
history: In 1608 King James I granted Sir Thomas Phillips a licence to distil in the territory around Bushmills — the basis of the brand's 'oldest licensed' claim — though the Old Bushmills Distillery Company itself was registered in 1784. A fire levelled the distillery in 1885, and rebuilt Victorian buildings still stand. Uniquely, Bushmills kept faith with malt whiskey through the era when Dublin's pot still houses dominated. Ownership has churned in the modern era: Irish Distillers from 1972, Pernod Ricard, Diageo from 2005, and since 2015 Proximo Spirits, which acquired Bushmills in a swap that sent Diageo full control of Don Julio tequila.
production: Bushmills mashes 100% unpeated malted barley and triple distils the spirit in relatively small copper pot stills, taking a narrow, clean cut — the source of the house's light, fruity style. The 10-year-old matures mainly in ex-bourbon American oak barrels, with a portion of Oloroso sherry casks contributing to the marriage before bottling at 40% ABV. All spirit is distilled, matured and bottled at the village site on Northern Ireland's Causeway Coast.
barrel: Mainly ex-bourbon American oak barrels with a portion of Oloroso sherry casks

tastingNotes.nose: Honey, vanilla, ripe apple and banana with light toasted oak.
tastingNotes.palate: Soft and silky — milk chocolate, malt, orchard fruit and a whisper of dried fruit sweetness.
tastingNotes.finish: Gentle and clean, honeyed malt fading with mild oak spice.

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature (18–20 °C) or with a small cube
facts: The famous 1608 licence was granted to the surrounding territory by King James I; the Bushmills company itself was registered in 1784.
Bushmills kept making malt whiskey through centuries when pot still blends dominated Irish distilling.
In 2015 Diageo traded Bushmills to Proximo Spirits in exchange for full ownership of Don Julio tequila plus cash.
The distillery sits a few miles from the Giant's Causeway on the Antrim coast.

timeline.event: King James I licenses distilling in the Bushmills territory
Old Bushmills Distillery Company registered
Fire destroys the distillery, which is rebuilt
Proximo Spirits acquires Bushmills from Diageo in the Don Julio swap

name: Yamazaki 12 Year
description: Yamazaki 12 is the whisky that introduced the world to Japanese single malt — launched in 1984 from Japan's first malt distillery and, in 2003, the first Japanese whisky to win gold at the International Spirits Challenge. Its style is precise and quietly exotic: ripe peach and persimmon, honey and coconut from American oak, sherried depth from Spanish oak, and the signature of Japanese mizunara oak — sandalwood and temple-incense spice found nowhere else in whisky. The global Japanese whisky boom turned it from bartender's secret to allocated trophy; official prices have climbed steeply and street prices sit far above even those. It remains the definitive expression of Suntory's elegant, blending-house approach to malt whisky, and the single bottle most drinkers name when Japanese whisky comes up.
history: Shinjiro Torii built the Yamazaki distillery in 1923 in a misty river-confluence valley between Osaka and Kyoto, hiring Masataka Taketsuru — later founder of rival Nikka — as his first distillery manager. For decades its malt vanished into blends; the Yamazaki single malt appeared only in 1984, as the flagship of Suntory's push to elevate the category. The 2003 ISC gold medal, the same era's international awards for its siblings, and the 2014-onward boom in Japanese whisky demand transformed the 12 into one of the world's most sought-after standard bottlings.
production: Yamazaki is engineered for variety: wooden and stainless washbacks, multiple yeast strains, and eight pairs of pot stills in different shapes and sizes produce dozens of distinct distillate styles on one site. The 12 marries malt matured in American white oak, Spanish oak sherry casks and rare Japanese mizunara oak, whose porous, slow-grown wood contributes incense and sandalwood notes. Water comes from the bamboo-covered slopes of the Yamazaki valley, and bottling is at 43% ABV.
barrel: American white oak, Spanish oak sherry casks and Japanese mizunara oak

tastingNotes.nose: Peach, persimmon and candied orange with honey, coconut and a thread of incense.
tastingNotes.palate: Silky and layered — ripe stone fruit, honeyed malt, cinnamon, and mizunara's sandalwood spice.
tastingNotes.finish: Long and gently spiced, dried fruit and incense with a clean oak fade.

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature (18–20 °C); in Japan also served mizuwari (with water and ice)

awards.award: Gold — International Spirits Challenge (first Japanese whisky to win ISC gold)

facts: Yamazaki is Japan's first malt whisky distillery, built in 1923 on a site prized for water once used by tea master Sen no Rikyu.
Masataka Taketsuru, who later founded rival Nikka, was Yamazaki's first distillery manager.
Mizunara oak is so porous and knotty that coopers consider it among the hardest woods to make casks from — and it takes centuries-old trees to yield staves.
Sustained shortages mean the 12 now sells well above its official price almost everywhere.

timeline.event: Construction of the Yamazaki distillery begins under Shinjiro Torii
Yamazaki 12 launched as Suntory's flagship single malt
First Japanese whisky awarded gold at the International Spirits Challenge
Official prices raised sharply amid continued global allocation

name: Hakushu 12 Year
description: Hakushu 12 is the green counterpart to Yamazaki's amber — a single malt from Suntory's forest distillery in the foothills of the Southern Japanese Alps that tastes the way its setting looks: fresh mint, green apple, cucumber and young bamboo over soft granite-water malt, with a faint curl of smoke from lightly peated barley. It is the definitive whisky for the Japanese highball; cut with sparkling water it turns almost alpine. Demand from the Japanese whisky boom outran mature stock so badly that Suntory suspended the 12 in 2018, restoring it only in 2021, and it has been allocated ever since, with street prices well above the official figure. Among the world's major malts, nothing else occupies its niche of herbal, forest-floor freshness.
history: Suntory built Hakushu in 1973, on the fiftieth anniversary of breaking ground at Yamazaki, choosing a forested site 700 metres up in Yamanashi Prefecture for its soft snowmelt water. Through expansions in the 1980s the complex ranked among the largest malt distilleries in the world. The 12-year-old became the flagship of its single malt range in the 1990s, was suspended in 2018 when aged stocks ran dry — a defining episode of the Japanese whisky shortage — and returned to shelves in 2021, still rationed.
production: Hakushu ferments in wooden washbacks with multiple yeast strains and distils through pot stills of assorted shapes and sizes, generating many distillate types, some from lightly peated malt. Water is snowmelt from Mount Kaikomagatake, filtered through granite — the same source Suntory bottles as mineral water. The 12 draws mainly on American oak ex-bourbon casks matured in cool, high-altitude forest warehouses and is bottled at 43% ABV.
barrel: Mainly American oak ex-bourbon hogsheads and barrels, matured in high-altitude forest warehouses

tastingNotes.nose: Green apple, mint, cucumber and pine needles with a suggestion of citrus and smoke.
tastingNotes.palate: Crisp and vivid — pear, yuzu, sweet malt and green herbs, the peat a background whisper.
tastingNotes.finish: Clean and refreshing, gently smoky, with lingering mint and green tea.

servingTemp: Neat at 18–20 °C, or as a Japanese highball: 1:3–1:4 with well-chilled sparkling water over clear ice
facts: Opened in 1973 exactly fifty years after Yamazaki, and expanded until it ranked among the world's largest malt distilleries.
The grounds double as a certified bird sanctuary in the forest at about 700 metres elevation.
The 12-year-old was suspended in 2018 for lack of aged stock — a signature casualty of the Japanese whisky boom — and only returned in 2021.
The distillery shares its granite-filtered water source with one of Japan's best-selling bottled mineral waters.

timeline.event: Hakushu distillery opens in the forests of Yamanashi Prefecture
Hakushu 12 established as the distillery's flagship single malt
Sales suspended as aged stock runs out
Hakushu 12 returns to the market on allocation

name: Hibiki Japanese Harmony
description: Hibiki Japanese Harmony is Suntory's showcase of the blender's art — malt whiskies from Yamazaki and Hakushu married with Chita grain whisky across five cask types, including sherry butts and Japanese mizunara oak. The result is a study in seamlessness: honey, candied orange peel, white chocolate and rose-like florals in a texture so polished that no single element protrudes, which is precisely the point. Its 24-faceted decanter, representing the twenty-four small seasons of the traditional Japanese calendar, and paper label of handmade Echizen washi make it one of the most recognisable bottles in spirits. Launched in 2015 as an unaged successor when stocks of the beloved Hibiki 12 ran short, Harmony carries the brand whose 17-year-old sibling starred in the film Lost in Translation.
history: Suntory created Hibiki in 1989 to mark the company's ninetieth anniversary, positioning it as the pinnacle of Japanese blending. The range's aged bottlings — 12, 17, 21 and 30 years — accumulated an outsized share of international blended-whisky awards through the 2000s. As the Japanese whisky boom drained mature inventory, the 12 was discontinued and Japanese Harmony arrived in 2015 as the new entry point; the 17, immortalised by Bill Murray's Suntory commercial scenes in Lost in Translation (2003), was suspended in 2018.
production: Harmony is blended from more than ten whiskies drawn from Suntory's three distilleries: malt from Yamazaki and Hakushu and grain from Chita, matured in five cask types — American white oak, ex-sherry, ex-bourbon, mizunara and others; the precise recipe is undisclosed. Suntory's blending team, led by its chief blender, assembles each batch to a fixed profile, and the whisky is bottled at 43% ABV.
barrel: Five cask types including American white oak, ex-sherry butts and Japanese mizunara oak

tastingNotes.nose: Honey, orange peel, rose and lily florals with white chocolate and light oak.
tastingNotes.palate: Silky and rounded — apricot, honeycomb, candied citrus and a delicate mizunara spice.
tastingNotes.finish: Medium, elegant and clean, with soft spice and a floral echo.

servingTemp: Neat at 18–20 °C, or in a highball with chilled sparkling water
facts: The decanter's 24 facets represent the sekki — the twenty-four subdivisions of the traditional Japanese lunar calendar.
Labels are made from Echizen washi, handmade paper from a fifteen-hundred-year-old Japanese craft tradition.
Hibiki means 'resonance' or 'echo' in Japanese.
The brand's 17-year-old expression featured in Sofia Coppola's Lost in Translation before being suspended in 2018 amid stock shortages.

timeline.event: Hibiki brand launched for Suntory's 90th anniversary
Japanese Harmony released as the range's new no-age-statement flagship
Sibling Hibiki 17 suspended amid the Japanese whisky shortage

name: Nikka From the Barrel
description: Nikka From the Barrel packs more whisky per centimetre than almost anything on the shelf: a high-strength blend of Yoichi and Miyagikyo malts with Coffey-still grain whisky, re-married in casks and bottled at 51.4% ABV in a squat, minimalist 500 ml flask that has become a design icon. The concept is right in the name — deliver the dense, spicy, cask-fresh character of whisky drawn straight from the barrel. It drinks far above its price: toffee, winter spice, dried fruit and oak arrive with real force, then settle into surprising polish. Long a cult bottle among bartenders in Japan and Europe, it finally received official US distribution in 2018 and remains one of the consensus value picks in Japanese whisky, allocation madness notwithstanding.
history: Nikka launched From the Barrel in Japan in 1985, an era when whisky at home meant long highballs and light blends — a concentrated 51.4% pocket flask was a deliberate contrarian statement. The company behind it was founded in 1934 by Masataka Taketsuru, the Japanese chemist who apprenticed in Scottish distilleries (including Hazelburn in Campbeltown) and built Yoichi on Hokkaido to replicate Scotland's conditions. The blend's European release in 2014 and US arrival in 2018 turned a domestic curiosity into a global staple.
production: Malt whiskies from Nikka's two distilleries — coal-fired, heavy Yoichi and elegant Miyagikyo — are blended with grain whisky from Miyagikyo's antique Coffey stills; the recipe is undisclosed. Unusually, the finished blend is returned to used casks for several months of 'marriage' so the components knit together, then bottled with minimal dilution at 51.4% ABV in the signature 500 ml square bottle.
barrel: Component whiskies fully matured in oak, then re-casked together for several months of marrying

tastingNotes.nose: Toffee, baked apple, orange peel, clove and polished oak with a whiff of smoke.
tastingNotes.palate: Big and mouth-filling — caramel, dried fruit, cinnamon, dark chocolate and peppery oak spice.
tastingNotes.finish: Long and warming, spice and fruitcake slowly receding into oak.

servingTemp: Neat at 18–20 °C; takes water and ice exceptionally well at 51.4%
facts: Bottled at 51.4% ABV — the equivalent of 90 proof on the old British scale.
The 500 ml bottle's tiny label and squat form were designed to suggest whisky decanted straight from a cask sample.
Nikka founder Masataka Taketsuru apprenticed at Scottish distilleries, including Hazelburn in Campbeltown, before building Yoichi in 1934.
Despite decades of cult status abroad, it was not officially sold in the United States until 2018.

timeline.event: Masataka Taketsuru founds Nikka and builds the Yoichi distillery
From the Barrel launched in Japan
Official United States release

name: Kavalan Classic Single Malt
description: Kavalan Classic is the whisky that put Taiwan on the map — the inaugural single malt from a distillery that did not exist before 2005 and was beating Scottish veterans in blind tastings within five years. Its secret is climate: in subtropical Yilan County, heat and humidity drive whisky into oak at a furious pace, with evaporation losses many times Scotland's, so a few years of maturation deliver the depth of far older malts. The Classic tastes unmistakably of that hothouse: mango, papaya and banana over vanilla, honey and clean oak, polished and tropical. Carrying no age statement because age means something different here, it stands as the proof of concept for New World whisky — that geography can compress time.
history: Taiwan's King Car Group began building Kavalan in 2005, the island's first whisky distillery, named for the indigenous people of the Yilan plain and guided by the late maturation scientist Dr Jim Swan. The first spirit ran in 2006 and the first bottles, the Classic among them, went on sale in December 2008. In 2010 a Kavalan topped a Burns Night blind tasting in Scotland reported by The Times, and in 2015 its Solist Vinho Barrique was named World's Best Single Malt at the World Whiskies Awards — a coronation for Taiwanese whisky barely a decade old.
production: Kavalan distils in copper pots using snowmelt-fed spring water from the mountains above Yilan, then matures in a hot, humid multi-storey warehouse where summer temperatures soar — the angel's share can exceed ten per cent a year, versus roughly two in Scotland. The Classic is vatted from a range of casks including ex-bourbon, ex-sherry and wine barrels, then bottled at 40% ABV. The exact recipe is undisclosed.
barrel: A vatting of ex-bourbon, ex-sherry and wine casks matured in hot subtropical warehouses

tastingNotes.nose: Mango, banana and melon with vanilla, honey and light floral oak.
tastingNotes.palate: Ripe tropical fruit, butterscotch, coconut and gentle ginger spice on a clean malt base.
tastingNotes.finish: Medium, sweet and softly oaky with lingering tropical fruit.

servingTemp: Neat at 18–20 °C; holds up well over a single large cube in warm weather
facts: Kavalan is named for the indigenous Kavalan people of the Yilan plain where the distillery stands.
Taiwan's heat drives an angel's share of ten per cent or more a year — roughly five times Scotland's — so whisky matures dramatically faster.
In January 2010 a Kavalan malt topped a blind Burns Night tasting in Scotland against Scotch competition, a result reported worldwide.
The late Dr Jim Swan, consultant architect of Kavalan's maturation, was nicknamed 'the Einstein of whisky'.

timeline.event: King Car Group begins construction of Taiwan's first whisky distillery
First spirit runs from the stills
Kavalan Classic released as the distillery's first single malt
Kavalan Solist Vinho Barrique named World's Best Single Malt at the World Whiskies Awards

name: Amrut Fusion
description: Amrut Fusion is the bottle that forced the whisky world to take India seriously. The name describes the recipe: about three-quarters unpeated barley grown in north India fused with a quarter peated barley imported from Scotland, distilled in Bangalore and matured at 3,000 feet in a climate that triples the pace of Scottish aging. Bottled at a muscular 50% ABV without chill filtration, it piles dark chocolate, barley richness, tropical fruit and drifting campfire smoke onto a dense, chewy frame. When Jim Murray's Whisky Bible named it the third-finest whisky in the world in its 2010 edition, a distillery few outside India had heard of became a fixture of serious whisky shelves — and 'Indian single malt' stopped being a punchline and became a category.
history: Amrut Distilleries, founded in Bangalore in 1948 by the Jagdale family, spent decades making spirits for the Indian market before quietly perfecting single malt. Doubting India would buy premium home-grown whisky, the family launched Amrut Single Malt in Glasgow in 2004 — reasoning that winning over the Scots would settle the argument. Fusion followed in 2009 as the flagship of the export range, and the 2010 Whisky Bible ranking made it the most celebrated Indian whisky ever bottled.
production: Unpeated six-row barley from the plains of Punjab and Rajasthan and peated barley malted in Scotland are mashed, fermented and distilled separately in Amrut's pot stills, then married after maturation. Aging runs roughly four years in ex-bourbon and new American oak at Bangalore's 3,000-foot elevation, where the angel's share exceeds ten per cent a year. Fusion is bottled at 50% ABV, non-chill filtered.
barrel: Ex-bourbon and new American oak casks, matured at altitude in Bangalore's tropical climate

tastingNotes.nose: Rich barley, dark chocolate, mango and orange with soft peat smoke and baking spice.
tastingNotes.palate: Dense and chewy — toffee, espresso, dried fruit and chilli-flecked smoke at full 50% strength.
tastingNotes.finish: Long, spicy and gently smoky, with cocoa and barley sugar persisting.

servingTemp: Neat at 18–20 °C; a few drops of water open the fruit at 50%
facts: Named 'Third-Finest Whisky in the World' in Jim Murray's Whisky Bible 2010, the review that made Amrut's global reputation.
Amrut means 'nectar of immortality' in Sanskrit, from the churning-of-the-ocean myth.
Amrut launched its single malt in Glasgow in 2004, betting that Scottish acceptance would validate Indian whisky.
Bangalore's climate matures whisky roughly three times faster than Scotland's, at the cost of an angel's share above ten per cent a year.

timeline.event: Amrut Distilleries founded in Bangalore by the Jagdale family
Amrut Single Malt launched in Glasgow, Scotland
Amrut Fusion released
Ranked third-finest whisky in the world by Jim Murray's Whisky Bible

name: Flóki Icelandic Single Malt
description: Flóki is Iceland's first single malt whisky — a young, defiantly local spirit made by the family-run Eimverk distillery from 100% Icelandic barley, a crop that barely ripens at 64 degrees north. That hardy, slow-grown grain carries less sugar than commercial malt, so each bottle takes roughly half again as much barley, and it stamps the whisky with a flavour unlike anywhere else: earthy young cereal, dried grass and citrus over new-oak vanilla and a mineral, almost volcanic edge, bottled at 47% ABV. Nobody mistakes it for an old Speyside — it is proudly youthful frontier whisky, named for Hrafna-Flóki, the Norse explorer who gave Iceland its name. For drinkers charting whisky's newest latitudes, it is the northern datapoint, and sibling releases smoked over sheep dung push the local-terroir idea even further.
history: The Thorkelsson family founded Eimverk in 2009 in Garðabær, outside Reykjavík, testing over a hundred trial distillations before settling on their barley-forward recipe. First came Flóki Young Malt in 2014, an under-three-years preview, while full maturation continued; the first proper Flóki Icelandic Single Malt followed in late 2017, the first whisky ever produced entirely in Iceland. The distillery also makes Vor gin and Víti brennivín, building a portfolio entirely from Icelandic grain and botanicals, and later reserve editions extended the whisky range with sherry casks and barley smoked over sheep dung in the traditional island manner.
production: Eimverk grows and sources hardy Icelandic six-row barley — no pesticides are needed in the subarctic climate — and malts, brews and pot-distils it on site. Because the local grain yields less fermentable sugar, each bottle consumes roughly fifty per cent more barley than a typical malt. The spirit matures at least three years, primarily in new American oak whose active wood suits a young whisky, and is bottled at 47% ABV. Smoked variants kiln the barley over sheep dung, Iceland's traditional fuel.
barrel: Primarily new American oak casks; matured a minimum of three years in Iceland

tastingNotes.nose: Fresh barley, hay and lemon peel with new-oak vanilla and a mineral, earthy note.
tastingNotes.palate: Young and vigorous — malt loaf, green banana, oak spice, white pepper and dried grass.
tastingNotes.finish: Medium and drying, cereal and oak with a faintly smoky, earthy fade.

servingTemp: Neat at 18–20 °C
facts: Named after Hrafna-Flóki (Raven-Flóki), the Viking explorer credited with giving Iceland its name.
Icelandic barley yields so little sugar that each bottle requires roughly fifty per cent more grain than a typical single malt.
Eimverk's smoked expressions kiln barley over sheep dung, the traditional Icelandic smoking fuel used for delicacies like hangikjöt.
The 2017 release was the first single malt whisky ever produced in Iceland.

timeline.event: Eimverk distillery founded by the Thorkelsson family in Garðabær
Flóki Young Malt released as an under-three-year preview
First fully matured Flóki Icelandic Single Malt released

name: Glenfiddich 12 Year
description: Glenfiddich 12 is the flagship of the world's best-selling single malt Scotch and, for a huge share of drinkers, the first single malt they ever taste. It is a textbook gentle Speyside: fresh pear and green apple over light honey, soft vanilla and a clean, subtly oaky finish, bottled at 40% ABV. The whisky comes from the family-owned Dufftown distillery that did more than any other to create the modern single malt category, and its triangular green-tinted bottle with the stag emblem is one of the most recognizable packages in spirits. Critics rarely call it profound, but its consistency, availability and approachable price have made it the standard reference point for the Speyside style — the bottle against which lighter, orchard-fruited malts are routinely measured.
history: William Grant built Glenfiddich by hand with his nine children, and the first spirit ran from the stills on Christmas Day 1887. The distillery remains in the hands of his descendants at William Grant & Sons. In 1963 the company began actively marketing Glenfiddich outside Scotland as a single (then 'straight') malt — a move widely credited with launching the modern single malt category. The 12-year-old expression took over as the standard-bearer of the range in 1999, replacing the earlier Special Reserve bottling.
production: Malted barley is mashed with water from the Robbie Dhu spring, fermented, and distilled twice in unusually small copper stills — Glenfiddich runs one of the largest stillhouses in Scotland by keeping many small stills rather than a few big ones. Maturation combines American oak ex-bourbon casks with European oak sherry casks; the whisky is then married in large oak tuns for several months before bottling at 40% ABV.
barrel: American oak ex-bourbon and European oak Oloroso sherry casks; married in oak tuns before bottling
coloring: Caramel colour (E150a) used for batch consistency

tastingNotes.nose: Fresh pear, green apple, cut grass, light honey and a soft floral maltiness.
tastingNotes.palate: Gentle and sweet — orchard fruit, vanilla cream, butterscotch and a whisper of oak spice.
tastingNotes.finish: Short to medium, clean and mellow, with lingering pear and soft malt.

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature (18–20 °C); also works over ice or in a highball
facts: Glenfiddich means 'valley of the deer' in Gaelic — hence the stag on every label.
The first spirit ran from the stills on Christmas Day 1887.
The triangular bottle, introduced in 1957, was designed by Hans Schleger.
In 1963 Glenfiddich became the first distillery to actively market single malt outside Scotland, and in 1969 it opened the first distillery visitor centre in Scotland.

timeline.event: First spirit distilled at Glenfiddich on Christmas Day
Distinctive triangular bottle introduced
Glenfiddich begins marketing single malt internationally, effectively creating the category
12-year-old expression becomes the flagship of the core range

name: The Macallan Sherry Oak 12 Year
description: The Macallan Sherry Oak 12 is the entry point to the most heavily sherried house style among major Speyside distilleries, and one of the world's most prestigious whisky brands. Matured entirely in sherry-seasoned oak casks commissioned in Jerez, it delivers dried fruit, ginger, orange zest and sweet wood spice with a depth of colour that comes from the casks alone — The Macallan adds no caramel. The brand's cask-first philosophy, its 'curiously small' spirit stills and its record-shattering auction results have given it a luxury aura few distilleries can match, and prices have climbed accordingly. As an introduction to classic sherry-cask maturation, though, the 12-year-old remains the benchmark: rich but polished, it is the bottle most drinkers mean when they say a whisky tastes 'sherried'.
history: Alexander Reid licensed The Macallan in 1824, making it one of the first legal distilleries in Speyside after the 1823 Excise Act. It sat for most of its life on the Easter Elchies estate above the River Spey, supplying blenders before its single malt rose to prominence in the late twentieth century under Highland Distillers, later Edrington. In 2018 a striking £140 million distillery, cut into the hillside under a rippling turf roof, replaced the old plant. In 2023 a 1926 Macallan became the most expensive bottle of whisky ever sold at auction.
production: The Macallan distils a deliberately narrow spirit cut through some of the smallest spirit stills in Speyside, giving a rich, oily new-make. The Sherry Oak range matures exclusively in oak casks — mostly European oak with some American — that are coopered and seasoned with Oloroso sherry in Jerez, Spain, to the distillery's specification. Colour is entirely natural, and the 12-year-old is bottled at 43% ABV for the US market.
barrel: Exclusively sherry-seasoned oak casks (European and American oak), coopered and seasoned in Jerez, Spain
coloring: Natural colour — no caramel added

tastingNotes.nose: Dried fruits, sherry richness, ginger, toffee and a hint of orange peel.
tastingNotes.palate: Raisin, fig and date sweetness with wood spice, dark chocolate, hazelnut and zesty orange.
tastingNotes.finish: Medium-long, warming ginger and dried fruit with gently drying oak.

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature (18–20 °C); a few drops of water soften the spice
facts: The Macallan never adds colouring — the deep amber comes entirely from sherry-seasoned oak.
Its spirit stills are among the smallest in Speyside, a feature the distillery credits for its rich new-make.
A 1926 Macallan sold for about £2.19 million at Sotheby's in 2023, the world auction record for a bottle of whisky.
The new distillery, opened in 2018, sits beneath an undulating timber-and-turf roof designed by Rogers Stirk Harbour + Partners.

timeline.event: Alexander Reid licenses The Macallan on the Easter Elchies estate
New £140 million distillery opens beneath a turf roof
A 1926 Macallan sets the world auction record for a bottle of whisky (~£2.19 m)

name: Lagavulin 16 Year
description: Lagavulin 16 is the reference point for mature Islay whisky: a dense, tarry, deeply smoky malt that trades the aggression of younger peated drams for something slower and more contemplative. Sixteen years in oak knit thick peat smoke into layers of dried fruit, seaweed, iodine, sweet sherry and old leather, with a finish that famously goes on for minutes. Chosen as the Islay representative in Diageo's original Classic Malts line-up in 1988, it has been the south-shore icon ever since, and its cult status was only amplified when it became the on-screen dram of choice of Ron Swanson in Parks and Recreation. For many whisky drinkers it is the definitive winter-evening malt — peat as depth and texture rather than shock value.
history: Lagavulin was licensed in 1816 by John Johnston, on a bay where illicit distilling reportedly dates back to the 1740s. In the late nineteenth century it became the engine of Peter Mackie's White Horse blend, one of the great Victorian Scotch brands. Diageo's predecessors selected the 16-year-old for the Classic Malts range in 1988, turning a blender's workhorse into a single malt icon. The distillery celebrated its bicentenary in 2016, and since 2019 has bottled the Offerman Edition series with actor Nick Offerman.
production: Lagavulin's malt is peated to roughly 35 ppm phenols at Port Ellen Maltings. The distillery ferments slowly and runs what it describes as the slowest distillation on Islay — a spirit run of around nine hours through pear-shaped stills — producing a heavy, oily spirit. Maturation is predominantly in refill American oak hogsheads with a proportion of European oak sherry wood, and the whisky is bottled at 43% ABV.
barrel: Mainly refill American oak hogsheads with a proportion of European oak ex-sherry casks
coloring: Caramel colour (E150a) used for batch consistency

tastingNotes.nose: Thick peat smoke, iodine, seaweed and tar over raisins, vanilla and old sherry.
tastingNotes.palate: Rich and mouth-coating — bonfire smoke, sweet dried fruit, espresso, salted caramel and dark chocolate.
tastingNotes.finish: Very long and smouldering; ash, leather, brine and a sweet malty echo.

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature (18–20 °C); a teaspoon of water unlocks sweeter fruit under the smoke
facts: Chosen in 1988 as the Islay representative of the original six Classic Malts.
The distillery claims the slowest distillation on Islay — the spirit run takes around nine hours.
Nick Offerman's Parks and Recreation character Ron Swanson made Lagavulin 16 a pop-culture icon, leading to the real Offerman Edition bottlings from 2019.
Lagavulin was the heart of the White Horse blend created by Peter Mackie in 1890.

timeline.event: John Johnston licenses the Lagavulin distillery
Lagavulin 16 launched as part of the Classic Malts selection
Distillery bicentenary marked with special releases
First Offerman Edition released with Nick Offerman

name: Ardbeg 10 Year
description: Ardbeg Ten is one of the most intensely peated widely available single malts, yet its trick is brightness: beneath the tar and bonfire smoke runs a seam of lime, pear and vanilla that keeps the whisky lifted rather than leaden. Bottled at 46% ABV, non-chill filtered and without added colour, it is unusually transparent for a flagship malt, and its pale colour surprises drinkers expecting darkness to accompany that much smoke. The Ten anchored Ardbeg's improbable comeback after the distillery spent much of the 1980s and 1990s silent or barely running, and it has since built one of whisky's most devoted followings through the Ardbeg Committee. Among the Kildalton coast peat heavyweights it is the citric, high-definition one — sootier than Lagavulin, less medicinal than Laphroaig.
history: Founded in 1815 by John Macdougall on Islay's south coast, Ardbeg supplied blenders for over a century before falling on hard times: it closed in 1981, ran intermittently from 1989 and was nearly lost. In 1997 Glenmorangie plc bought the derelict site for around £7 million and restored production. The definitive 10-year-old appeared in 2000, the same year the Ardbeg Committee fan organisation was founded. LVMH acquired Glenmorangie, and with it Ardbeg, in 2004, and demand eventually so outgrew the little distillery that a second stillhouse doubled capacity in 2021.
production: Ardbeg's malt is peated to roughly 50–55 ppm phenols, among the highest of any regularly produced Scotch. A purifier pipe on the spirit still returns heavier vapours for redistillation, preserving citrusy, estery top notes despite the massive peating — the source of the whisky's signature lime-and-smoke balance. Maturation is chiefly in first-fill and refill ex-bourbon American oak, and the whisky is bottled at 46% ABV, non-chill filtered, with no added colour.
barrel: First-fill and refill ex-bourbon American oak casks
coloring: No added colour

tastingNotes.nose: Sharp peat smoke, lime zest, brine, smoked fish and vanilla cream.
tastingNotes.palate: Espresso, tar and black pepper cut by sweet lemon, pear drops and toasted marshmallow.
tastingNotes.finish: Long and ashy with fading citrus, aniseed and sea-spray salinity.

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature (18–20 °C); stands up well to a splash of water
facts: Ardbeg sent vials of new-make spirit and oak to the International Space Station in 2011 to study maturation in microgravity; the samples returned in 2014.
The distillery was bought back from dereliction in 1997 for around £7 million by Glenmorangie plc.
Despite malt peated to roughly 50–55 ppm, a purifier on the spirit still keeps the whisky bright and citric.
The Ardbeg Committee, founded in 2000, has grown to well over one hundred thousand members worldwide.

timeline.event: John Macdougall founds Ardbeg on the Kildalton coast
Distillery closes as blend demand collapses
Glenmorangie plc buys and revives the distillery
Ardbeg Ten launched; Ardbeg Committee founded

name: Laphroaig 10 Year
description: Laphroaig 10 is whisky's great polarizer — the famously 'medicinal' Islay malt whose iodine, TCP-antiseptic and seaweed notes drinkers either adore or never touch again. The distillery leans into it, having long marketed the whisky with the slogan of love it or hate it. Beneath the surgical-ward smoke sits surprising sweetness: vanilla, coconut and custard from ex-bourbon barrels, many of them sourced from Maker's Mark, its sibling under Suntory ownership. Part of the character comes from Laphroaig's own floor maltings, where barley is cold-smoked over peat before kilning. The 10-year-old has been the house standard for over a century and carries a Royal Warrant granted by the then Prince of Wales in 1994 — a curious honour for so confrontational a dram, and proof of its singular place in Scotch whisky.
history: Brothers Donald and Alexander Johnston founded Laphroaig around 1815 beside a sheltered bay on Islay's south coast. During US Prohibition it was reportedly sold legally as medicinal spirit, its iodine reek persuading customs agents it was medicine. Ian Hunter, the last of the founding family, left the distillery in 1954 to Bessie Williamson, the first woman to own and run a Scotch whisky distillery in the twentieth century. The Prince of Wales granted his Royal Warrant in 1994, and Laphroaig passed to Beam, then Suntory, in 2014.
production: Laphroaig still malts a portion of its own barley on traditional floor maltings, cold-smoking it over Islay peat for a tarry, medicinal phenol character that complements malt from Port Ellen Maltings (overall around 40–45 ppm). After double distillation, the spirit matures mostly in first-fill ex-bourbon barrels — many previously holding Maker's Mark — close to the sea. The standard US bottling is 43% ABV and chill filtered.
barrel: Predominantly first-fill ex-bourbon American oak barrels, many sourced from Maker's Mark
coloring: Caramel colour (E150a) used for batch consistency

tastingNotes.nose: Iodine, antiseptic smoke, seaweed and tar over vanilla and a faint coconut sweetness.
tastingNotes.palate: Big ashy peat, sea salt and liquorice with custard, oak and a surprising malty sweetness underneath.
tastingNotes.finish: Long, drying and smoky with lingering salt, ash and gentle vanilla.

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature (18–20 °C); water tames the antiseptic edge
facts: During Prohibition, Laphroaig was reportedly imported into the US legally as medicinal spirit — the iodine aroma helped convince the authorities.
Holds a Royal Warrant from the Prince of Wales (now King Charles III), granted during his 1994 visit to Islay.
Friends of Laphroaig members receive a lifetime lease on one square foot of Islay, with annual rent payable as a dram.
Bessie Williamson, who inherited the distillery in 1954, was the first woman to own and manage a Scotch whisky distillery in the twentieth century.

timeline.event: Donald and Alexander Johnston establish Laphroaig
Bessie Williamson inherits the distillery from Ian Hunter
Royal Warrant granted by the Prince of Wales; Friends of Laphroaig founded
Laphroaig becomes part of Beam Suntory (now Suntory Global Spirits)

name: Talisker 10 Year
description: Talisker 10 is the definitive maritime single malt — a whisky that tastes of where it is made, on the shore of Loch Harport beneath the Cuillin hills of Skye. Its signature is a one-two of sea salt and cracked black pepper: medium peat smoke rolls over sweet barley and dried fruit before the famous 'chilli catch' flares on the swallow. Bottled at an unusual 45.8% ABV, it is punchier than most core-range malts and was chosen in 1988 as the island representative of Diageo's Classic Malts. Old-fashioned worm tub condensers preserve a slightly sulphury, muscular weight that modern condensers would strip away. It is the standard recommendation for drinkers ready to step from gentle Speyside toward Islay — smoke as seasoning rather than the whole dish.
history: Brothers Hugh and Kenneth MacAskill founded Talisker in 1830 at Carbost, and for 187 years it was the Isle of Skye's only licensed distillery. Robert Louis Stevenson name-checked it approvingly in an 1880 poem as one of the great drinks of Scotland. A stillhouse fire in 1960 destroyed the five stills, which were rebuilt as exact replicas two years later. Selected for the Classic Malts range in 1988, Talisker has since grown into one of Diageo's flagship single malts under the 'Made by the Sea' identity.
production: Talisker mashes lightly peated malt (around 18–22 ppm) and ferments long, then distils through an idiosyncratic setup of two wash stills — fitted with purifier pipes that recycle heavier vapours — and three spirit stills. Vapour is condensed in traditional outdoor worm tubs, retaining meaty, full-bodied character. Maturation is mainly in refill American oak hogsheads, and bottling is at the distillery's traditional 45.8% ABV.
barrel: Mainly refill American oak hogsheads
coloring: Caramel colour (E150a) used for batch consistency

tastingNotes.nose: Sea spray, smoke and citrus over sweet malt and a whiff of oilskin.
tastingNotes.palate: Rich dried fruit and barley sugar swept up in peat smoke, brine and cracked black pepper.
tastingNotes.finish: Long and warming with the signature peppery 'chilli catch' and drifting smoke.

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature (18–20 °C); also excellent in a highball with a grind of black pepper
facts: Robert Louis Stevenson praised Talisker in verse in 1880, ranking it among 'the king o' drinks'.
Was the only distillery on the Isle of Skye from 1830 until Torabhaig opened in 2017.
A 1960 stillhouse fire destroyed all five stills; they were rebuilt as exact copies to preserve the spirit character.
Bottled at 45.8% ABV, a hangover from an old proof standard the distillery never abandoned.

timeline.event: Hugh and Kenneth MacAskill found Talisker at Carbost
Stillhouse fire destroys the stills, rebuilt as exact replicas
Talisker 10 launched as part of the Classic Malts selection

name: Highland Park 12 Year Viking Honour
description: Highland Park 12 — badged Viking Honour since 2017 — is often held up as the great all-rounder of single malt: a whisky that balances honeyed sweetness, sherried fruit and a wisp of aromatic smoke so evenly that it satisfies peat lovers and Speyside drinkers alike. The smoke is distinctive because the peat is: Orkney's Hobbister Moor peat is heather-rich and woodless, giving fragrant, floral smoke quite unlike Islay's tarry iodine. Around a fifth of the malt is still turned by hand on the distillery's own floors, and maturation leans on sherry-seasoned oak with no added colouring. From Scotland's northernmost distillery, founded in 1798, it remains one of the most complete whiskies in its price bracket and a fixture on best-value lists.
history: Highland Park traces its origin to 1798 and Magnus Eunson, an Orkney beadle and butcher who ran an illicit still on the site before the distillery was formally established. Licensed in 1826, it prospered supplying blenders and by the twentieth century had built a single malt reputation under Highland Distillers, later the Edrington Group. In 2017 the range was repackaged around the islands' Norse heritage, renaming the 12-year-old Viking Honour — Orkney was ruled by Norway for centuries, and the branding leans into that history.
production: About twenty per cent of Highland Park's barley is floor-malted on site and kilned over heather-rich peat cut from Hobbister Moor (around 30–40 ppm on that portion), then blended with unpeated mainland malt for a gently smoky mash. After distillation the spirit matures predominantly in sherry-seasoned European and American oak casks in cool Orkney warehouses. The 12 is married before bottling at 43% ABV with natural colour.
barrel: Predominantly sherry-seasoned European and American oak casks, with refill wood
coloring: Natural colour — no caramel added

tastingNotes.nose: Heather honey, orange peel and gentle floral peat smoke with sherried dried fruit.
tastingNotes.palate: Honeyed malt, marzipan, winter spice and citrus wrapped in soft aromatic smoke.
tastingNotes.finish: Medium-long, warming and lightly smoky with a sweet heathery fade.

servingTemp: Neat at room temperature (18–20 °C)
facts: The most northerly Scotch whisky distillery, edging out neighbouring Scapa by less than a mile.
Orkney's Hobbister Moor peat is heather-rich and virtually treeless, which is why Highland Park's smoke reads floral rather than tarry.
Founder Magnus Eunson was by day a church officer and by night a smuggler, hiding whisky under the pulpit according to distillery lore.
Around 20% of the malt is still turned by hand on traditional malting floors.

timeline.event: Whisky-making begins on the Highland Park site under Magnus Eunson
Distillery formally licensed
12-year-old repackaged as Viking Honour
